This means F accumulates both during the come-up time (heating phase) and the cooling phase — not just during the holding period. KTU numerical problems often specify *only* the holding time, and you must use the full integral. The simple formula $F = t \cdot L$ is valid only for **constant temperature**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Across the Food & Dairy Industry

| Industry Segment | Thermal Process | Typical Conditions | Purpose |
|------------------|-----------------|---------------------|---------|
| Liquid Milk (Dairy) | HTST Pasteurization | **72 °C × 15 s** | Pathogen kill (TB, Brucella) + shelf life |
| Liquid Milk (Dairy) | UHT (Aseptic) | **135–150 °C × 2–5 s** | Commercial sterility, ambient storage |
| Canned Vegetables | In-container sterilization | **121 °C × 3–15 min** (F₀ ≥ 3 min) | *Clostridium botulinum* control |
| Meat & Fish (Canned) | Retort sterilization | **121 °C × 30–90 min** | Spore inactivation |
| Fruit Juices | Hot-fill / pasteurization | **85–95 °C × 1–3 min** | Enzyme + mold/yeast inactivation |
| Beer | Flash pasteurization | **71–74 °C × 30 s** | Stabilization |
| Infant Formula | Spray drying + retort | **115–121 °C** | Long shelf life, safety |
| Eggs (Liquid) | Pasteurization | **60–64 °C × 3.5–4 min** | *Salmonella* inactivation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Microbial Death Kinetics

The death of vegetative microorganisms under isothermal conditions follows a **log-linear** pattern. The rate of microbial inactivation is proportional to the number of viable cells present at any time.

**Step 1: Define the rate equation.** Let $N(t)$ be the number of viable microorganisms per unit mass (CFU/g) at time $t$. The fractional death rate is:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d \cdot N$$

**Step 2: Separate variables and integrate.** This is a standard first-order linear differential equation. Separating:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \, dt$$

**Step 3: Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = t$** (where $N_0$ is the initial count and $N$ is the count at time $t$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k_d \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Convert natural log to base-10 logarithm** (since D-value is conventionally defined in log₁₀):

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k_d \cdot t}{2.303}$$

**Step 5: Define the D-value.** By definition, $D$ is the time required for a **10-fold reduction** (i.e., $N/N_0 = 10^{-1}$):

$$-1 = -\frac{k_d \cdot D}{2.303} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \frac{2.303}{k_d}$$

**Step 6: Substitute back.** Replacing $k_d = 2.303/D$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D}$$

**Step 7: Solve for survivors explicitly.** The final canonical form used in KTU problems:

$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The negative exponent means $N$ decreases exponentially with time. The larger the $D$-value, the more heat-resistant the organism. Spores of *Clostridium botulinum* have $D_{121.1} \approx 0.21$ min — extremely resistant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Temperature Dependence — The Z-Value

The $D$-value is itself a strong function of temperature. Empirically, $\log(D)$ varies **linearly** with temperature.

**Step 1: Start with experimental observation** (Bigelow, 1921):

$$\log(D) = -\frac{T}{z} + C$$

where $C$ is a constant. The slope of $\log(D)$ vs. $T$ is $-1/z$.

**Step 2: Apply to two temperatures** $T_1$ and $T_2$ with corresponding $D$-values $D_1$ and $D_2$:

$$\log(D_1) = -\frac{T_1}{z} + C$$
$$\log(D_2) = -\frac{T_2}{z} + C$$

**Step 3: Subtract the two equations** to eliminate $C$:

$$\log(D_1) - \log(D_2) = -\frac{T_1}{z} + \frac{T_2}{z} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$$

**Step 4: Use logarithm property** $\log(D_1) - \log(D_2) = \log(D_1/D_2)$:

$$\log\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$$

**Step 5: Solve explicitly for $z$:**

$$z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log(D_1 / D_2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** $T_2 > T_1 \Rightarrow D_2 < D_1$ (higher T = lower D = faster kill). The denominator is therefore **negative**, giving a positive $z$.

**Step 6: Express $D$ at any temperature** relative to a reference:

$$D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/z}$$

This is the form used in KTU problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Process Lethality (F-Value) from Lethal Rate

The **lethal rate** $L$ is defined as the ratio of the D-value at a reference temperature to the D-value at the current temperature:

$$L = \frac{D_{ref}}{D_T} = \frac{D_{ref}}{D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/z}} = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$$

**Step 1: For a constant-temperature hold** of duration $t$ at temperature $T$:

$$F = L \cdot t = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$$

**Step 2: For a variable temperature profile** $T(\tau)$ (which is the real case during come-up, holding, and cooling), integrate:

$$F = \int_0^{t_{total}} 10^{(T(\tau) - T_{ref})/z} \, d\tau$$

**Step 3: Discretize for practical computation** (KTU exam approach). Break the heating profile into small time intervals $\Delta \tau$ of width $\Delta \tau$ (typically 1 minute):

$$F \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta\tau \cdot 10^{(T_i - T_{ref})/z}$$

**Step 4: Special case — Sterilization Value (F₀).** For low-acid canned foods, the reference is $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C and $z = 10$ °C:

$$F_0 = \int_0^{t_{total}} 10^{(T(\tau) - 121.1)/10} \, d\tau$$

**Step 5: Botulinum Cook Criterion.** For commercial sterility against *C. botulinum*, KTU (and FDA) mandates a minimum **12-D reduction** at $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C, i.e.,

$$F_0 \geq 12 \cdot D_{121.1, C.botulinum} = 12 \times 0.21 \approx 2.52 \text{ min (commonly rounded to 3 min)}$$

This is the famous **"botulinum cook"** of $F_0 = 3$ min.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A milk sample contains $N_0 = 10^5$ CFU/mL of *Listeria monocytogenes*. The $D_{72}$ value is 0.6 s. The HTST pasteurizer holds the milk at 72 °C for 15 s. Calculate (a) the log reduction, (b) the surviving population, and (c) the F-value at 72 °C reference.

**Solution:**

**(a) Log reduction** $n = t/D = 15/0.6 = 25$ decimal reductions.

**(b) Survivors:**

$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-n} = 10^5 \cdot 10^{-25} = 10^{-20} \text{ CFU/mL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is essentially zero — a 25-log reduction is far in excess of the **5-log minimum** required by the US FDA and FSSAI for pasteurization of milk. The product is microbiologically safe.

**(c) F-value (F₇₂):** Since temperature is constant at 72 °C, the lethal rate $L = 10^{(72-72)/7} = 10^0 = 1$.

$$F_{72} = L \cdot t = 1 \times 15 = 15 \text{ s}$$

Equivalently, $F_{72} = n \cdot D = 25 \times 0.6 = 15$ s. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Thermal Processing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food or Milk] --> B{Product Type?}
    B -->|Low-Acid pH > 4.6| C[Sterilization Required]
    B -->|Acid pH le 4.6| D[ Pasteurization Sufficient]
    C --> E[Commercial Sterility Target: F0 ge 3 min]
    E --> F[In-container Retort or UHT Aseptic]
    D --> G[Pathogen Kill: min 5-log reduction]
    G --> H{Heat-Resistant Spores?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Hot-Fill + Refrigeration]
    H -->|No| J[HTST or LTLT Pasteurization]
    F --> K[Long Shelf Life: Months to Years]
    I --> L[Short Shelf Life: Weeks]
    J --> M[Short Shelf Life: Days to Weeks]
    K --> N[Quality Optimization: Minimize over-processing]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Packaged Product]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram – Process Lethality (F) Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Heating] --> B[Come-up Time CUT]
    B --> C[Holding Period at T]
    C --> D[Cool Down Time CDT]
    D --> E[Process End]
    B --> F[Lethality from CUT]
    C --> G[Lethality from Holding]
    D --> H[Lethality from CDT]
    F --> I[Total Process F]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{F ge Required F?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Process ACCEPTED]
    J -->|No| L[Process REJECTED - Extend Time]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map – Importance of Thermal Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Processing Importance] --> B[Microbial Safety]
    A --> C[Enzyme Control]
    A --> D[Shelf Life Extension]
    A --> E[Commercial Value]
    A --> F[Regulatory Compliance]
    B --> B1[Pathogen Destruction]
    B --> B2[Spoilage Organism Control]
    C --> C1[Lipase and Protease Inactivation]
    C --> C2[PPO and Peroxidase Control]
    D --> D1[Ambient Storage Capability]
    D --> D2[Reduced Logistics Cost]
    E --> E1[Market Expansion]
    E --> E2[Product Diversification]
    F --> F1[FSSAI BIS Codex Standards]
    F --> F2[FDA USDA EFSA Regulations]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Thermal Processing System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_Stage
        A1[Raw Material Receiving] --> A2[Pre-treatment Cleaning Standardization]
    end
    subgraph Processing_Stage
        A2 --> B1[Heat Exchanger Pre-heater]
        B1 --> B2[Hold Tube / Retort Vessel]
        B2 --> B3[Heat Exchanger Cooler]
    end
    subgraph Packaging_Stage
        B3 --> C1{Aseptic or In-container?}
        C1 -->|Aseptic| C2[Sterile Tank + Aseptic Filler]
        C1 -->|In-container| C3[Can Seamer or Bottle Closer]
    end
    subgraph Storage_Stage
        C2 --> D1[Ambient Warehouse]
        C3 --> D2[Ambient Warehouse]
    end
    subgraph Quality_Monitoring
        B2 --> Q1[Temperature Recorders]
        Q1 --> Q2[F-value Calculator]
        Q2 --> Q3{Process Within Limits?}
        Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Release Approved]
        Q3 -->|No| Q5[Reject Reprocess]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of the Architecture:**
> The **Input_Stage** ensures uniformity (critical for predictable lethality). The **Processing_Stage** is where lethality is delivered — the **hold tube** (for HTST/UHT) or the **retort vessel** (for in-container sterilization) is the heart of the operation. The **Packaging_Stage** locks in sterility — once contaminated post-process, the product is spoiled regardless of the heat treatment. The **Quality_Monitoring** block uses **time-temperature integrators** to confirm the delivered F-value meets the regulatory minimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q: Define thermal processing. List any four objectives of thermal processing of foods.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermal processing is the controlled application of heat to foods for a specified time at a defined temperature, primarily to inactivate pathogenic and spoilage microorganisms, destroy deteriorative enzymes, and extend shelf life.

**Four objectives:**
1. **Microbial inactivation** — destruction of pathogens (*Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria, Mycobacterium*) and spoilage organisms.
2. **Enzyme inactivation** — destruction of native food enzymes (lipase, protease, polyphenol oxidase) and microbial enzymes.
3. **Shelf-life extension** — creation of shelf-stable products that can be stored without refrigeration.
4. **Quality preservation** — retention of nutritional, sensory, and functional attributes through optimized time-temperature combinations.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark for each objective = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Style]**
**Q: What is the D-value in thermal processing? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
The **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time)** is defined as the time required, at a specified temperature, to reduce the microbial population by **90% (one logarithmic cycle, i.e., from $N_0$ to $N_0/10$)**.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$D = \frac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0 / N)}$$

**Example:** If the $D_{72}$ value for *Listeria monocytogenes* in milk is 0.6 s, it means that at 72 °C, the count of *Listeria* decreases by 90% every 0.6 seconds of holding. So a 6 s hold would yield a 100-fold (2-log) reduction.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for equation + 1 mark for example = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]**

**Q: (a)** Define and explain the D-value and Z-value with their significance in thermal processing. Derive the relationship between F-value, D-value, and initial microbial load. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A canned food product contains an initial spore load of $N_0 = 10^6$ spores/can of *Clostridium botulinum*. The $D_{121.1}$ value is 0.21 min and $z = 10$ °C. If the product is processed at 115 °C for 35 min, calculate: (i) the $D_{115}$ value, (ii) the F-value at 121.1 °C ($F_0$), and (iii) whether the process meets the 12-D botulinum cook criterion. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):**
The D-value is the time at a constant reference temperature required to destroy 90% of the viable microbial population. It is a measure of the heat resistance of an organism. A high D-value indicates high heat resistance.

**Z-value:**
The Z-value is the temperature change required to cause a **ten-fold change** in the D-value. It is a measure of the **temperature sensitivity** of the organism. A small z means the organism is highly sensitive to temperature changes.

**Derivation of F-D-N relationship:**

Starting from the survivor equation $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$, take the base-10 log:

$$\log_{10}(N) = \log_{10}(N_0) - \frac{t}{D}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{t}{D} = \log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N) = \log_{10}(N_0 / N)$$

If the process runs for the time $t = F$ at the reference temperature (where $D$ is the reference D-value), then:

$$F = D \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** between process lethality, heat resistance, and microbial load. (7 marks allocated across definition of D, definition of z, derivation, and significance)

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $N_0 = 10^6$ spores/can; $D_{121.1} = 0.21$ min; $z = 10$ °C; processing at $T = 115$ °C for $t = 35$ min.

**(i) Calculate $D_{115}$:**

$$D_{115} = D_{121.1} \cdot 10^{(121.1 - 115)/z} = 0.21 \cdot 10^{6.1/10}$$

$$D_{115} = 0.21 \cdot 10^{0.61} = 0.21 \times 4.074 = 0.8555 \text{ min} \approx 0.86 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Calculate $F_0$ (lethality at the reference 121.1 °C):**

Method 1 — using lethal rate at 115 °C:

$$L = 10^{(T - 121.1)/z} = 10^{(115 - 121.1)/10} = 10^{-0.61} = 0.2455$$

$$F_0 = L \cdot t = 0.2455 \times 35 = 8.59 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Check the 12-D botulinum cook:**

Required $F_0$ for 12-D = $12 \times D_{121.1} = 12 \times 0.21 = 2.52$ min.

Since $F_0 = 8.59$ min $\gg 2.52$ min, the process **comfortably exceeds** the 12-D criterion. The product is **commercially sterile** and safe.

**Alternative verification via survivors:**

$$\log(N_0 / N) = F_0 / D_{121.1} = 8.59 / 0.21 = 40.9 \text{ log reductions}$$

$$N = 10^6 \cdot 10^{-40.9} = 10^{-34.9} \text{ spores/can} \approx 0$$

(7 marks: 2 for part i, 3 for part ii, 2 for part iii)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the importance of thermal processing in the food and dairy industry. Discuss the different methods of pasteurization (LTLT, HTST, UHT) with their time-temperature combinations and target organisms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fruit juice has an initial pectin methylesterase (PME) activity of 100 units/mL. The $D_{80}$ for PME is 2 min, and $z = 7$ °C. If the juice is heated at 90 °C for 6 min, calculate the residual PME activity. Comment on the quality implication. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Importance of thermal processing in food and dairy industry:**

Thermal processing is critical to the food and dairy industry for the following reasons:
- **Public health protection** — destroys pathogens that cause typhoid, tuberculosis, brucellosis, listeriosis, salmonellosis, and botulism.
- **Commercial shelf life** — extends product life from days to months/years, enabling long-distance distribution.
- **Regulatory compliance** — meets FSSAI, BIS, Codex, FDA, and EU statutory requirements for safe dairy and food products.
- **Economic value** — reduces post-harvest losses, supports value-added dairy product manufacturing (cheese, yogurt, condensed milk), and enables the global dairy trade.
- **Quality assurance** — combined with HACCP and aseptic packaging, it is the foundation of modern food safety systems.

**Methods of Pasteurization:**

| Method | Full Form | Time-Temperature | Target Organisms |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| LTLT | Low-Temperature Long-Time (Batch) | **63 °C × 30 min** | *M. tuberculosis*, *Brucella*, *Coxiella burnetii* |
| HTST | High-Temperature Short-Time (Continuous) | **72 °C × 15 s** | Same as LTLT; also *E. coli*, *Salmonella* |
| UHT | Ultra-High Temperature (Aseptic) | **135–150 °C × 2–5 s** | All vegetative pathogens and most spores |
| Thermization | Sub-pasteurization | **57–68 °C × 10–15 s** | Psychrotrophs (cheese milk pretreatment) |

(7 marks: 4 for importance + 3 for pasteurization methods)

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $A_0 = 100$ units/mL (PME activity); $D_{80} = 2$ min; $z = 7$ °C; $T = 90$ °C; $t = 6$ min.

**Step 1: Calculate $D_{90}$:**

$$D_{90} = D_{80} \cdot 10^{(80 - 90)/7} = 2 \cdot 10^{-10/7} = 2 \cdot 10^{-1.4286}$$

$$D_{90} = 2 \times 0.0373 = 0.0746 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2: Calculate log reduction** (treating enzyme inactivation as first-order, same math as microbial kill):

$$n = \frac{t}{D_{90}} = \frac{6}{0.0746} = 80.4 \text{ decimal reductions}$$

**Step 3: Calculate residual activity:**

$$A = A_0 \cdot 10^{-n} = 100 \cdot 10^{-80.4} \approx 10^{-78.4} \text{ units/mL}$$

This is essentially **zero activity** — PME is completely inactivated.

**Step 4: Quality comment:**

While 90 °C × 6 min inactivates PME and ensures cloud stability of the juice, this is **severe over-processing** for enzyme control. A typical industry target is **5–10% residual PME activity** to preserve cloud. A milder process (e.g., 80 °C × 1 min) would suffice for enzyme inactivation while preserving vitamin C, color, and flavor. This illustrates the **trade-off** between microbial/enzyme safety and quality retention.

(7 marks: 2 for D-value computation, 2 for log reduction, 2 for residual activity, 1 for quality comment)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Thermal Processing Numerical Problems:**
>
> 1. **Z-value sign error** — When using $D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/z}$, students frequently invert the sign. **Memory aid:** if $T > T_{ref}$, the exponent is negative, so $D_T < D_{ref}$ (heat kills faster at higher T). Always check this logic.
> 2. **Unit mismatch between D and t** — D-value may be in **seconds** in some references and **minutes** in others. The t and D must be in the **same units** before dividing.
> 3. **F₀ vs F confusion** — $F$ is a generic process value; $F_0$ specifically refers to the value at $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C with $z = 10$ °C. A student writing $F$ when the question asks for $F_0$ loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the 12-D botulinum cook** — For low-acid canned food, the minimum $F_0$ is **3 min** (or 2.52 min strictly), not 1 min or 0.21 min. The 12-D is a regulatory standard, not a choice.
> 5. **Only counting lethality during the holding phase** — In real thermal processes, the **come-up time and cool-down time** also contribute to F. Unless the problem explicitly says "isothermal hold," you cannot assume $F = t_{hold} \times L$ alone.
> 6. **Confusing pasteurization and sterilization references** — For pasteurization, $z = 7$ °C and $T_{ref} = 72$ °C. For sterilization, $z = 10$ °C and $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C. Mixing these constants leads to wrong answers.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Importance of Thermal Processing**
>
> - **Thermal processing** = controlled heat application to inactivate microbes + enzymes + extend shelf life.
> - **Three pillars of importance:** (1) Microbial safety, (2) Enzyme control, (3) Shelf-life extension.
> - **D-value** = time for 90% (1 log) microbial reduction at a fixed T. Higher D = more heat-resistant.
> - **Z-value** = temperature change for 10-fold change in D. Lower z = more temperature-sensitive.
> - **F-value** = integrated lethality at a reference T; $F = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$ (constant T) or integral form (variable T).
> - **F₀** = sterilization value at $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C, $z = 10$ °C.
> - **Botulinum cook:** $F_0 \geq 3$ min (12-D reduction of *C. botulinum*).
> - **HTST pasteurization:** **72 °C × 15 s** (continuous flow).
> - **LTLT pasteurization:** **63 °C × 30 min** (batch).
> - **UHT treatment:** **135–150 °C × 2–5 s** (aseptic).
> - **Target organisms in pasteurization:** *M. tuberculosis, Brucella, Coxiella burnetii, Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria*.
> - **Survivor equation:** $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$.
> - **Cold spot** is the slowest heating point — process lethality must be validated there.
> - **Quality vs Safety trade-off:** Higher temperature = more safety, but more vitamin/quality loss.
> - **First-order kinetics** govern both microbial death and enzyme inactivation.
> - **Thermal death time (TDT)** is the heating time required to inactivate a given number of spores at a fixed T.
> - **Commercial sterility** ≠ absolute sterility — it means absence of *pathogens and spoilage organisms* of public health significance.
> - **FSSAI regulations** in India mandate 5-log reduction of *Coxiella burnetii* in milk; **FDA mandates 5-log reduction** of relevant pathogens.
> - **Aseptic packaging** combined with UHT gives 6–12 months of ambient shelf life.
> - **Units to remember:** D in min (or s), z in °C (or °F), T in °C, F in min (or s — be consistent).
> - **Log reduction formula:** $n = \log(N_0 / N) = t / D = F / D_{ref}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles of Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Heat Transfer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Heat Transfer? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > **Heat transfer** is the branch of thermal engineering that deals with the generation, use, conversion, and exchange of thermal energy (heat) between physical systems. It is classified into three fundamental modes: **conduction**, **convection**, and **radiation**, governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** — heat spontaneously flows from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature until thermal equilibrium is established. In the context of **Thermal Processing of Food**, heat transfer is the foundational phenomenon that governs operations like pasteurization, sterilization, blanching, drying, baking, evaporation, and frying. Without a precise understanding of how heat moves through food matrices, designing safe and energy-efficient processing equipment becomes impossible. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Flow" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** > Imagine you have a metal spoon placed in a hot cup of tea. After a minute, the handle of the spoon becomes warm. The **heat from the tea** traveled through the **metal spoon** to your hand. This is **conduction** — heat moving through a solid material, molecule by molecule, like a chain of people passing a bucket of water. > Now imagine blowing on hot soup to cool it. The moving air carries the heat away. This is **convection** — heat transferred by the bulk movement of a fluid (liquid or gas). > Finally, you feel the warmth of a fire even from across the room, without any direct contact. This is **radiation** — heat traveling as electromagnetic waves through space, just like sunlight warming your face. --- ## 1.3 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer — Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Modes of Heat Transfer:** > 1. **Conduction** — Transfer of heat through a stationary medium (solid, liquid, or gas) by direct molecular/atomic interaction, without bulk motion of matter. > 2. **Convection** — Transfer of heat between a surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas) due to combined effects of conduction and bulk fluid motion. > 3. **Radiation** — Transfer of heat in the form of electromagnetic waves (photons) that does **not** require a material medium; it can occur through vacuum. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants Used Throughout Heat Transfer:** > - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ):** $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$ > - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $8.314 \, \text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$ > - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $101.325 \, \text{kPa}$ > - **Freezing point of water:** $0\,^\circ\text{C} = 273.15\,\text{K}$ > - **Boiling point of water:** $100\,^\circ\text{C} = 373.15\,\text{K}$ --- ## 1.5 Why Heat Transfer Matters in Food Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Relevance:** > Almost every unit operation in food engineering — **sterilization of canned foods, pasteurization of milk, drying of grains, baking of bread, frying of snacks, evaporation of juices, and refrigeration of perishables** — is fundamentally a heat transfer problem. The KTU syllabus emphasizes these principles because selecting the right mode of heat transfer and the right equipment (heat exchangers, retorts, dryers) directly impacts **product quality, microbial safety, nutrient retention, and energy efficiency**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mode 1 — Heat Conduction (Diffusion of Thermal Energy) ### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Conduction Conduction occurs when thermal energy is transferred through a medium by: - **Molecular collisions** (in gases and liquids) - **Lattice vibrations / phonons** (in non-metallic solids) - **Free electron diffusion** (in metals — the dominant mechanism) In food systems, conduction is the dominant mode inside **solid foods** (e.g., heat penetrating a piece of meat during roasting) and during **contact heating** operations such as griddle cooking or pan-frying. ### 2.1.2 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (One-Dimensional Steady-State) For a plane wall of thickness $L$, with surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ ($T_1 > T_2$), the steady-state heat transfer rate is given by: $$ \dot{Q} = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx} $$ where: - $\dot{Q}$ = heat transfer rate **(W)** - $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material **(W/m·K)** - $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow **(m²)** - $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow **(K/m)** - The negative sign indicates heat flows from **high** to **low** temperature (Second Law) For a uniform plane wall, this integrates to: $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L} $$ --- ## 2.2 Mode 2 — Heat Convection (Combined Conduction + Advection) ### 2.2.1 Mechanism of Convection Convection involves two simultaneous processes: 1. **Diffusion** of thermal energy across the fluid boundary layer adjacent to the surface (conduction at the microscopic scale). 2. **Advection** — bulk fluid motion carries the absorbed energy away. Convection is classified into two sub-types: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Natural (Free) Convection:** Fluid motion is driven by **buoyancy forces** caused by density differences due to temperature variations (e.g., warm air rising above a hot baking tray). > **Forced Convection:** Fluid motion is driven by an **external agency** such as a fan, pump, or agitator (e.g., air blown by a blower across a food dryer). ### 2.2.2 Newton's Law of Cooling The rate of convective heat transfer between a surface at temperature $T_s$ and a fluid at bulk temperature $T_\infty$ is given by: $$ \dot{Q} = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty) $$ where: - $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient **(W/m²·K)** - $A$ = surface area exposed to convection **(m²)** > [!NOTE] > **Typical Magnitudes of $h$ in Food Processing:** > | Process | $h$ Range (W/m²·K) | > |---|---| > | Natural convection (air) | 5–25 | > | Forced convection (air) | 10–200 | > | Forced convection (water) | 100–20,000 | > | Condensing steam | 5,000–100,000 | --- ## 2.3 Mode 3 — Heat Radiation (Electromagnetic Energy Transfer) ### 2.3.1 Mechanism of Radiation All bodies with a temperature **above absolute zero (0 K)** emit electromagnetic radiation. Unlike conduction and convection, radiation does **not require a medium** and can occur through vacuum. In food processing, radiation heating is used in **infrared baking, microwave heating, and ohmic heating**. ### 2.3.2 Stefan-Boltzmann Law The maximum rate of radiation emitted by a perfect blackbody per unit area is: $$ E_b = \sigma \, T^4 $$ where: - $E_b$ = emissive power of a blackbody **(W/m²)** - $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$ - $T$ = absolute surface temperature **(K)** For a **real (gray) surface** with emissivity $\varepsilon$: $$ E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4 $$ Net radiative exchange between a small surface (1) completely enclosed by a large surface (2): $$ \dot{Q}_{1 \to 2} = \varepsilon_1 \, \sigma \, A_1 \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4) $$ --- ## 2.4 Steady-State vs Unsteady-State Heat Transfer > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steady-State:** Temperature at any point in the system **does not change with time** — heat inflow equals heat outflow at every location. Example: a wall in thermal equilibrium with surroundings on both sides. > **Unsteady-State (Transient):** Temperature **varies with both position and time** — heat is being absorbed or released. Example: heating a can of food in a retort — the cold center gradually warms up over time. Most food processing operations are **transient in nature**. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fourier's Law (general) | $\dot{Q} = -k \, A \, \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$: W/m·K; $A$: m²; $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$: K/m | | 2 | Conduction through plane wall | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | $L$: m; $T_1, T_2$: K or °C | | 3 | Conduction through cylindrical wall | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{2\pi \, k \, L \, (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | $r_1, r_2$: m; $L$: m | | 4 | Thermal Resistance (conduction) | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{k \, A}$ | K/W | | 5 | Thermal Resistance (convection) | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{h \, A}$ | K/W | | 6 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $\dot{Q} = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$: W/m²·K | | 7 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (blackbody) | $E_b = \sigma \, T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ | | 8 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (real surface) | $E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$ | $\varepsilon$: 0 to 1 | | 9 | Net radiation between surfaces | $\dot{Q} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | $T$ in Kelvin (mandatory) | | 10 | Overall heat transfer coefficient | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | $U$: W/m²·K | | 11 | Biot Number | $Bi = \dfrac{h \, L_c}{k}$ | Dimensionless | | 12 | Fourier Number | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \, t}{L_c^2}$ | Dimensionless | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Applications of Heat Transfer Principles:** > - **Heat Exchangers (Pasteurizers):** Use conduction through tube walls and convection between milk and heating medium. > - **Canning / Retort Sterilization:** Involves unsteady-state conduction to determine cold-point lethality (F₀ value). > - **Spray Dryers:** Combine convection (hot air) and evaporation (mass + heat transfer coupled). > - **Microwave / RF Ovens:** Use volumetric radiation heating — heat is generated **inside** the food by dielectric loss. > - **Cold Storage / Freezing:** Involves unsteady-state conduction with possible phase change. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Fourier's Law from First Principles ### 3.1.1 Starting Assumptions Consider an isotropic solid slab of thickness $dx$ with thermal conductivity $k$. The left face is at temperature $T$, and the right face is at temperature $T + dT$. Heat flows in the $+x$ direction. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation **Step 1:** By definition, the rate of heat flow per unit area (heat flux) is proportional to the temperature gradient. $$ q_x \propto \frac{dT}{dx} $$ **Step 2:** Introducing the proportionality constant (thermal conductivity $k$): $$ q_x = -k \, \frac{dT}{dx} $$ **Step 3:** The negative sign is added because heat flows from hot to cold — i.e., in the direction of **decreasing** temperature. Thus, if $T$ decreases in the $+x$ direction, $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ is negative, and $-k \, \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ becomes positive (heat flowing in the $+x$ direction). **Step 4:** Multiplying by the cross-sectional area $A$ to obtain the total heat transfer rate: $$ \dot{Q} = q_x \cdot A = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx} $$ **Step 5:** For a **plane wall of uniform thickness $L$** with constant $k$ and constant surface temperatures $T_1$ (hot side) and $T_2$ (cold side), the temperature gradient becomes constant: $$ \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L} $$ **Step 6:** Substituting back: $$ \dot{Q} = -k \, A \, \left( \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L} \right) = \frac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L} $$ This is the **integrated form of Fourier's Law for a plane wall** — the most commonly used form in KTU problems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Concept of Thermal Resistance (Electrical Analogy) ### 3.2.1 Motivation Heat flow through a composite system is analogous to current flow in an electrical circuit. The "thermal resistance" is the opposition to heat flow. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Consider heat flow through a plane wall under steady-state: $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{k \, A \, \Delta T}{L} $$ **Step 2:** Rearranging to resemble Ohm's Law ($\dot{Q} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$): $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{\dfrac{L}{k \, A}} $$ **Step 3:** Defining thermal resistance: $$ R_{th} = \frac{L}{k \, A} \quad \text{(units: K/W or °C/W)} $$ **Step 4:** Similarly for a convective boundary: $$ \dot{Q} = h \, A \, \Delta T = \frac{\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{h \, A}} $$ Therefore: $$ R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h \, A} $$ **Step 5:** For composite (multi-layer) walls in series, resistances add: $$ R_{total} = R_{conv,i} + R_{cond,1} + R_{cond,2} + \cdots + R_{conv,o} $$ **Step 6:** The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ is defined such that: $$ \dot{Q} = U \, A \, \Delta T $$ Solving for $U$: $$ \frac{1}{U \, A} = \frac{1}{h_i \, A} + \frac{L}{k \, A} + \frac{1}{h_o \, A} $$ Which simplifies to: $$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection in Food Processing | Operation | Dominant Mode(s) | Governing Law | Key Variable | |---|---|---|---| | Retort sterilization of canned food | Conduction (inside food) + Convection (heating medium) | Fourier + Newton | Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ | | Hot-air drying of grains | Forced convection | Newton's Law | Air velocity, $h$ | | Baking bread in oven | Radiation + Natural convection | Stefan-Boltzmann + Newton | Surface emissivity | | Milk pasteurization in PHE | Forced convection + Conduction | Newton + Fourier | $U$-value | | Microwave heating | Radiation (volumetric) | Dielectric loss equation | Dielectric constant | | Frying (deep-fat) | Conduction (in oil) + Convection (bubbles) | Fourier + Newton | Oil viscosity | | Infrared heating of snacks | Radiation | Stefan-Boltzmann | Surface temperature | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Style Numerical > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A stainless-steel plane wall ($k = 16 \, \text{W/m·K}$) has thickness $L = 0.02 \, \text{m}$ and area $A = 1.5 \, \text{m}^2$. The inner surface temperature is $T_1 = 90\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the outer surface is at $T_2 = 70\,^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate: > (a) The steady-state heat transfer rate through the wall. > (b) The thermal resistance of the wall. > (c) The heat flux. **Solution:** **Part (a): Heat transfer rate** Using Fourier's Law for a plane wall: $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L} $$ Substituting values: $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{16 \times 1.5 \times (90 - 70)}{0.02} $$ $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{24 \times 20}{0.02} $$ $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{480}{0.02} = 24{,}000 \, \text{W} = 24 \, \text{kW} $$ **Part (b): Thermal resistance** $$ R_{th} = \frac{L}{k \, A} = \frac{0.02}{16 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.02}{24} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K/W} $$ **Part (c): Heat flux** $$ q = \frac{\dot{Q}}{A} = \frac{24{,}000}{1.5} = 16{,}000 \, \text{W/m}^2 = 16 \, \text{kW/m}^2 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** $q = k \, \dfrac{\Delta T}{L} = 16 \times \dfrac{20}{0.02} = 16{,}000 \, \text{W/m}^2$ ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Modes of Heat Transfer (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Heat Source at Higher Temperature"] --> B["Heat Transfer Mechanism Selection"] B --> C1["Mode 1: Conduction"] B --> C2["Mode 2: Convection"] B --> C3["Mode 3: Radiation"] C1 --> D1["Governed by Fourier's Law"] C2 --> D2["Governed by Newton's Law of Cooling"] C3 --> D3["Governed by Stefan-Boltzmann Law"] D1 --> E1["Solid Foods, Contact Heating, Tube Walls"] D2 --> E2["Pasteurization, Hot Air Drying, Frying"] D3 --> E3["Baking, Infrared, Microwave Heating"] E1 --> F["Heat Reaches Food at Lower Temperature"] E2 --> F["Heat Reaches Food at Lower Temperature"] E3 --> F["Heat Reaches Food at Lower Temperature"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Thermal Resistance Network (Electrical Analogy) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Fluid Inside
Temperature T inf i
h i"] --> A2["R conv i
1 over h i A"] A2 --> A3["R cond
L over k A"] A3 --> A4["R conv o
1 over h o A"] A4 --> A5["Fluid Outside
Temperature T inf o
h o"] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Heat Transfer in a Canned Food Retort (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Steam Supply at High Pressure
T steam approx 121 C"] --> E1["Heat Exchanger Wall
Conduction k metal"] E1 --> E2["Convective Boundary Layer
Inside Retort
h steam"] E2 --> C1["Can Wall
Stainless Steel
Conduction"] C1 --> C2["Food Inside Can
Unsteady Conduction
Governed by Fourier Number Fo"] C2 --> C3["Cold Point
Geometric Center of Can
Lowest Temperature"] C3 --> M1["Microbial Lethality Check
F zero and D value"] M1 --> R1["Sterilization Achieved
if F zero is reached"] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Comparison of the Three Modes ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Conduction K1["Stationary Medium Required"] --> K2["Solid, Liquid, or Gas"] K3["Fourier Law"] --> K4["k in W per m K"] end subgraph Convection L1["Moving Fluid Required"] --> L2["Liquid or Gas"] L3["Newton Law"] --> L4["h in W per m2 K"] end subgraph Radiation M1["No Medium Required"] --> M2["Even Through Vacuum"] M3["Stefan Boltzmann Law"] --> M4["sigma equals 5.67e-8"] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between conduction and convection modes of heat transfer. Give one example of each from food processing.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Conduction | Convection | |---|---|---| | Medium required | Yes (stationary) | Yes (moving fluid) | | Mechanism | Molecular/atomic vibration | Bulk fluid motion + diffusion | | Governing law | Fourier's Law | Newton's Law of Cooling | | Key coefficient | Thermal conductivity $k$ (W/m·K) | Heat transfer coefficient $h$ (W/m²·K) | | Food example | Heat penetration in canned food during retort processing | Cooling of hot bread by forced air in a bakery | --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: State Stefan-Boltzmann law. What is the significance of emissivity in radiation heat transfer?** **Model Answer:** The Stefan-Boltzmann Law states that the total radiation energy emitted per unit surface area of a blackbody is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature: $$ E_b = \sigma \, T^4 $$ For a real (gray) surface: $$ E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4 $$ where $\varepsilon$ is the **emissivity** ($0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$). Emissivity represents the **efficiency** of a real surface in emitting radiation compared to an ideal blackbody. A blackbody has $\varepsilon = 1$, while polished metals have $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$ and most food surfaces have $\varepsilon \approx 0.9$–$0.95$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **A stainless-steel plane wall used in a milk pasteurizer has the following specifications:** - Thickness $L = 0.015 \, \text{m}$ - Area $A = 2.0 \, \text{m}^2$ - Thermal conductivity $k = 16.2 \, \text{W/m·K}$ - Inner surface temperature (milk side): $T_1 = 75\,^\circ\text{C}$ - Outer surface temperature (steam side): $T_2 = 95\,^\circ\text{C}$ **(a)** Calculate the steady-state heat transfer rate through the wall and the thermal resistance. **(b)** If the convective heat transfer coefficient on the steam side is $h_o = 8000 \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ and on the milk side is $h_i = 1500 \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$, calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ and the overall thermal resistance. --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Heat Transfer Rate and Thermal Resistance** Using Fourier's Law for the plane wall: $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{k \, A \, (T_2 - T_1)}{L} $$ Substituting the given values (note: $T_2 > T_1$, heat flows from steam to milk): $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{16.2 \times 2.0 \times (95 - 75)}{0.015} $$ $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{32.4 \times 20}{0.015} $$ $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{648}{0.015} = 43{,}200 \, \text{W} = 43.2 \, \text{kW} $$ The thermal resistance of the wall: $$ R_{cond} = \frac{L}{k \, A} = \frac{0.015}{16.2 \times 2.0} = \frac{0.015}{32.4} = 4.63 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K/W} $$ **Part (b) — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** The total thermal resistance is the sum of three resistances in series: $$ R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_i \, A} + \frac{L}{k \, A} + \frac{1}{h_o \, A} $$ Computing each term: $$ \frac{1}{h_i \, A} = \frac{1}{1500 \times 2.0} = \frac{1}{3000} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K/W} $$ $$ \frac{L}{k \, A} = 4.63 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K/W} \quad \text{(from part a)} $$ $$ \frac{1}{h_o \, A} = \frac{1}{8000 \times 2.0} = \frac{1}{16000} = 6.25 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{K/W} $$ Adding them: $$ R_{total} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} + 4.63 \times 10^{-4} + 6.25 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ R_{total} = (3.333 + 4.630 + 0.625) \times 10^{-4} = 8.588 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K/W} $$ The overall heat transfer coefficient: $$ U = \frac{1}{R_{total} \times A} = \frac{1}{8.588 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0} $$ $$ U = \frac{1}{1.7176 \times 10^{-3}} = 582.2 \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - Many students **forget to multiply** $L/(kA)$ by area when computing $U$ — they use $L/k$ directly. **Always check** whether $R_{th}$ values are area-based (used in $\dot{Q} = \Delta T / R$) or unit-area-based (used in $1/U$). > - In this problem, $R_{cond}$ in **Part (a)** is in K/W (with $A$ included), but in **Part (b)**, when computing $1/U$, you must divide by $A$ **after** summing the resistances. Alternatively, use the unit-area form: $1/U = 1/h_i + L/k + 1/h_o$. > - Students often **swap $T_1$ and $T_2$** — always confirm the direction of heat flow (hot to cold). --- ### Question B [14 Marks] [Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain the three modes of heat transfer with neat sketches and governing equations. Mention the role of each mode in thermal processing of foods. **(b)** A blackbody food product is at temperature $T_1 = 200\,^\circ\text{C}$. It radiates to surrounding walls at $T_2 = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$. If the radiating surface area is $0.5 \, \text{m}^2$, calculate the net radiative heat loss. Given $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$. --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Three Modes of Heat Transfer** > **[Already covered in Sections 1.3, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3 of these notes. The student should write a concise 7-mark answer covering:]** 1. **Conduction:** Fourier's Law, mechanism, role in food processing (heat penetration in solids, sterilization). 2. **Convection:** Newton's Law, free vs forced convection, role in drying, pasteurization. 3. **Radiation:** Stefan-Boltzmann Law, role in baking, infrared heating. A simple textual sketch is sufficient. The student should clearly distinguish between **modes**, **mechanisms**, and **governing laws**. **Part (b) — Net Radiative Heat Loss** **Step 1:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin (mandatory for radiation calculations). $$ T_1 = 200 + 273 = 473 \, \text{K} $$ $$ T_2 = 30 + 273 = 303 \, \text{K} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the net radiation equation. Since the food is a blackbody, $\varepsilon = 1$: $$ \dot{Q}_{net} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4) $$ **Step 3:** Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$. $$ T_1^4 = (473)^4 $$ Intermediate steps: $$ 473^2 = 223{,}729 $$ $$ T_1^4 = (223{,}729)^2 = 5.0054 \times 10^{10} \, \text{K}^4 $$ $$ T_2^4 = (303)^4 $$ Intermediate steps: $$ 303^2 = 91{,}809 $$ $$ T_2^4 = (91{,}809)^2 = 8.4289 \times 10^{9} \, \text{K}^4 $$ **Step 4:** Compute the difference. $$ T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 5.0054 \times 10^{10} - 8.4289 \times 10^{9} $$ $$ T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 5.0054 \times 10^{10} - 0.84289 \times 10^{10} = 4.1625 \times 10^{10} \, \text{K}^4 $$ **Step 5:** Compute net heat loss. $$ \dot{Q}_{net} = 1 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.5 \times 4.1625 \times 10^{10} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{net} = 5.67 \times 0.5 \times 4.1625 \times 10^{2} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{net} = 5.67 \times 0.5 \times 416.25 $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{net} = 2.835 \times 416.25 \approx 1180.07 \, \text{W} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{net} \approx 1.18 \, \text{kW} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** — radiation calculations are invalid in Celsius. Loss of 1–2 marks is common. > - **Calculation errors in $T^4$** — use scientific notation consistently. > - **Using Celsius in Stefan-Boltzmann equation** gives a wrong answer of a completely different order of magnitude. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** > - **Three modes** of heat transfer: **conduction, convection, radiation** — know the governing law and key coefficient for each. > - **Fourier's Law** uses thermal conductivity $k$ (W/m·K); **Newton's Law** uses heat transfer coefficient $h$ (W/m²·K); **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** uses emissivity $\varepsilon$ and requires temperatures in **Kelvin**. > - **Steady-state** means temperatures don't change with time; **unsteady-state** (transient) means they do — most food operations are transient. > - **Thermal resistance** is the key concept for series/parallel composite systems, analogous to electrical resistance. > - **Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$** combines all resistances: $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$. > - **High $k$** = good conductor (metals); **low $k$** = insulator (wood, foam, oil). > - **Blackbody** has $\varepsilon = 1$; **polished metal** has $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$; **most foods** have $\varepsilon \approx 0.9$. > - For radiation, always convert °C to K; for conduction/convection, the difference in °C equals the difference in K. > - **Biot Number** $Bi = hL_c/k$ indicates whether internal conduction resistance is significant compared to surface convection resistance. > - **Fourier Number** $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2$ governs the progress of unsteady-state heating — important for retort process design. > - Typical $h$ ranges: natural convection (air) ~ 5–25, forced convection (air) ~ 10–200, condensing steam ~ 5,000–100,000 W/m²·K.

topics.title: Fundamentals of Heat Transfer in Food
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Heat Transfer in Food

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Processing of Food** relies fundamentally on the science of **heat transfer** — the movement of thermal energy from one region of a food system to another, or from an external heating medium into the food matrix. According to standard food engineering terminology adopted in the KTU 2024 OEFTT616 syllabus, *heat transfer in food* is defined as the study of the rates at which thermal energy is transported through food materials, across food surfaces, and between food and its surrounding medium, governed by the laws of thermodynamics and transport phenomena.

In food systems, heat transfer is critical for:

- **Sterilization** and **pasteurization** (microbial inactivation)
- **Cooking** (starch gelatinization, protein denaturation)
- **Dehydration** and **evaporation** (moisture removal)
- **Freezing** and **refrigeration** (heat extraction)
- **Baking** and **roasting** (Maillard reactions, crust formation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 of OEFTT616 mandates the study of the *three fundamental modes of heat transfer* — **Conduction**, **Convection**, and **Radiation** — and their application to food materials with consideration of thermal properties such as thermal conductivity (*k*), specific heat (*Cp*), density (*ρ*), and thermal diffusivity (*α*).

## 1.2 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer — A First Look

| Mode | Mechanism | Food Processing Example |
|------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Conduction** | Molecular/atomic vibration and electron transfer through a stationary medium | Heat penetrating from the surface of a steak into its center during pan-frying |
| **Convection** | Bulk motion of a fluid (liquid or gas) carrying thermal energy | Hot water blanching of vegetables; forced-air cooling of baked products |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic energy emission from all bodies above absolute zero | Infrared baking of biscuits; microwave heating of ready-to-eat meals |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Cup of Tea Story"

Imagine you pour hot tea (the **heat source**) into a ceramic cup sitting on a steel spoon (the **food/food-contact material**).

- The warmth that creeps slowly from the hot tea through the ceramic wall into your hand is **conduction** — energy moving through a still material, molecule by molecule.
- The swirling motion of the tea as steam rises and mixes the liquid is **convection** — actual chunks of the fluid physically carrying heat along.
- The warmth you feel on your cheek from across the table, without touching the cup, is **radiation** — invisible infrared energy shooting through the air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Food Engineers:** In *conduction* and *convection*, a **medium** is required. In *radiation*, energy can travel through a **vacuum** (e.g., microwave oven, solar drying). All three modes act **simultaneously** in real food processes, but one usually dominates depending on the system.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following constants and metrics must be memorized for the KTU examination:

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ)** = **5.669 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Standard temperature reference** for food: Often **0 °C (273.15 K)** for thermal property tables
- **Unit of heat transfer rate (Q)**: **Watt (W)** = **J·s⁻¹**
- **Unit of heat flux (q″)**: **W·m⁻²**
- **Unit of thermal conductivity (k)**: **W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always quote **units** alongside every numerical answer. KTU evaluators explicitly award the "unit" step (½ to 1 mark) in Part A and Part B numericals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mode 1 — Conduction (The Diffusion of Heat Through Matter)

Conduction is the transfer of thermal energy through a material by **direct molecular contact**, without any bulk movement of the material itself. In food matrices, conduction occurs through:

- Vibrational transfer between adjacent molecules (dominant in solid foods like meat, bread, cheese)
- Translational motion of free electrons (negligible in foods, dominant in metals)
- Lattice vibrations in crystalline regions (relevant in frozen foods)

### 2.1.1 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (1-D Steady State)

For a plane wall of thickness *L* with surface temperatures *T₁* and *T₂*:

$$q_{x} = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- $q_x$ = heat transfer rate in the *x* direction (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material (W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²)
- $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient in the *x* direction (K·m⁻¹)

The **negative sign** indicates that heat flows from higher to lower temperature (down the gradient).

### 2.1.2 Physical Meaning of Thermal Conductivity (*k*)

Thermal conductivity is a **material property** that quantifies a substance's ability to conduct heat. In food systems:

- **High *k*** (e.g., water at 0.61 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, fats at 0.17–0.20 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹): fast heat transfer
- **Low *k*** (e.g., air at 0.026 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, porous dried foods): acts as **insulator**

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Engineering Insight:** Frozen foods have *k* values 3–4 times higher than the same food unfrozen because ice (≈ 2.2 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹) conducts heat much better than water. This is why frozen meat "feels cold faster" to the touch than thawed meat at the same temperature.

### 2.1.3 Thermal Diffusivity (α)

Thermal diffusivity combines three important properties and governs the **rate of temperature change** within a food:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, C_{p}}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²·s⁻¹)
- $\rho$ = density of the food (kg·m⁻³)
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity at constant pressure (J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹)

A high α means the food will reach thermal equilibrium with its surroundings **quickly** — important in sterilization design.

## 2.2 Mode 2 — Convection (Heat Transfer by Fluid Motion)

Convection involves heat transfer between a **surface** and a **moving fluid**. In food processing, common convective situations are:

- **Forced convection**: hot air blast in a tray dryer, scraped surface heat exchanger
- **Natural (free) convection**: still air cooling of bread on a rack
- **Condensing steam**: in retort sterilization
- **Boiling**: in evaporators concentrating milk or fruit juice

### 2.2.1 Newton's Law of Cooling

$$q = h \, A \, (T_{s} - T_{\infty})$$

Where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W·m⁻²·K⁻¹)
- $A$ = surface area in contact with fluid (m²)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature of the food (K or °C)
- $T_{\infty}$ = bulk temperature of the fluid far from the surface (K or °C)

### 2.2.2 Typical Values of *h* in Food Processing

| Process | Approx. *h* (W·m⁻²·K⁻¹) |
|---------|--------------------------|
| Air, natural convection | **5 – 25** |
| Air, forced convection (tunnel dryer) | **30 – 300** |
| Water, natural convection | **100 – 600** |
| Water, forced convection | **500 – 20,000** |
| Condensing steam | **5,000 – 100,000** |
| Boiling water | **2,500 – 50,000** |

## 2.3 Mode 3 — Radiation (Energy Transfer by Electromagnetic Waves)

Thermal radiation is emitted by **all bodies** with temperature above 0 K. Unlike conduction and convection, no medium is required.

### 2.3.1 Stefan-Boltzmann Law

$$q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^{4}$$

Where:
- $q$ = radiative heat transfer rate (W)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of the surface (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1, dimensionless)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.669 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- $A$ = surface area (m²)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 2.3.2 Net Radiation Exchange Between Two Surfaces

For a small food object (surface 1) inside a large enclosure (surface 2):

$$q_{1 \leftrightarrow 2} = \varepsilon_{1} \, \sigma \, A_{1} \, (T_{1}^{4} - T_{2}^{4})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Application:** In **infrared baking** of pizza, the oven walls reach 250–300 °C (523–573 K), and emissivities of 0.85–0.95 are common for the ceramic walls. The T⁴ dependence means a small temperature increase produces a **large** radiation gain.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table** — questions in KTU ESE frequently require direct formula substitution and unit consistency.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Key Variables & Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Fourier's Law (1-D) | $q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$: W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, $A$: m², $\frac{dT}{dx}$: K·m⁻¹ |
| 2 | Steady state plane wall | $q = \frac{kA}{L}(T_1 - T_2)$ | $L$: thickness (m) |
| 3 | Steady state hollow cylinder | $q = \frac{2\pi L k (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | $r_1, r_2$: inner/outer radii (m) |
| 4 | Steady state hollow sphere | $q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)}$ | $r_1, r_2$: inner/outer radii (m) |
| 5 | Thermal diffusivity | $\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho C_p}$ | $\alpha$: m²·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$: W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ |
| 7 | Overall heat transfer coefficient | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | $U$: W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ |
| 8 | Fourier number (Fo) | $Fo = \frac{\alpha \, t}{L^2}$ | $t$: time (s) |
| 9 | Biot number (Bi) | $Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k}$ | $L_c$: characteristic length (m) |
| 10 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | $\sigma$ = 5.669 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |
| 11 | Net radiation between surfaces | $q = \varepsilon_1 \sigma A_1 (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | $T$ in Kelvin (mandatory) |
| 12 | Lumped capacitance cooling | $\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{hA}{\rho V C_p} t\right)$ | $V$: volume (m³) |
| 13 | Heat capacity rate | $Q = m C_p \Delta T$ | $m$: mass (kg) |
| 14 | Sensible heat (food) | $Q = m C_p (T_f - T_i)$ | $T_f, T_i$: final/initial T (K) |
| 15 | Latent heat (phase change) | $Q = m \lambda$ | $\lambda$: latent heat (J·kg⁻¹) |

## 2.5 Dimensionless Numbers — Critical for Food Process Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **Biot (Bi)** and **Fourier (Fo)** numbers under Module 1. Memorize their definitions and physical meanings.

- **Biot number (Bi):** Ratio of internal conduction resistance to surface convective resistance.
  - If **Bi < 0.1** → **Lumped capacitance analysis is valid** (uniform internal temperature)
  - If **Bi > 0.1** → internal gradients exist; full transient conduction analysis required

- **Fourier number (Fo):** Dimensionless time representing the ratio of heat conducted to heat stored.
  - $Fo \rightarrow \infty$ → thermal equilibrium reached
  - $Fo \rightarrow 0$ → initial state retained

- **Characteristic length ($L_c$):** Defined as **volume / surface area** for lumped analysis, or **thickness/2** for a slab.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The fundamentals of heat transfer are the **design basis** for:

1. **Retort sterilization** — ensuring the slowest-heating point in a can reaches the required *F₀* value for *Clostridium botulinum* inactivation.
2. **Plate heat exchangers (PHE)** in pasteurization of milk — using *U* and log-mean temperature difference (LMTD).
3. **Spray dryers** — coupling convection with simultaneous moisture evaporation.
4. **Microwave ovens** — volumetric heating by radiation at 2.45 GHz, where penetration depth of electromagnetic waves in food is ~1–3 cm.
5. **Cold storage design** — calculating *U*-values of insulated walls to determine refrigeration load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Heat Conduction Through a Plane Wall

**Statement to derive:** $q = \frac{kA}{L}(T_1 - T_2)$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state ($\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$)
2. One-dimensional heat flow in *x* direction
3. Constant thermal conductivity *k*
4. No internal heat generation

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Starting from Fourier's law in 1-D:

$$q_{x} = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx}$$

At steady state, $q_x$ is constant along *x* (no accumulation), so we separate variables and integrate between the two surfaces of the wall:

$$q_{x} \int_{0}^{L} dx = -k \, A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Logic:** The heat flux must equal the constant flow through every cross-section, so we can integrate the differential form across the wall thickness.

$$q_{x} [x]_{0}^{L} = -k \, A [T]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

Evaluating both sides:

$$q_{x} \cdot L = -k \, A (T_2 - T_1)$$

Simplifying by removing the negative sign (heat flows from hot to cold, so we take $T_1 > T_2$):

$$q_{x} \cdot L = k \, A (T_1 - T_2)$$

Solving for $q_x$:

$$\boxed{q_{x} = \frac{k \, A}{L} (T_1 - T_2)}$$

**Result:** This is the **fundamental plane-wall conduction equation**, used for computing heat loss through insulated walls of cold rooms, retort shells, and oven panels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Heat Conduction Through a Hollow Cylinder

**Statement to derive:** $q = \frac{2\pi L k (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state, 1-D radial conduction
2. Constant *k*
3. No internal heat generation
4. Cylinder length *L* ≫ radii, so end effects are neglected

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For a hollow cylinder, the cross-sectional area at any radius *r* is $A = 2\pi r L$. Substituting into Fourier's law in radial form:

$$q_{r} = -k \, (2\pi r L) \, \frac{dT}{dr}$$

At steady state, $q_r$ is constant. Separate variables:

$$q_{r} \, \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \, dT$$

Integrate from inner radius $r_1$ (temperature $T_1$) to outer radius $r_2$ (temperature $T_2$):

$$q_{r} \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$q_{r} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = -2\pi k L (T_2 - T_1)$$

Removing the negative sign by recognizing $T_1 > T_2$:

$$\boxed{q_{r} = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Application in food industry:** Used to design **tubular heat exchangers** for pasteurization of liquid foods (milk, juice) and for sizing **steam-jacketed kettles** used in jam/sauce production.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Diffusivity and the Lumped Capacitance Model

**Starting energy balance for a food particle cooling in a fluid:**

A small food particle of mass *m*, surface area *A*, and volume *V* is cooled by convection. Internal conduction is assumed fast (Bi < 0.1), so the food has a **uniform temperature *T(t)*** that changes with time.

Energy balance (Rate of heat lost by food = Rate of convective heat transfer from food to fluid):

$$-m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A (T - T_{\infty})$$

**Logic:** The food loses sensible heat, which is removed by the surrounding fluid. The negative sign indicates the food is cooling.

Rearrange to separate variables:

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_{\infty}} = -\frac{hA}{m C_p} dt$$

Since $m = \rho V$:

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_{\infty}} = -\frac{hA}{\rho V C_p} dt$$

Integrate from initial state $(t=0, T=T_i)$ to a later state $(t=t, T=T)$:

$$\int_{T_i}^{T} \frac{dT}{T - T_{\infty}} = -\frac{hA}{\rho V C_p} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}}\right) = -\frac{hA}{\rho V C_p} t$$

Exponentiating:

$$\boxed{\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{hA}{\rho V C_p} t\right)}$$

This is the **Lumped Capacitance Equation**, used for:

- Cooling of small fruits/vegetables in a chiller
- Heating of small droplets in spray drying
- Thawing of small meat pieces (after the phase change)

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Check:** This equation is valid **only when Bi < 0.1**. Always verify this condition **before** applying the lumped model in KTU exam problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Fundamental Radiation Principles

For completeness, the total emissive power of a **black body** is obtained by integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths:

$$E_{b} = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$$

Planck's spectral emissive power:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5} \left[\exp(C_2 / \lambda T) - 1\right]}$$

The definite integral evaluates to:

$$E_{b} = \sigma T^{4}$$

For a real (gray) surface with emissivity ε, the emitted power is reduced:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

Total emitted energy from surface of area *A*:

$$\boxed{q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}}$$

This is the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**, central to infrared baking, oven design, and radiation sterilization.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Heat Transfer Problems

| Problem Scenario | Governing Mode | Key Equation | Required Properties |
|------------------|----------------|--------------|---------------------|
| Heat loss through metal wall of retort | Steady conduction | $q = \frac{kA}{L}(T_1 - T_2)$ | *k* of metal |
| Heat flow through steam pipe lagging | Steady conduction (cylinder) | $q = \frac{2\pi L k (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | *k* of insulation |
| Cooling of peas in cold water | Transient convection (lumped) | $\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{hA}{\rho V C_p} t\right)$ | *h, ρ, Cp* |
| Surface sterilization by infrared | Radiation | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | *ε, σ, T* |
| Pasteurization in PHE | Convection + conduction | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | *U, A, LMTD* |

> [!TIP]
> **Step-by-Step Solution Approach (for KTU problems):**
> 1. **Identify the dominant mode** of heat transfer from the problem statement.
> 2. **Sketch the system** with boundary labels (T₁, T₂, L, r, etc.).
> 3. **Check steady vs. transient** and **lumped vs. distributed**.
> 4. **List known quantities with units**.
> 5. **Select the appropriate equation** from the formula sheet.
> 6. **Substitute, solve, and box the final answer with units.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4:** Just as electrical resistances in series add to give a total resistance, **thermal resistances in series add** to give the total resistance from hot to cold fluid. The heat transfer rate is then: $q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{total}}$, analogous to Ohm's law.
