If the process temperature accidentally drops to 118 °C, what would be the new $F_0$, and what is the percentage loss in lethality? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Construction:**
A hydrostatic sterilizer is a continuous retort consisting of:
1. **Inlet water column** (≈ 18–20 m tall) — filled with hot water for pre-heating and sealing the steam chamber at the bottom.
2. **Steam chest** (sterilizing zone) — a horizontal pressure vessel containing saturated steam at 121.1 °C.
3. **Outlet water column** (similar height) — for cooling the cans and sealing the steam chamber on the exit side.
4. **Conveyor system** — carries cans through the three zones at controlled speeds (chain or belt type).

**Working:**
- Cans enter via the inlet water column and pre-heat gradually as they descend toward the steam chest.
- They pass through the steam chest, where they reach and hold at the sterilizing temperature (121.1 °C).
- They then ascend the outlet water column, cooling progressively.
- The hydrostatic head of water in both columns provides the pressure to contain the steam — no pressure vessel is required for the steam zone.

**Advantages over still retorts:**
- Continuous operation → high throughput.
- Uniform processing → better quality.
- No pressure vessel → lower capital and energy cost.
- Suitable for large containers (no. 10 cans).
- Reduced labor cost (automated).

*(Diagram expected for full marks: show three zones, can path, water heights.)*

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — $F_0$ at 121.1 °C for 18 min:**

$$F_0 = t \times 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{z}} = 18 \times 10^{\frac{121.1 - 121.1}{10}} = 18 \times 10^{0} = 18.0 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — $F_0$ at 118 °C for 18 min:**

$$F_0 = 18 \times 10^{\frac{118 - 121.1}{10}} = 18 \times 10^{-0.31}$$

Computing $10^{-0.31}$:
$$10^{-0.31} = \frac{1}{10^{0.31}} \approx \frac{1}{2.042} \approx 0.4897$$

Therefore:
$$F_0 = 18 \times 0.4897 \approx 8.81 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage loss in lethality:**

$$\text{Loss \%} = \frac{18.0 - 8.81}{18.0} \times 100 = \frac{9.19}{18.0} \times 100 \approx 51.1 \text{ \%}$$

**Conclusion:** A 3.1 °C temperature drop below reference (118 °C vs 121.1 °C) results in a **~51 % loss in sterilizing value**, dropping $F_0$ from 18 min to 8.81 min. This is why temperature monitoring and control are critical in retort operations.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for computation at 121.1, 2 for computation at 118, 1 for conclusion.)*

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **aseptic processing system using a plate heat exchanger (PHE)**. Discuss the role of the **regeneration section** in energy efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is $F_0$ value? Why is *Clostridium botulinum* chosen as the reference organism? A heat penetration study shows that the cold spot of a can takes 12 minutes to reach 121.1 °C and then holds for 14 minutes. Calculate the minimum $F_0$ delivered. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Construction of Aseptic PHE System:**
1. **Balance tank** — holds raw product, prevents pump cavitation.
2. **Regeneration section** — incoming cold product is heated by outgoing hot product across plate surfaces.
3. **Final heating section** — steam-heated plates raise product to UHT temperature (135–150 °C).
4. **Holding tube** — provides the precise residence time (2–8 s for UHT).
5. **Regeneration section (cooling side)** — hot product gives up heat to incoming product.
6. **Final cooling section** — chilled water/ice-water plates cool product to 4–5 °C.
7. **Aseptic filler** — fills pre-sterilized containers in a sterile environment.

**Working:**
- Product flows through the plate stack, gaining heat progressively.
- Steam side flows counter-current, ensuring the final plate sees the highest temperature steam.
- The holding tube's residence time is critical — it must be long enough to deliver required lethality, short enough to minimize quality damage.

**Role of Regeneration Section:**
- Heat from the *outgoing hot sterilized product* pre-warms the *incoming cold raw product*.
- Typical regeneration efficiency: **85–95 %**.
- This means that of the total heat input needed, only **5–15 %** must be supplied by fresh steam.
- **Energy savings:** For a 1 L/s milk plant, regeneration can save **> 90 % of steam** compared to a non-regenerative system.
- **Additional benefit:** The hot product is also pre-cooled in regeneration, reducing chilled water demand.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Definition of $F_0$:**
$F_0$ is the equivalent time, in minutes, that a product would need to be held at the reference temperature of **121.1 °C** to deliver the same lethal effect as the actual time-temperature history experienced by the product. Mathematically:

$$F_0 = \int 10^{\frac{T(t) - 121.1}{z}} dt$$

**Why *C. botulinum* is the reference organism:**
- It is the **most heat-resistant foodborne pathogen of public health significance** in low-acid canned foods.
- Its toxin is **lethal in trace amounts** (≈ 1 µg).
- It produces **heat-resistant spores**.
- The **12-D concept** (12-decimal reduction, $F_0 = 12 \times 0.21 = 2.52$ min) is the basis of botulinum safety.
- Conservative KTU/food industry standard uses **$F_0 \geq 3.0$ min** for an extra safety margin.

**Calculation:**
- Come-up time to 121.1 °C: 12 min (no lethality assumed, since temperatures < 121.1 °C contribute negligibly; conservative assumption).
- Hold time at 121.1 °C: 14 min.

$$F_0 = 14 \times 10^{\frac{121.1 - 121.1}{10}} = 14 \times 1 = 14.0 \text{ min}$$

**Conclusion:** $F_0 = 14.0$ min, which is well above the minimum 3.0 min standard — the process is microbiologically safe with a large safety margin.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for choosing C. botulinum, 3 for calculation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in $F_0$ calculation.** Always state "minutes" — losing this loses a half-mark.
> 2. **Confusing D-value and z-value.** D is *time* (min), z is *temperature* (°C). Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Using 121 °C instead of 121.1 °C** in $F_0$ calculations — small error, but shows lack of precision.
> 4. **Skipping the come-up time in $F_0$ problems** when given. The CUT may or may not be included; read the question carefully.
> 5. **Drawing retort diagrams without labels** — KTU awards marks for labeled diagrams (input, output, steam, water, pressure gauge).
> 6. **Confusing still and rotary retorts** in construction questions — rotary retorts have an internal rotating reel/rack; still retorts do not.
> 7. **Mixing up direct vs. indirect UHT** — direct uses steam injection (dilutes product), indirect uses heat exchangers (no dilution).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist — Equipment Used for Sterilization**

- [ ] **Sterilization** in food processing aims at **commercial sterility**, not absolute sterility.
- [ ] The **reference target** is *Clostridium botulinum* with $D_{121.1} = 0.21$ min, $z = 10$ °C.
- [ ] **Minimum safe $F_0$** for low-acid canned foods = **3.0 min** (12-D principle).
- [ ] **Still retort:** static, conduction-based, slow, low-cost, simple.
- [ ] **Rotary retort:** agitated, faster heat transfer, better quality, higher cost.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic continuous sterilizer:** uses water columns (≈ 20 m) to generate steam pressure; ideal for high-volume, large cans.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic height formula:** $h = P/(\rho g)$ — memorize the substitution.
- [ ] **Aseptic PHE:** three sections — regeneration, heating, cooling; **regeneration efficiency 85–95 %** saves energy.
- [ ] **UHT temperatures:** **135–150 °C for 2–8 s**; preserves quality.
- [ ] **Steam injection (direct UHT):** adds water to product → must flash-cool; steam infusion (product into steam) is the alternative.
- [ ] **Scraped-surface HE:** for highly viscous/particulate products; high shear.
- [ ] **Ohmic & microwave:** athermal/volumetric heating; suitable for particulate liquids and premium products.
- [ ] **Retort cooling must use counter-pressure** (pressurized air first) to prevent can buckling.
- [ ] **Lethal rate $L = 10^{(T-121.1)/z}$** — the *per-minute lethality contribution*.
- [ ] **A 5 °C rise in retort temperature (with $z = 10$ °C) increases $F_0$ by a factor of $10^{0.5} \approx 3.16$.**
- [ ] **Lethality during come-up time is small but not zero** — include in precise calculations using $F_0 = \sum L \cdot \Delta t$.
- [ ] **Cold spot identification** is critical for process validation; usually at the geometric center of conduction-heating cans.
- [ ] **CIP (Cleaning-In-Place)** is integral to all modern sterilization equipment for hygienic operation.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 2 — Equipment Used for Sterilization**
> **Course:** OEFTT616 — Thermal Processing of Food | **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University, Kerala | 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects on Food Quality and Nutrition
Blanching: Principles and Objectives of Blanching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Blanching: Principles and Objectives of Blanching # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Blanching ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > **Blanching** is a *preliminary heat treatment* applied to fruits and vegetables (and occasionally some animal tissues) by exposing them to hot water or steam at temperatures typically ranging from **75 °C to 100 °C** for a brief, controlled duration (**1 to 10 minutes**), primarily aimed at **inactivating native and microbial enzymes** that would otherwise cause deterioration in color, flavor, texture, and nutritional quality during subsequent processing and storage. In the strict sense of the KTU 2024 Food Technology syllabus, blanching is the **bridge operation** between raw-material preparation and the main preservation step (such as freezing, canning, or dehydration). It is *not* a sterilization step — its purpose is **enzyme inactivation**, not microbial destruction. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Chemical Lock-Down" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition):** > Think of a freshly cut apple slice. Leave it on the table, and within minutes it turns brown. What you are seeing is **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** — an enzyme — reacting with oxygen in the air and phenolic compounds in the apple. Now imagine dipping that same apple slice in boiling water for *just thirty seconds*. The browning disappears — not because the oxygen is gone, but because the **enzyme has been thermally destroyed** (its 3-D protein structure has "unfolded" and locked into a non-functional shape, just like crumpling a paperclip so it can no longer hold papers). > > That is blanching — a *surgical* heat treatment designed to **selectively disable enzymes without cooking the food**. ## 1.3 Why Blanching Matters in Food Engineering - Vegetables and fruits harvested from the field carry **living cells with active metabolic machinery**. - The most important trouble-makers are enzymes of the **oxidoreductase** and **hydrolase** classes. - These enzymes survive ordinary cooking minimally and survive freezing/dehydration **very well** — meaning a frozen, unblanched vegetable will still turn brown, mushy, and rancid in your freezer within weeks. - Hence blanching is the *gate-keeper* of the entire downstream thermal processing chain. > [!TIP] > **Key Board Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Why is blanching done before freezing/canning/dehydration?"*, your model answer must begin with **"To inactivate enzymes…"** — never "to kill germs" (that is pasteurization, a different operation). ## 1.4 Standard Processing Metrics (Must Memorize) | Metric | Typical Range | |---|---| | Water blanching temperature | **75 – 100 °C** | | Steam blanching temperature | **100 °C (saturated)** | | Residence time | **1 – 10 minutes** (size-dependent) | | Indicator enzyme | **Peroxidase** (most heat-resistant) | | Inactivation target | **≥ 90 % destruction of peroxidase** | | pH range for optimum activity | **pH 4 – 7** | | Critical water:product ratio | **5 : 1 to 10 : 1** | | Blancher capacity | **kg product / m² belt area** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis — Principles, Mechanism & Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Principle: Thermal Enzyme Inactivation The principle of blanching rests on the **thermal denaturation kinetics of enzymes**. Enzymes are globular proteins; their catalytic activity depends on a precise 3-D conformation (the *tertiary structure*) maintained by weak hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions. Heat supplies enough kinetic energy to disrupt these bonds, causing the enzyme to **unfold irreversibly**. ### 2.1.1 First-Order Inactivation Kinetics The thermal destruction of enzymes follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to active enzyme concentration. $$\frac{dE}{dt} = -k \cdot E(t)$$ Where: - $E(t)$ = residual enzyme activity at time $t$ (in **units of activity / g**) - $k$ = thermal destruction rate constant (**min⁻¹**), itself an Arrhenius function of temperature - $t$ = exposure time (**minutes**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that enzyme inactivation is *exponential*, not linear. This means the **first second of blanching destroys more enzyme than the last second**. Hence commercial blanchers are sized using **logarithmic** calculations, not simple arithmetic averages. ### 2.1.2 D-Value (Decimal Reduction Time) A central concept borrowed from microbiology: $$D = \frac{2.303}{k}$$ Where **D** is the time (at a reference temperature $T_{ref}$) required to reduce the enzyme activity to **10 %** of its original value (i.e., a *1-log* reduction). ### 2.1.3 Z-Value (Thermal Resistance Constant) $$z = \frac{T_{ref} - T}{\log_{10}(D_{ref}) - \log_{10}(D)}$$ Typical Z-value for vegetable peroxidase is approximately **z ≈ 14 °C to 25 °C**, meaning the D-value drops by a factor of 10 for every 14–25 °C rise in temperature. ## 2.2 The Five Engineering Objectives of Blanching > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Favourite — 14-Mark Theory Question:** *"Discuss the principles and objectives of blanching in food processing."* Memorize the **5 objectives** below as numbered pillars. ### Objective 1 — **Enzyme Inactivation (Primary Goal)** - Target enzymes in vegetables: **Peroxidase (POD)**, **Catalase (CAT)**, **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)**, **Lipoxygenase (LOX)**, **Ascorbic Acid Oxidase (AAO)**. - In fruits: **PPO** is the chief culprit. - Peroxidase is used as the **indicator enzyme** because it has the **highest thermal resistance** — once POD is destroyed, all other enzymes are assumed dead. ### Objective 2 — **Texture Modification (Pectin Methyl-Esterase Activation)** - A *gentle* blanch activates **Pectin Methyl-Esterase (PME)**, which demethylates pectin and creates Ca²⁺ cross-bridges. - This **firm-fixes** the tissue, preventing excessive softening during canning. - Over-blanching, however, solubilizes pectin → tissue becomes mushy. ### Objective 3 — **Color Preservation** - Destroys oxidative enzymes → prevents **enzymatic browning** (PPO) and **pinking** (peroxidase in radish, horseradish). - In green vegetables (peas, beans, spinach), blanching **fixes chlorophyll** by releasing intracellular acids and converting chlorophyll to **pheophytin** under controlled conditions. ### Objective 4 — **Intercellular Gas Expulsion** - Heating expands the gases trapped in intercellular spaces. - During subsequent vacuum-drying, vacuum-frying, or vacuum-packaging, this pre-expulsion prevents **shrinkage, collapse, and case-hardening**. - Result: a **porous** structure ideal for rehydration and crisp texture. ### Objective 5 — **Microbial Load Reduction & Cleaning** - Reduces surface microbial count by **1 to 2 log cycles** (90 % – 99 %). - Removes soil, insects, pesticide residues, and field heat. - *Note:* Blanching is *not* sterilization — surviving spores germinate during storage if the product is improperly handled. ## 2.3 The Principles (How It Works — The Thermodynamic Logic) | Step | What Happens Inside the Cell | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | **1. Heat Transfer Begins** | Hot water/steam transfers sensible heat from the medium → food surface → food interior. | Surface blanching precedes interior blanching; **lag phase** exists. | | **2. Intercellular Gas Expulsion** | Thermal expansion of air/gases inside the tissue. | Volume ↑ by 2–3 %; prep for vacuum processing. | | **3. Protein Denaturation** | Hydrogen bonds in enzyme tertiary structure break; hydrophobic groups expose. | Catalytic activity → 0. | | **4. Pectin & Cell Wall Changes** | PME activates; protopectin → pectin; turgor pressure collapses. | Slight softening; controlled firmness. | | **5. Surface Sterilization** | Vegetative microbes + spores partially destroyed. | Extended shelf life; reduced thermal load downstream. | | **6. Cooling Phase** | Cold water dip or moving air stops the cook immediately. | Preserves color, nutrients, and texture. | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Note:** Always emphasize the **cooling step**. Many students forget to mention that *cooling immediately halts the process* and prevents over-cooking. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction point if missed. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick-Reference) | # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Typical Value (Food Eng.) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{dE}{dt} = -kE$ | First-order enzyme inactivation | — | | 2 | $E(t) = E_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Residual enzyme activity | — | | 3 | $\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_t}\right) = \dfrac{t}{D}$ | Log reduction of enzyme | — | | 4 | $D = \dfrac{2.303}{k}$ | Decimal reduction time | POD ≈ **1 – 3 min @ 90 °C** | | 5 | $\dfrac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{\,(T_2-T_1)/z}$ | Z-value relationship | z_POD ≈ **14 – 25 °C** | | 6 | $k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence | $E_a \approx$ **40 – 80 kJ/mol** | | 7 | $Q_{10} = \dfrac{k_{(T+10)}}{k_T} \approx 2$ to $3$ | Temperature quotient | POD ≈ **2.5** | | 8 | $F_0 = 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z} \cdot t$ | Lethality / Equivalent blanching time | $T_{ref} = 90$ °C | | 9 | Leaching loss $L = \dfrac{C_0 - C_f}{C_0} \times 100$ | % nutrient loss in water blanch | **5 – 25 % solids** | | 10 | $h = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T}$ | Heat-transfer coefficient | $h_{steam} \gg h_{water}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Mandate — Pipe Symbol Rule:** Notice that the table above uses **plain parentheses** like `E_0`, `E_t`, `(T+10)`, etc. — never a literal vertical bar `|` inside the table cells. This is mandatory for clean markdown rendering in the KTU study portal. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Frozen peas industry** — Every freezer uses a *continuous steam blancher* operating at exactly 95–100 °C for 1.5–3 min, followed by a cold-water chiller. - **Dehydrated onion/garlic flakes** — Hot water blanching removes the pungent sulfur compounds and prevents pink discoloration. - **Infant formula and canned baby corn** — Blanching with steam preserves bright green/yellow hues. - **Vacuum-fried fruits** — Pre-blanch expels internal air, giving the *porous*, low-density crunch. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Enzyme Inactivation Equation (Full, Exhaustive) **Starting from the fundamental first-order postulate:** $$\frac{dE}{dt} = -k \cdot E(t)$$ ### Step 1 — Variable Separation Rearrange so that all $E$-terms are on one side and $t$ on the other: $$\frac{dE}{E(t)} = -k \, dt$$ ### Step 2 — Integration Between Limits Integrate from the initial condition $t = 0$, $E = E_0$ to the current condition $t = t$, $E = E(t)$: $$\int_{E_0}^{E(t)} \frac{dE}{E} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integrals The left integral is a standard logarithmic form: $$\big[\ln E\big]_{E_0}^{E(t)} = -k \, [t]_{0}^{t}$$ Therefore: $$\ln E(t) - \ln E_0 = -kt$$ ### Step 4 — Simplify to the Exponential Form $$\ln\!\left(\frac{E(t)}{E_0}\right) = -kt$$ Exponentiating both sides: $$E(t) = E_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to the Log-Reduction Form Used in Food Engineering Take base-10 logarithm (more convenient since D is defined per decade): $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E(t)}\right) = \frac{k \cdot t}{2.303}$$ ### Step 6 — Introduce the D-Value By definition, $D$ is the time for a **10-fold** (i.e., *1-log*) reduction in enzyme activity. So when $E(t)/E_0 = 0.1$: $$\log_{10}(10) = 1 = \frac{k \cdot D}{2.303}$$ Hence: $$D = \frac{2.303}{k}$$ ### Step 7 — Final Working Equation for KTU Problems $$\boxed{\;\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_t}\right) = \frac{t}{D}\;}$$ This is the **single most-tested equation** for blanching problems in the KTU board exam. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Time Required to Inactivate 99 % of Peroxidase in Green Peas > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):** > Green peas are steam-blanched at **95 °C**. The decimal reduction time (D-value) for peroxidase in peas at 95 °C is **D = 1.5 min**. Calculate the time required to inactivate **99 %** of the peroxidase. ### Step A — Identify Given Data - $T = 95$ °C - $D_{95} = 1.5$ min - Required destruction: $99\,\% \Rightarrow E_t / E_0 = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01$ ### Step B — Compute the Log-Reduction $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_t}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{0.01}\right) = \log_{10}(100) = 2$$ ### Step C — Apply the Working Equation $$\frac{t}{D} = 2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 2 \cdot D = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ min}$$ ### Step D — Final Answer $$\boxed{\;t = 3.0 \text{ minutes}\;}$$ > [!TIP] > **Practical Engineering Insight:** Real industrial blanchers add a **safety margin of 50 %** to the calculated time. Hence the commercial hot-water residence time for peas at 95 °C is closer to **4.5 min** to ensure POD is *completely* destroyed. The factory operator must also account for **come-up time** (the time for the *cold centre* of the pea to reach 95 °C), which is often longer than the kinetic destruction time itself. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Blanching Methods | Parameter | **Hot Water Blanch** | **Steam Blanch** | **Microwave Blanch** | **Ohmic Blanch** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Temperature | 75–100 °C | 100 °C | 80–100 °C | 80–100 °C | | Heat transfer | Convection (medium) | Condensation (high) | Volumetric dielectric | Volumetric Joule | | Uniformity | Poor (boundary layer) | Good | Excellent | Excellent | | Nutrient leaching | **High** (15–25 %) | Low (5–10 %) | Negligible | Negligible | | Water usage | **Very high** | None | None | Minimal | | Energy efficiency | Low | Medium | High | High | | Capital cost | Low | Medium | High | High | | **Best for** | Root veg., peas | Leafy greens, beans | Premium products | Pumpable purees | | Industrial example | Carrots, peas | Spinach, broccoli | IQF berries | Tomato paste | --- ## 3.4 Quantitative Trade-off — Leaching Loss Calculation Leaching losses in water blanching are quantified by: $$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{C_{\text{raw}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{raw}}} \times 100$$ Where: - $C_{\text{raw}}$ = concentration of water-soluble nutrient (e.g., Vitamin C, sugars, minerals) in the raw food - $C_{\text{after}}$ = concentration in the blanched food > **Example:** Carrots lose **15 – 30 % of their sugar**, **10 – 20 % of minerals**, and **10 – 25 % of Vitamin C** during a 5-minute hot-water blanch at 95 °C. This is the **central engineering trade-off** that pushes industry toward **steam or microwave** systems for premium products. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — Single-Stage Hot-Water Blancher (Continuous Type) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Material
Receiving Hopper] --> B[Pre-Wash
Drum Washer] B --> C[Inspection
Conveyor Belt] C --> D[Hot Water Blanching Tank
75 to 100 Degree C] D --> E[Steam Injection
Heater Bank] E --> D D --> F[Cold Water Chiller
10 to 15 Degree C] F --> G[De-watering Vibratory Screen] G --> H[Next Process Stage
Freezing or Drying] H --> I[Blanch Effluent to ETP] style A fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3c763d,color:#000 style D fill:#fcf8e3,stroke:#8a6d3b,color:#000 style F fill:#d9edf7,stroke:#31708f,color:#000 style H fill:#f2dede,stroke:#a94442,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - The product enters at **(A)**, gets cleaned at **(B)**, sorted at **(C)**, and enters the *main blanching tank* **(D)**. > - Inside **(D)**, live steam is injected via **(E)** to maintain the precise temperature band (75 – 100 °C). > - After the residence time (1–10 min) the product exits to a **chiller (F)** — this *stops the cook* instantly. > - **(G)** removes surface water, and **(H)** routes the product to the next preservation step. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — The Five Objectives of Blanching ```mermaid flowchart TD BL[Blanching Operation] --> O1[Objective 1
Enzyme Inactivation] BL --> O2[Objective 2
Texture Modification] BL --> O3[Objective 3
Color Preservation] BL --> O4[Objective 4
Intercellular Gas Expulsion] BL --> O5[Objective 5
Microbial Load Reduction] O1 --> E1[POD most heat resistant] O1 --> E2[PPO destroyed] O1 --> E3[LOX destroyed] O2 --> T1[PME activation] O2 --> T2[Calcium pectate bridges] O3 --> C1[Stops enzymatic browning] O3 --> C2[Fixes green color in chlorophyll] O4 --> G1[Enables vacuum drying] O4 --> G2[Improves rehydration] O5 --> M1[Reduces count by 1 to 2 log] O5 --> M2[Removes field heat] style BL fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000 style O1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000 style O2 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000 style O3 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 style O4 fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000 style O5 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#9c640c,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Steam Blancher Control System ```mermaid flowchart LR FEED[Feed Conveyor
Variable Speed Drive] --> SEAL[Steam Sealing
Section A] SEAL --> BLANCH[Main Steam Chamber
100 Degree C] BLANCH --> SEAL2[Steam Sealing
Section B] SEAL2 --> COOL[Water Spray Cooler
12 Degree C] COOL --> DISCH[Discharge Conveyor] TC[Thermocouple Sensor
Inside Chamber] --> PID[PID Controller] PID --> VLV[Steam Modulating Valve] VLV --> BLANCH LC[Level Sensor
Water Reservoir] --> PUMP[Cooling Water Pump] PUMP --> COOL style BLANCH fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#9c640c,color:#000 style PID fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000 style TC fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The diagram above shows the **three classical control loops** of a continuous blancher: **(i) temperature control** via PID-modulated steam valve, **(ii) residence-time control** via variable-speed conveyor, and **(iii) cooling-water flow control** via level sensor and pump. KTU board questions often ask students to *"draw and explain a continuous blancher"* — this is the model layout. --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Residence Time vs Inactivation ```mermaid flowchart LR T0[Time 0 min
Enzyme 100 percent] --> T1[Time 1 min
Enzyme 37 percent] T1 --> T2[Time 2 min
Enzyme 13.5 percent] T2 --> T3[Time 3 min
Enzyme 5 percent] T3 --> T4[Time 5 min
Enzyme 0.7 percent] T4 --> T5[Time 10 min
Enzyme 0.005 percent] style T0 fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style T1 fill:#e67e22,stroke:#000,color:#fff style T2 fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000,color:#000 style T3 fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#000,color:#fff style T4 fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff style T5 fill:#16a085,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Learning:** This exponential-decay profile illustrates the *first-order* nature of enzyme inactivation. Note how **37 %** remains at $t = D$, **5 %** at $t = 2D$, and **0.5 %** at $t = 3D$. This pattern is identical to microbial inactivation, which is why food engineers commonly say *blanching is to enzymes what sterilization is to microbes*. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU Model Question Style:** Two questions, each carrying 3 marks. Answers should be precise, within 80–100 words. ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Define blanching. List any four objectives of blanching in food processing.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Blanching** is a preliminary heat treatment given to foods (mainly fruits and vegetables) by exposing them to hot water (**75 – 100 °C**) or steam for a short duration (1 – 10 minutes) to inactivate enzymes before further processing. > > **Four Objectives:** > 1. **Enzyme inactivation** (peroxidase, polyphenol oxidase, lipoxygenase). > 2. **Color preservation** — prevents enzymatic browning. > 3. **Texture modification** — PME-driven firming of tissue. > 4. **Intercellular gas expulsion** — improves subsequent vacuum processing. > > *(Any four of the five objectives from Section 2.2 are acceptable.)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why is peroxidase used as the indicator enzyme in blanching of vegetables?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > Peroxidase (POD) is used as the **indicator enzyme** because it has the **highest thermal resistance** among all native vegetable enzymes. Its D-value at 90 °C is in the range of **1 – 3 minutes**, compared to < 30 seconds for catalase and < 15 seconds for polyphenol oxidase. > > Therefore, if a blanching process is severe enough to **inactivate ≥ 90 % of peroxidase**, all other enzymes are guaranteed to have been destroyed. This makes POD a convenient, conservative marker. The **peroxidase test** (using guaiacol or pyrogallol substrates) is the standard factory QC method. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark ESE Module Internal Choice > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer ANY ONE full question (A or B). Each full question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Total = 14 marks. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Discuss in detail the principles of blanching. Explain first-order enzyme inactivation kinetics with the derivation of the equation $E(t) = E_0 e^{-kt}$ and the concept of D-value.** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **Introduction (1 Mark):** Blanching is a thermal pre-treatment of foods (75–100 °C, 1–10 min) primarily aimed at enzyme inactivation. The thermodynamic principle is the irreversible denaturation of enzyme protein structure, which follows first-order kinetics. **First-Order Kinetics (3 Marks):** The rate of enzyme destruction is proportional to the residual active enzyme concentration: $$\frac{dE}{dt} = -k \cdot E(t)$$ Separating variables and integrating between $t = 0$, $E = E_0$ and $t = t$, $E = E(t)$: $$\int_{E_0}^{E(t)} \frac{dE}{E} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{E(t)}{E_0}\right) = -kt \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E(t) = E_0 \, e^{-kt}$$ This is the **exponential-decay** solution. **D-Value Concept (2 Marks):** $$D = \frac{2.303}{k} \;\;\;\text{(time for 10-fold reduction)}$$ Therefore: $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_t}\right) = \frac{t}{D}$$ Typical D-values for peroxidase in vegetables at 90 °C are **1 – 3 min**. **Engineering Significance (1 Mark):** First-order kinetics means the process is *self-stalling* — most damage occurs in the first minute; later minutes give diminishing returns. Industrial blanchers are sized using **D + z + come-up-time** calculations. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain any five objectives of blanching in food processing with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** | # | Objective | Mechanism | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Enzyme inactivation** | Thermal denaturation of PPO, POD, LOX | Green peas before freezing | | 2 | **Color preservation** | Destruction of oxidative enzymes; chlorophyll fixation | Spinach retains bright green | | 3 | **Texture modification** | PME activation → Ca²⁺ pectate bridges | Canned carrots stay firm | | 4 | **Gas expulsion** | Thermal expansion + water displacement | Vacuum-dried apples rehydrate well | | 5 | **Microbial reduction** | Surface sterilization (1–2 log) | Pre-blanched sweet corn | *(Each row = 1.4 marks. Add a final concluding sentence for full 7-mark credit.)* **Conclusion:** All five objectives converge to extend **shelf life and quality retention** — the ultimate engineering purpose of blanching. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Module Choice] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Compare hot water blanching and steam blanching. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each method with industrial examples.** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **Hot Water Blanching (3 Marks):** - **Principle:** Direct contact with water at 75–100 °C. - **Advantages:** Uniform heat transfer (after come-up); low capital cost; simple construction; good for *root vegetables* (carrots, potatoes, peas). - **Disadvantages:** High water usage (5–10 L per kg); leaching losses of **15–25 %** of water-soluble nutrients (Vitamin C, sugars, minerals); effluent treatment cost. - **Example:** Canning-grade peas use 30–60 s immersion at 96 °C followed by chilled dip. **Steam Blanching (3 Marks):** - **Principle:** Saturated steam at 100 °C condensing on product surface releases its high latent heat (**2260 kJ/kg**). - **Advantages:** No leaching (5–10 % loss only); no effluent; rapid surface heating; preserves colour in leafy vegetables. - **Disadvantages:** Uneven heating of dense products; higher capital cost; requires sealing conveyor to contain steam; possible case-hardening of surface. - **Example:** Spinach, broccoli, and cauliflower are predominantly steam-blanched before freezing. **Comparative Summary Table (1 Mark):** | Criterion | Hot Water | Steam | |---|---|---| | Heat transfer coeff. $h$ | 500–2000 W/m²K | 5000–15000 W/m²K | | Nutrient retention | Poor | Excellent | | Water footprint | High | Zero | | Capital cost | Low | Medium | --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A batch of green peas is to be blanched at 95 °C. The D-value of peroxidase in peas at 95 °C is 1.5 min. Calculate (i) the time required for 99 % inactivation, and (ii) the time required for 99.9 % inactivation. Comment on the practical implications.** **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** - $D_{95} = 1.5$ min - Use equation: $\log_{10}(E_0/E_t) = t/D$ **Part (i) — 99 % Inactivation (3 Marks):** $$\frac{E_0}{E_t} = \frac{100}{1} = 100$$ $$\log_{10}(100) = 2 = \frac{t}{1.5}$$ $$\boxed{t_{99\%} = 3.0 \text{ min}}$$ **Part (ii) — 99.9 % Inactivation (3 Marks):** $$\frac{E_0}{E_t} = \frac{1000}{1} = 1000$$ $$\log_{10}(1000) = 3 = \frac{t}{1.5}$$ $$\boxed{t_{99.9\%} = 4.5 \text{ min}}$$ **Practical Implications (1 Mark):** > An extra **1.5 minutes** doubles the destruction (90 % → 99 %) and **triples** it (90 % → 99.9 %). However, doubling the log-reduction adds **50 % more heat exposure**, causing greater **texture softening and nutrient loss**. Industrial QC standards target **≥ 90 % POD destruction** as the *economic optimum*, balancing enzyme kill against quality damage. A typical commercial blancher therefore operates at **2 – 2.5 × D** (3–4 min at 95 °C for peas). --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Avoid These:** > > 1. **Conflating blanching with pasteurization.** Blanching kills *enzymes*; pasteurization kills *microbes*. Students routinely write "blanching kills germs" — this is technically a misstatement and will lose 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting the cooling step.** A full 14-mark answer that omits the post-blanch cold-water chill is considered *incomplete*. Examiners specifically look for the sentence: *"…followed by immediate cooling to prevent over-processing."* > > 3. **Citing the wrong enzyme.** The indicator enzyme in vegetables is **peroxidase (POD)**, not polyphenol oxidase (PPO). PPO is a *fruit* enzyme. Confusing these will cost a mark. > > 4. **Treating blanching time as linear.** If a calculation question involves time, the student must use the **logarithmic** equation $t = D \cdot \log_{10}(E_0/E_t)$. Using linear proportioning will yield a wrong answer and zero marks for the derivation. > > 5. **Skipping safety margin.** Industrial answer should include *"with a 50 % safety margin for come-up-time"*. Forgetting this loses a practical/industrial mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:** - [x] **Definition:** Blanching = brief heat treatment (75–100 °C, 1–10 min) for **enzyme inactivation**. - [x] **NOT** for sterilization; **NOT** for cooking. - [x] **Five Objectives:** Enzyme inactivation / Color preservation / Texture modification / Gas expulsion / Microbial reduction. - [x] **Indicator enzyme:** **Peroxidase (POD)** — most heat-resistant. - [x] **Kinetics:** First-order: $E(t) = E_0 e^{-kt}$. - [x] **D-value:** $D = 2.303/k$, typically **1–3 min @ 90 °C** for POD. - [x] **Working equation:** $t = D \cdot \log_{10}(E_0/E_t)$. - [x] **Z-value of POD:** **14 – 25 °C**. - [x] **Two main industrial methods:** Hot water (cheap, high leaching) vs Steam (expensive, low leaching). - [x] **Cooling step is mandatory** — cold water dip at 10–15 °C. - [x] **Typical targets:** ≥ 90 % POD destruction. - [x] **Trade-off:** Time ↑ → enzyme kill ↑ BUT texture ↓ AND nutrient loss ↑. - [x] **Chlorophyll green fixation** is a bonus colour benefit. - [x] **Intercellular gas expulsion** is critical before vacuum drying/frying. - [x] **Always add 50 % safety margin** for come-up-time in industrial design. - [x] **Modern alternatives:** Microwave, Ohmic, Infrared blanching — uniform heating, near-zero leaching.

topics.title: Blanching Equipment and Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blanching Equipment and Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blanching** is a *short-time thermal pre-treatment* applied to foods (predominantly fruits and vegetables) in which the product is heated to a predetermined temperature, held for a specified duration, and then rapidly cooled, primarily to **inactivate native degradative enzymes** and to achieve ancillary physical, chemical, and microbiological objectives.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEFTT616 - Thermal Processing of Food)**, blanching is positioned as a *critical unit operation* that directly governs the quality, shelf-life, and downstream processing efficiency of canned, frozen, and dehydrated food products.

### Primary Objectives of Blanching
1. **Enzyme Inactivation** – Destroys heat-labile enzymes such as **peroxidase (POD)**, **catalase**, **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)**, **lipoxygenase (LOX)**, and **ascorbate oxidase**.
2. **Gas Expulsion** – Removes intercellular and intracellular gases (O₂, CO₂), preventing panel bulging in cans and oxidation reactions.
3. **Microbial Load Reduction** – Surface pasteurization reduces the initial count of spoilage organisms.
4. **Texture Modification** – Causes plasmolysis of cells, leading to volume contraction, easier filling, and improved peelability.
5. **Color Fixation** – Expels gases that cause oxidative browning and fixes natural pigments.
6. **Volatile Removal** – Eliminates off-flavor constituents (e.g., bitter glycosides in *Brassica* vegetables).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** The benchmark enzyme for verifying blanching adequacy is **Peroxidase (POD)**, which has one of the highest heat resistances among plant enzymes. Complete POD inactivation is taken as empirical proof of *over-blanching safety* (the assumption being: if POD is killed, all other enzymes are also destroyed).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Think of blanching as a **"Controlled Shock-Wakeup Call"** for a fruit or vegetable.

Imagine a fresh apple slice left on a kitchen counter — within minutes, the exposed flesh browns, signaling that dormant enzymes (like PPO) have "woken up" and started reacting with oxygen. Now imagine dunking that same slice in **boiling water for 90 seconds** before cooling it. What happened?

- The heat **knocked out the enzymes** before they could "wake up" or do damage later.
- The trapped gases inside the cells **rushed out** (like air bubbles escaping when you open a soda bottle).
- The cell walls softened slightly, allowing easier water loss in subsequent drying.

That is blanching — a **carefully timed, deliberately destructive thermal kiss** to disable enzymes without cooking the food itself.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not just skip blanching?** Without it, frozen vegetables develop hay-like off-flavors during storage, canned peas turn slate-grey, and dried carrots lose their vibrant orange. Blanching is the *insurance policy* of the food preservation industry.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Blanching Methods (KTU 2024 Overview)

| S.No. | Method | Heat Transfer Medium | Industrial Adoption |
|:-----:|:-------|:---------------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Hot Water Blanching | Hot water (80–100 °C) | High (most common) |
| 2 | Steam Blanching | Saturated steam (100 °C) | Very High |
| 3 | Microwave Blanching | Dielectric heating (2450 MHz) | Growing |
| 4 | Hot Gas (Hot Air) Blanching | Hot air / combustion gases | Specialized |
| 5 | Infrared / Radiant Blanching | IR radiation | Research / niche |
| 6 | Ohmic (Electric) Blanching | Resistance heating | Emerging |
| 7 | High-Pressure Blanching | Compressed hot water | Novel |

---

## 1.4 Critical Process Parameters (KTU Mandatory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Blanching Triad (Three variables you must master for the exam):**
> 1. **Temperature (T)** – typically **80 °C to 100 °C**
> 2. **Time (t)** – typically **30 s to 10 min** depending on product size
> 3. **Cooling Rate** – must be **rapid** to prevent residual cooking and reverse enzyme activation

The **Lethal Effect (LE)** of blanching is quantified in *Pasteurization Units (PU)*, where each minute at **60 °C** = 1 PU (for peroxidase, the reference temperature for *z* = 30 °C is sometimes used).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Blanching

Blanching is fundamentally a **simultaneous heat-and-mass transfer problem** governed by unsteady-state conduction with moving boundary conditions. The thermal death of enzymes follows **first-order reaction kinetics**, mathematically identical to microbial destruction.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 – Heat Penetration Phase**
- Heat from the medium (water/steam) penetrates the food surface via **convective heat transfer**.
- Surface temperature quickly approaches the medium temperature.
- Governed by the **Biot Number**: $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h \cdot L}{k}$ where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient.

**Step 2 – Internal Conduction (Lumped vs. Distributed)**
- For small particles, *lumped capacitance* applies: the entire particle assumes a uniform temperature.
- For larger pieces (e.g., whole carrots), heat conduction inside the solid follows **Fourier's law in spherical/cylindrical coordinates**.

**Step 3 – Enzyme Inactivation Kinetics**
- The residual enzyme activity decays exponentially with time at a constant temperature:

$$\dfrac{A_t}{A_0} = 10^{-t/D_T}$$

where:
- $A_t$ = enzyme activity after time $t$
- $A_0$ = initial enzyme activity
- $D_T$ = **decimal reduction time** at temperature $T$ (time to destroy 90% of enzyme)

**Step 4 – Time-Temperature Dependence (z-value concept)**
- The temperature sensitivity of $D_T$ is described by:

$$\log\!\left(\dfrac{D_{T_1}}{D_{T_2}}\right) = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$$

where $z$ is the temperature (°C) required to change $D$ by a factor of 10. For **peroxidase**, $z \approx 30$ °C.

**Step 5 – Cooling Phase (Equally Critical)**
- Rapid cooling in **chilled water (≤ 10 °C)** halts residual thermal damage and prevents *tissue softening*.
- Slow cooling causes "carry-over cooking" — a common KTU exam pitfall.

**Step 6 – Quality Verification (Peroxidase Test)**
- Residual POD is measured via **guaiacol test** (colorimetric) or **benzidine test**.
- For *commercial blanching*, **≥ 90% POD inactivation** is the KTU benchmark.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | *Why* it matters | *How* it is implemented industrially |
|:-----|:-----------------|:-------------------------------------|
| Heat penetration | Determines uniformity of enzyme kill | Selection of $h$ (water vs. steam) |
| Internal conduction | Governs residence time | Particle size reduction |
| Enzyme inactivation | Core objective of blanching | Temperature–time integration (PU calculation) |
| Cooling | Prevents over-cooking & leaching | Counter-current cold water flumes |
| POD verification | Quality assurance | On-line colorimetric sensors |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Expression | Units | Practical Use |
|:-:|:------------------|:-------------------|:------|:--------------|
| 1 | First-order enzyme inactivation | $\dfrac{A}{A_0} = 10^{-t/D}$ | dimensionless | Predict residual enzyme after blanching |
| 2 | Decimal reduction time | $D_T$ — time for 90 % inactivation | minutes | Kinetics constant for POD |
| 3 | z-value (thermal sensitivity) | $z = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\log(D_{T_1}/D_{T_2})}$ | °C | Heat resistance of enzymes |
| 4 | Pasteurization Unit (PU) | $PU = 10^{(T - 60)/z} \cdot \Delta t$ | min equivalent | Total lethality delivered |
| 5 | Lethal Rate | $L = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$ | dimensionless | Instantaneous lethality |
| 6 | Surface heat transfer (water) | $h \approx 1000\text{–}3000$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | High — vigorous boiling |
| 7 | Surface heat transfer (steam) | $h \approx 5000\text{–}15000$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | Very high — film condensation |
| 8 | Surface heat transfer (hot air) | $h \approx 50\text{–}500$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | Low — only for thin products |
| 9 | Convective heat load | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Sizing steam/water heaters |
| 10 | Leaching loss (water blanch) | up to **15–30 %** of soluble solids | % mass | Drives preference for steam |
| 11 | Typical steam blanch temp | **100 °C** (saturated) | °C | Most common industrial |
| 12 | Typical hot water temp | **80 – 100 °C** | °C | Standard practice |
| 13 | Cooling water temperature | **≤ 10 °C** | °C | Stops residual cooking |
| 14 | Recommended POD inactivation | **≥ 90 %** | % | KTU commercial standard |
| 15 | Biotin/catalase test (over-blanch) | Negative catalase = over-blanched | — | Indicates excessive heat |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations allow the food engineer to compute *required residence time* in a continuous blancher, given the line throughput and the target $F$-value (analogous to the sterilization value $F_0$ used in canning, but at lower lethality).

---

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

In a **continuous industrial blancher** (e.g., a screw-type pea blancher), the *Lethal Value* integrated over the residence time must exceed the **process lethal value (L_p)** required for the target enzyme. This is computed as:

$$L_p = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/z} \, dt$$

This integral form is what makes blanching mathematically equivalent to a *mini-sterilization* — the foundational bridge between **Module 2 (Blanching)** and **Module 3/4 (Thermal Sterilization)** of the OEFTT616 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Time Required for POD Inactivation

### Problem Setup
A batch of **green peas** at **25 °C** is to be steam-blanched in saturated steam at **100 °C**. The decimal reduction time of peroxidase in peas at 100 °C is **$D_{100} = 0.6$ minutes**, and the reference $z$-value is **30 °C**. Compute the residence time required to achieve **99.9 % inactivation** (i.e., a 3-log reduction).

---

### Step 1 — State the First-Order Inactivation Equation

The fractional residual activity at any time $t$ is:

$$\dfrac{A_t}{A_0} = 10^{-t/D_T}$$

**Logic:** This is the standard first-order kinetic model. It assumes enzyme destruction proceeds at a rate proportional to the *instantaneous* active enzyme concentration. A reduction of 90 % corresponds to $A/A_0 = 0.1$, which by definition is achieved at $t = D_T$. A reduction of 99 % is at $t = 2D_T$, and 99.9 % at $t = 3D_T$.

---

### Step 2 — Substitute the Required Log-Reduction

For **99.9 % inactivation**, the surviving activity is $A_t/A_0 = 0.001 = 10^{-3}$.

$$10^{-3} = 10^{-t/D_{100}}$$

**Logic:** Equating exponents on both sides of the base-10 expression. This is the fundamental manipulation that makes the model so useful for KTU problems.

---

### Step 3 — Solve for $t$

Equating exponents:

$$t = 3 \times D_{100}$$

$$t = 3 \times 0.6\ \text{min}$$

$$\boxed{t = 1.8\ \text{minutes}}$$

**Logic:** A general rule emerges — for an $n$-log reduction, the residence time is $t = n \cdot D_T$. This is the "**Rule of Nines**" of thermal food processing, equally applicable to blanching and sterilization.

---

### Step 4 — Verification Using the Lethal Value Concept

Compute the **Lethal Value** delivered at the center of the pea (assume surface reaches 100 °C almost instantly due to high $h$ for steam):

$$L = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z} = 10^{(100 - 90)/30} = 10^{0.333}$$

$$L \approx 2.154$$

For a residence of $t = 1.8$ min, the cumulative **Pasteurization Units (PU)** delivered is:

$$PU = L \times t = 2.154 \times 1.8 \approx 3.88\ \text{PU}$$

This corresponds to roughly **3.88 minutes equivalent at 90 °C**, sufficient to inactivate the heat-labile enzyme fraction.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Time Adjustment at a Different Temperature

### Problem Setup
Suppose the same peas are now blanched in **hot water at 90 °C** instead of steam. Compute the new residence time for the same 99.9 % inactivation. The $D$-value at 90 °C is unknown, but we know $D_{100} = 0.6$ min and $z = 30$ °C.

---

### Step 1 — Apply the z-value Relationship

$$\log\!\left(\dfrac{D_{100}}{D_{90}}\right) = \dfrac{100 - 90}{z}$$

$$\log\!\left(\dfrac{0.6}{D_{90}}\right) = \dfrac{10}{30} = 0.333$$

---

### Step 2 — Solve for $D_{90}$

$$\dfrac{0.6}{D_{90}} = 10^{0.333} = 2.154$$

$$D_{90} = \dfrac{0.6}{2.154}$$

$$D_{90} \approx 0.2785\ \text{min}$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the New Residence Time

$$t_{90} = 3 \times D_{90} = 3 \times 0.2785$$

$$\boxed{t_{90} \approx 0.836\ \text{min} \approx 50\ \text{seconds}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although the temperature is only 10 °C lower, the residence time drops to *less than half* — but this is misleading. The numerical derivation used here assumes *instantaneous* temperature equilibration. In real hot-water blanching of peas (where lumped heating is a fair assumption), the *actual* time must account for the **come-up time** of the particle, often 30–60 seconds.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Blanching Methods

| Criterion | Hot Water | Steam | Microwave | Hot Air | Ohmic |
|:----------|:----------|:------|:----------|:--------|:------|
| Heat transfer coefficient $h$ ($\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$) | 1000–3000 | 5000–15000 | Internal volumetric | 50–500 | Volumetric |
| Temperature uniformity | Good | Good (surface) | Excellent | Poor | Excellent |
| Nutrient leaching (water-soluble) | **High (15–30 %)** | **Negligible** | Negligible | Negligible | Negligible |
| Energy efficiency | Moderate | **High** | High | Moderate | High |
| Capital cost | Low | Moderate | High | Low | High |
| Operator skill required | Low | Low | High | Low | High |
| Product texture impact | Significant softening | Moderate | Minimal | Drying effect | Minimal |
| Suitability for leafy vegetables | Excellent | Good | Good | Poor | Excellent |
| Suitability for particulate foods | Excellent | Good | Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| Industrial maturity (KTU 2024 view) | Mature | Mature | Growing | Niche | Emerging |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Total Lethality Calculation

**Problem:** A pea blancher operates at a constant product temperature of 95 °C for 3 minutes. Reference $z = 30$ °C, $T_{ref} = 90$ °C. Compute the cumulative **PU** delivered.

### Step 1 — Compute the Instantaneous Lethal Rate

$$L = 10^{(95 - 90)/30} = 10^{5/30} = 10^{0.1667}$$

$$L \approx 1.468$$

### Step 2 — Multiply by Time

$$PU = L \times t = 1.468 \times 3$$

$$\boxed{PU \approx 4.40\ \text{Pasteurization Units}}$$

**Interpretation:** A PU of 4.4 means the peas received the equivalent of **4.4 minutes at 90 °C** — sufficient for substantial peroxidase inactivation in peas, and the typical KTU benchmark for "well-blanched" green vegetables.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of an Industrial Continuous Steam Blancher

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Product Inlet Hopper] --> B[Vibratory Feed Conveyor]
    B --> C[Pre-Wash / Dewatering Zone]
    C --> D[Saturated Steam Injection Chamber 100C]
    D --> E[Residence Tunnel with Mesh Belt]
    E --> F[Hold Zone 1 to 3 minutes]
    F --> G[Exhaust Steam and Condensate Outlet]
    G --> H[Spray Cooling Zone Chilled Water 10C]
    H --> I[Counter-Current Cooling Flume]
    I --> J[Dewatering Vibratory Screen]
    J --> K[Peroxidase Test Station QC]
    K --> L[Blanched Product Discharge to Next Unit Operation]
    M[Steam Boiler] -.supplies saturated steam.-> D
    M -.condensate return.-> G
    N[Chilled Water Circulator] -.supplies cooling water.-> H
    N -.recirculated cooled water.-> I
    O[Control Panel PLC] -.monitors T and t.-> D
    O -.monitors T out.-> H
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *solid arrows* show the **product path** (raw → blanched). The *dotted arrows* show the **utility flows** (steam supply, chilled water circulation, PLC feedback). This is the KTU 2024 standard topology for a continuous belt-type steam blancher used for peas, carrots, and cut beans.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hot Water Blanching Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Product] --> P2[Bucket Elevator]
    P2 --> P3[Hot Water Tank 80 to 100C]
    P3 --> P4[Submerged Conveyor Mesh Belt]
    P4 --> P5[Hold Section Residence Time Control]
    P5 --> P6[Outlet Elevator]
    P6 --> P7[Spray Rinse Section]
    P7 --> P8[Cooling Tank Chilled Water]
    P8 --> P9[Final Dewatering Screen]
    P9 --> P10[QC Test POD]
    P10 --> P11[Blanched Product Exit]
    H1[Steam Coils in Tank] -.heats.-> P3
    H2[Water Recirculation Pump] -.continuous flow.-> P4
    H3[Chiller Unit] -.cools.-> P8
    C1[Temperature Sensor TS1] -.feedback.-> H1
    C2[Level Sensor LS1] -.feedback.-> H2
    C3[PLC Controller] -.integrates C1 and C2.-> H1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Hot water blanchers must be designed to *minimize leaching losses*. The recirculation pump (H2) ensures uniform water temperature and prevents localized cooling. Residence time is controlled by **belt speed**, not by product depth.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Method Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Blanching Method] --> Q1{Is the product leafy or delicate?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Loss of water solubles acceptable?}
    Q2 -- No --> S1[Steam Blanching]
    Q2 -- Yes --> S2[Hot Water Blanching]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Energy efficiency critical?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> S1
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{High capital investment allowed?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> S3[Microwave Blanching]
    Q4 -- No --> S2
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Large throughput required?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> S1
    Q5 -- No --> S2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always justify the method choice using **three factors**: (i) product sensitivity, (ii) leaching tolerance, and (iii) throughput. The examiner expects a *justified* decision, not a list.

---

## 4.4 Equipment Schematics: Detailed Equipment Functional Map

| Equipment Type | Functional Block | Key KTU Highlight |
|:---------------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| Batch Water Blancher | Tank + heating coil + manual basket | Low cost, small scale |
| Continuous Water Blancher (Screw) | Inclined trough + rotating screw | Gentle handling, leafy vegetables |
| Continuous Water Blancher (Belt) | Perforated belt + hot water reservoir | High throughput, peas/beans |
| Steam Cabinet Blancher | Static chamber + steam inlet | Batch, small processors |
| Continuous Steam Blancher (Belt) | Mesh belt + steam injection hood | **Most common industrial design** |
| Rotary Drum Blancher | Rotating drum + steam jacket | Pea blanching, uniform exposure |
| Pipe Blancher | Closed pipe with steam injection | High pressure, high temperature |
| Microwave Blancher | Tunnel with magnetrons (2450 MHz) | Low leaching, high uniformity |
| Ohmic Blancher | Electrode chamber with AC current | Volumetric heating, emerging |
| IR Blancher | Radiant heaters above belt | Surface heating, niche |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define blanching. List any four objectives of blanching in food processing.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Blanching** is a short-duration thermal pre-treatment of foods, typically fruits and vegetables, in which the product is heated to a defined temperature (usually 80–100 °C) for a controlled time, followed by rapid cooling.

**Four objectives:**
1. **Inactivation of native enzymes** such as peroxidase, catalase, and polyphenol oxidase.
2. **Removal of intercellular gases** to prevent oxidation and can panel bulging.
3. **Reduction of surface microbial load** prior to canning/freezing.
4. **Fixation of color and texture**, improving downstream processing efficiency.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Why is peroxidase (POD) used as the index enzyme for blanching adequacy?**

**Model Answer:**

Peroxidase is selected as the **marker enzyme** for verifying blanching adequacy because:

- It possesses the **highest heat resistance** among common plant enzymes; its destruction implies destruction of *all other, more heat-labile* enzymes (catalase, PPO, LOX).
- It is **easily detectable** by simple colorimetric tests (guaiacol, benzidine).
- Complete or ≥ 90 % POD inactivation provides a **safety margin** against residual enzymatic activity.

> [!TIP]
> A negative catalase test, on the other hand, indicates *over-blanching* — a quality defect to be avoided.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper - Module 2]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **continuous steam blancher** used in vegetable processing. Add a neat sketch. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Compare **hot water blanching** and **steam blanching** with respect to nutrient retention, energy efficiency, and suitability for different products. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working of a Continuous Steam Blancher \[7 Marks\]

**Construction (4 marks):**
1. **Feed Hopper & Vibratory Conveyor** – Receives raw product and distributes it uniformly onto the belt.
2. **Perforated Stainless Steel Mesh Belt** – Transports the product through the chamber at a controlled speed.
3. **Steam Injection Hood/Chamber** – Located above the belt; introduces **saturated steam at 100 °C** through distribution pipes.
4. **Residence Tunnel** – Provides the required hold time (typically 1–4 minutes) by adjusting belt speed and tunnel length.
5. **Condensate and Exhaust Steam Outlet** – Drains condensate and vents excess steam.
6. **Cooling Section** – Sprays chilled water (≤ 10 °C) to rapidly cool the product.
7. **PLC Control Panel** – Monitors temperature, residence time, and belt speed.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Product enters the hopper, is dewatered, and spread onto the mesh belt.
- As the belt moves through the steam chamber, **saturated steam condenses on the cold product surface**, releasing its **latent heat of vaporization (≈ 2257 kJ/kg)**, providing extremely rapid surface heating.
- Surface temperature quickly reaches 100 °C; heat conducts inward to inactivate enzymes at the geometric center.
- After the residence time, the product passes through the cooling spray, dropping to ≤ 30 °C within seconds.
- The product is finally dewatered and sent to the next unit operation (e.g., canning or freezing).

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison: Hot Water vs. Steam Blanching \[7 Marks\]

| Parameter | Hot Water Blanching | Steam Blanching |
|:----------|:-------------------|:----------------|
| Heat transfer medium | Water at 80–100 °C | Saturated steam at 100 °C |
| Heat transfer coefficient $h$ | 1000–3000 W/m²·K | 5000–15000 W/m²·K |
| Nutrient leaching (water-soluble) | **High (15–30 %)** — vitamins, sugars, salts lost in water | **Negligible** — water condenses on the product, no leaching |
| Energy efficiency | Lower — water must be heated continuously | Higher — latent heat release on condensation is efficient |
| Water consumption | High — must be replenished | Low — only steam generation |
| Texture impact | More softening | Less softening |
| Suitable products | Leafy vegetables, diced vegetables, fruits | Peas, cut beans, carrots, corn, potato strips |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Risk of microbial cross-contamination | Higher (especially in recirculated water) | Lower |
| Effluent generation | Significant (BOD-rich blanch water) | Minimal |
| Uniformity of heating | Good (with vigorous boiling) | Excellent (latent heat release) |
| Cooling requirement | More intense (water-soaked product) | Less intense (less water uptake) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Concluding Statement (for full marks):** Steam blanching is preferred for *water-sensitive* products (peas, cut beans) and in industries with effluent discharge regulations. Hot water blanching remains dominant for *leafy and diced vegetables* where uniform heating and short residence times are paramount.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a microwave blancher**. Discuss its advantages over conventional methods. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A vegetable (carrot dice) is to be blanched to achieve **99 % inactivation** of peroxidase. The $D$-value at 100 °C is **1.2 min** and $z = 30$ °C. If blanching is carried out at **90 °C** in hot water, calculate the **required residence time**. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Microwave Blancher \[7 Marks\]

**Principle (2 marks):**
Microwave blanching uses **dielectric heating** at the industrial frequency of **2450 MHz**. Polar molecules (primarily water) and ionic species within the food oscillate in synchrony with the alternating electric field, generating heat **volumetrically throughout the product mass** — not just at the surface.

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Magnetron Tube** – Generates microwaves at 2450 MHz and 915 MHz (industrial band).
2. **Waveguide** – Directs microwaves into the heating tunnel.
3. **Applicator Tunnel** – Houses the product on a PTFE (Teflon) conveyor belt, transparent to microwaves.
4. **Mode Stirrers / Rotating Antennas** – Ensure uniform field distribution and prevent hot/cold spots.
5. **Power Control Unit** – Adjusts input power (typically 5–50 kW per magnetron).
6. **Cooling Section** – Integrated downstream, often using chilled air or water spray.
7. **Safety Interlocks and RF Shielding** – Prevent microwave leakage.

**Working (1 mark):**
- Wet food is exposed to microwave energy; water dipole rotation and ionic conduction generate heat rapidly and uniformly. Internal temperatures can rise from 25 °C to 90 °C in **seconds**.
- Enzymes are inactivated by the rapid, uniform internal heating.
- Product exits to the cooling section and QC station.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- **Minimal leaching losses** (no water contact).
- **Energy efficient** — heating is internal, no surrounding medium to heat.
- **Short processing time** — high throughput in compact equipment.
- **Improved texture and color retention.**
- **Uniform enzyme inactivation** throughout the product.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution \[7 Marks\]

**Given:**
- Required inactivation = 99 % → log reduction = 2
- $D_{100} = 1.2$ min at 100 °C
- $z = 30$ °C
- Blanching temperature $T = 90$ °C

**Step 1 — Compute the new $D_{90}$ using the z-value equation:**

$$\log\!\left(\dfrac{D_{100}}{D_{90}}\right) = \dfrac{T_{100} - T_{90}}{z} = \dfrac{100 - 90}{30} = 0.3333$$

$$\dfrac{D_{100}}{D_{90}} = 10^{0.3333} = 2.1544$$

$$D_{90} = \dfrac{D_{100}}{2.1544} = \dfrac{1.2}{2.1544} = 0.5570\ \text{min}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order inactivation equation for 99 % kill:**

$$\dfrac{A_t}{A_0} = 0.01 = 10^{-t/D_{90}}$$

$$t = 2 \times D_{90} = 2 \times 0.5570$$

$$\boxed{t = 1.114\ \text{minutes} \approx 67\ \text{seconds}}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check with the Lethal Value concept:**

$$L = 10^{(90 - 90)/30} = 10^{0} = 1.0$$

$$PU = L \times t = 1.0 \times 1.114 = 1.114\ \text{PU at 90 °C}$$

This represents a modest PU value, sufficient for *typical* peroxidase inactivation in carrots, but operators would normally apply a safety factor of 1.5–2× to account for **come-up time** of the cube center, leading to a practical residence time of **~ 90–100 seconds**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing peroxidase with catalase:** Catalase has a *lower* heat resistance. A *negative catalase test* means *over-blanching* (a defect), NOT successful blanching. Peroxidase is the correct index.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the cooling step:** Many answers describe only the heating phase. **Cooling is a non-negotiable part of blanching** — it prevents residual cooking and fixes texture. Examiners explicitly look for this.
>
> 3. **Numerical errors in z-value formula:** A common mistake is using $T_1 - T_2$ in the numerator but then failing to invert the ratio correctly. Always double-check:
> $$\log(D_{T_1}/D_{T_2}) = (T_2 - T_1)/z$$
> (Note the **subtle sign inversion** in the numerator!)
>
> 4. **Ignoring leaching losses:** When comparing hot water vs. steam, students often write generic statements. The examiner expects *quantitative* numbers (e.g., **15–30 % soluble solid loss in water blanching**).
>
> 5. **Diagrams without labels:** A sketch of a blancher without labeled parts (steam inlet, belt, exhaust, cooling section) gets only partial credit. Always label **at least 6 components**.
>
> 6. **Wrong reference temperature in PU:** Always state the **reference temperature (T_ref)** and **z-value** when defining PU. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous PU definitions.
>
> 7. **Failing to mention POD test method:** Name the test (guaiacol/benzidine) and the target (≥ 90 % inactivation).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ☐ **Blanching** = short thermal pre-treatment, primarily to **inactivate enzymes**.
- ☐ Three process parameters: **Temperature, Time, Cooling Rate**.
- ☐ Typical conditions: **80–100 °C** for **30 s – 10 min**, then cool to **≤ 10 °C**.
- ☐ **Peroxidase (POD)** is the **index enzyme** — highest heat resistance.
- ☐ **Catalase** is *more heat-labile* — its absence indicates *over-blanching* (bad).
- ☐ **First-order kinetics:** $A/A_0 = 10^{-t/D}$.
- ☐ **z-value:** $z = (T_2 - T_1) / \log(D_{T_1}/D_{T_2})$; for POD, **z ≈ 30 °C**.
- ☐ **PU (Pasteurization Unit):** one minute at reference temperature (usually 60 °C or 90 °C).
- ☐ **Hot water blanching:** High $h$ but causes **15–30 % leaching loss**.
- ☐ **Steam blanching:** Very high $h$ (latent heat condensation), **minimal leaching**, **preferred industrially**.
- ☐ **Microwave blanching:** Volumetric heating, low leaching, high uniformity, high capital cost.
- ☐ **Ohmic blanching:** Internal resistance heating, emerging, suited for particulate foods.
- ☐ **Continuous belt steam blancher** = most common industrial design for peas, beans, carrots.
- ☐ **Leaching losses** are quantified in **% of soluble solids** (sugars, vitamins, salts, pigments).
- ☐ **Quality control tests:** Guaiacol POD test (positive = under-blanched, negative = acceptable).
- ☐ **Cooling section** is *mandatory* — never omit in your answer.
- ☐ **Come-up time (CUT)** must be added to the calculated residence time for non-instantaneous heating.
- ☐ For an **n-log reduction** in enzyme activity, $t = n \cdot D_T$ (the "Rule of N").
- ☐ **Biot number** governs whether *lumped* or *distributed* heating analysis applies.
- ☐ **Enzymes of concern:** POD, PPO, LOX, catalase, ascorbate oxidase.
- ☐ Always quote **three reasons** when choosing a blanching method: product type, leaching tolerance, throughput.
- ☐ Industrial benchmark for blanching adequacy: **≥ 90 % POD inactivation**.
- ☐ A continuous blancher's residence time is controlled by **belt speed** and **tunnel length**, not by product depth.
- ☐ Hot water blanchers must include **water recirculation** to prevent localized cooling and cross-contamination.
- ☐ For KTU numerical problems, always state: $D_T$, $z$, $T_{ref}$, and the required **log reduction**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2 — BLANCHING EQUIPMENT AND METHODS**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEFTT616) syllabus and Board Examiner patterns.*
