topics.title: Effects of Blanching on Food Quality and Enzyme Inactivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effects of Blanching on Food Quality and Enzyme Inactivation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Blanching** is a thermal pretreatment unit operation in food processing defined as a short-duration heat treatment (typically **70–100 °C** for **1–10 minutes**, depending on product size and type) applied to fruits, vegetables, and certain animal tissues prior to further processing operations such as freezing, drying, canning, or dehydration.

The primary objectives of blanching, in descending order of importance, are:

1. **Inactivation of deteriorative native enzymes** (peroxidase, catalase, polyphenol oxidase, lipase, chlorophyllase, etc.).
2. **Removal of intercellular gases** and consequent improvement in heat-transfer efficiency during downstream processing.
3. **Reduction of surface microbial load** by approximately **90–99 % (1–2 log cycles)**.
4. **Improvement of texture, color, and sensory attributes** by partial tissue softening and removal of raw/grass-like flavor notes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "blanching" specifically refers to a sub-cooking heat treatment. The process is **not** intended to fully cook the food — it is a *preparatory* thermal step that strikes a balance between *sufficient enzyme inactivation* and *minimal quality degradation*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pre-Game Warm-Up"

Imagine a football team preparing for an intense match in heavy rain (the match = subsequent preservation step such as freezing or canning). The coach gives the players a brief warm-up and a quick change into rain-ready gear (the warm-up = blanching). 

- If the warm-up is **too short**, the players get soaked and tired mid-match (enzymes remain active → browning, off-flavors).
- If the warm-up is **too long**, the players are exhausted before the match starts (over-blanching → nutrient loss, mushy texture).
- The **ideal warm-up duration** is precisely the "Goldilocks zone" — long enough to prepare, short enough to preserve energy.

Similarly, blanching is the **calibrated pre-treatment** that conditions the food for the "match" of long-term preservation.

## 1.3 Classification of Blanching Methods

| Method | Typical Temperature | Typical Time | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Water (Boiling) Blanching** | 95–100 °C | 1–8 min | Root vegetables, leafy greens |
| **Steam Blanching** | 100 °C (saturated steam) | 2–8 min | Cut fruits, peas, leafy vegetables |
| **Microwave Blanching** | Internal: 80–95 °C | 0.5–3 min | Premium-quality retention products |
| **Hot Gas / Infrared Blanching** | 110–150 °C (surface) | 30–120 s | Surface-only enzyme inactivation |
| **Chemical Blanching** | Ambient (with chemicals) | Variable | Specific industrial applications |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Convention:** Blanching efficacy is universally reported using **peroxidase activity** because peroxidase is the **most heat-stable enzyme** in most plant tissues. Complete inactivation of peroxidase is conventionally taken as the *marker* for adequate blanching.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Effects on Food Quality

### A. Effects on Enzymes (Primary Function)

- **Inactivation of deteriorative enzymes** follows **first-order reaction kinetics**.
- The order of heat sensitivity in typical plant tissue: **Lipoxygenase > Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO) > Catalase > Peroxidase (POD)**.
- **Peroxidase** is the most heat-resistant → used as the **over-blanching indicator**.
- An alternative marker is **catalase**; complete POD inactivation typically corresponds to ≈ 90–95 % inactivation of catalase.

### B. Effects on Texture

- **Pectin solubilization** via β-elimination (especially in the pH range 5.0–6.5).
- Intercellular **air removal** causes tissue softening and collapse → facilitates packing.
- Excessive blanching causes **mushiness** due to over-softening of middle lamella pectins.

### C. Effects on Color

- **Inactivation of chlorophyllase** slows chlorophyll → pheophytin conversion → preserves green color (steam blanching preferred).
- **Inactivation of polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** prevents enzymatic browning in apples, potatoes, mushrooms.
- **Leaching of water-soluble pigments** (anthocyanins, betalains) is a concern in hot-water blanching.

### D. Effects on Nutritional Quality

- **Vitamin C (ascorbic acid)** is the most labile nutrient — losses of **10–60 %** are typical.
- **B-complex vitamins** (thiamine, riboflavin) suffer leaching losses of **5–30 %**.
- **Minerals** leach into the blanching water.
- **Protein denaturation** is minor and often beneficial (improves digestibility).
- **Carotenoid bioavailability** may *increase* due to cell-wall disruption.

### E. Effects on Microbial Load

- Surface microflora reduced by **1–2 log cycles**.
- *Does not sterilize* — spoilage and pathogenic spores remain viable.

### F. Effects on Mass, Volume & Heat Transfer

- **Wilting** of leafy vegetables reduces volume by **20–40 %** → better packing density.
- Removal of intercellular gases eliminates "floating" in cans during thermal processing.
- Pre-heated tissue allows **faster heat penetration** in subsequent blanching/canning operations.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTE:** In all log-based equations below, **log** without a subscript denotes **log₁₀** (decadic logarithm). **ln** denotes natural logarithm.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | First-order enzyme inactivation (natural form) | A in units/g; t in s or min |
| 2 | $\ln(A) = \ln(A_0) - kt$ | Linearized natural-log form | Dimensionless + s/min |
| 3 | $\log(A) = \log(A_0) - \dfrac{t}{D}$ | Linearized log₁₀ form | Dimensionless + s/min |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{\ln(10)}{k} = \dfrac{2.303}{k}$ | Relationship between D-value and rate constant | Time (s or min) |
| 5 | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$ | z-value (thermal death time) relationship | T in °C or K; z in °C |
| 6 | $\log(D) = \log(D_{ref}) - \dfrac{T - T_{ref}}{z}$ | D-value at any temperature | Time; T in °C or K |
| 7 | $k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate equation | k in s⁻¹; Eₐ in J/mol |
| 8 | $\ln(k) = \ln(k_0) - \dfrac{E_a}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearized Arrhenius form | Dimensionless + K⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\text{Residual Activity (\%)} = \dfrac{A}{A_0}\times 100$ | Enzyme activity after treatment | Percentage |
| 10 | $\text{Inactivation (\%)} = 100\!\left(1 - \dfrac{A}{A_0}\right)$ | Percent enzyme destroyed | Percentage |

**Boundary Conditions & Engineering Assumptions:**

- Enzyme inactivation is assumed to follow **first-order kinetics** throughout blanching.
- **Heat transfer is rate-limiting** at the surface and follows Fourier conduction.
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$ is the universal gas constant.
- $T$ must be in **Kelvin (K)** when used inside the Arrhenius exponent.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This quantitative framework is foundational in designing:

- **Continuous belt blanchers** (e.g., Gregory tunnel blanchers) for peas and corn.
- **Rotary drum blanchers** for diced vegetables.
- **Steam-jacketed screw conveyors** for industrial-scale operations.
- **Process validation in HACCP** programs (CCP-2B type operation in food safety plans).
- **Microwave and ohmic blanching** kinetic studies for premium-quality frozen vegetables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of First-Order Enzyme Inactivation Kinetics

### Step 1 — Start with the Rate Law

The rate at which enzyme activity decreases is proportional to the *current* residual activity:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = -k \cdot A$$

**Why negative?** Activity ($A$) decreases with time ($t$), so the derivative is negative.

### Step 2 — Separate the Variables

Bring all $A$-terms to the left and all $t$-terms to the right:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -k \cdot dt$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Both Sides

Integrating from initial condition $(A_0, \, t=0)$ to final state $(A, \, t)$:

$$\int_{A_0}^{A} \frac{dA}{A} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Integrals

$$\ln(A) - \ln(A_0) = -k \cdot t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{A}{A_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

### Step 5 — Exponentiate Both Sides

$$\frac{A}{A_0} = e^{-kt}$$

$$\boxed{A = A_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}}$$

This is the **fundamental first-order inactivation equation** universally used in food blanching kinetics.

### Step 6 — Convert to Logarithm Base 10 (D-value form)

Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A}{A_0}\right) = -k \cdot t \cdot \log_{10}(e) = -\frac{k \cdot t}{2.303}$$

By definition, the **D-value** is the time required for a **10-fold (90 %) reduction** in activity, i.e., when $A/A_0 = 0.1$:

$$\log_{10}(0.1) = -1 = -\frac{k \cdot D}{2.303}$$

Solving for D:

$$\boxed{D = \frac{2.303}{k}}$$

Substituting back yields:

$$\boxed{\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A}{A_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the z-value Relationship

### Step 1 — Definition of z-value

The **z-value** is the temperature increase (in °C) required to reduce the D-value by a factor of 10.

### Step 2 — Express Mathematically

When temperature changes from $T_1$ to $T_2$, the D-value changes from $D_1$ to $D_2$:

$$\frac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{\,(T_2 - T_1)/z}$$

### Step 3 — Take Logarithm

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1 / D_2)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A peroxidase enzyme in green peas has a D-value of **24 s at 85 °C** and a z-value of **28 °C**.
> **(a)** Calculate the time required for **90 % inactivation** at 85 °C.
> **(b)** Calculate the D-value at **95 °C**.
> **(c)** Estimate the activation energy $E_a$ assuming $k_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{40} \; \text{s}^{-1}$ (typical for peroxidase).

### Part (a) — Time for 90 % Inactivation

**Given:** $A/A_0 = 0.10$ (since 90 % inactivated means 10 % remains); $D_{85} = 24$ s.

Apply Equation 3:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A}{A_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D}$$

$$\log_{10}(0.10) = -\frac{t}{24}$$

$$-1 = -\frac{t}{24}$$

$$\boxed{t = 24 \; \text{seconds}}$$

**Interpretation:** 24 seconds at 85 °C achieves 90 % peroxidase inactivation.

---

### Part (b) — D-value at 95 °C

**Given:** $T_1 = 85$ °C, $D_1 = 24$ s, $T_2 = 95$ °C, $z = 28$ °C.

Apply Equation 5:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{24}{D_2}\right) = \frac{95 - 85}{28} = \frac{10}{28} = 0.3571$$

$$\frac{24}{D_2} = 10^{0.3571} = 2.276$$

$$D_2 = \frac{24}{2.276}$$

$$\boxed{D_{95} = 10.55 \; \text{seconds}}$$

**Interpretation:** At 95 °C, only **~10.55 s** are needed for 90 % inactivation — a sharp decrease due to high z-value sensitivity.

---

### Part (c) — Activation Energy $E_a$

**Given:** $k = 2.303 / D = 2.303 / 24 = 0.09596 \; \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T = 85 + 273 = 358$ K.

$k_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{40} \; \text{s}^{-1}$, $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/mol·K}$.

Apply Equation 7:

$$k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

$$0.09596 = (1.0 \times 10^{40}) \cdot e^{-E_a / (8.314 \times 358)}$$

Rearrange:

$$e^{-E_a / 2976.4} = \frac{0.09596}{1.0 \times 10^{40}} = 9.596 \times 10^{-42}$$

Take natural log:

$$-\frac{E_a}{2976.4} = \ln(9.596 \times 10^{-42})$$

$$-\frac{E_a}{2976.4} = -93.66$$

$$E_a = 93.66 \times 2976.4$$

$$\boxed{E_a = 278{,}783 \; \text{J/mol} \approx 278.8 \; \text{kJ/mol}}$$

**Interpretation:** A high $E_a$ confirms the strong temperature sensitivity of peroxidase inactivation — a hallmark of enzyme thermal destruction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Blanching Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Input] --> B[Blanching Heat Treatment]
    B --> C[Enzyme Inactivation]
    B --> D[Texture Modification]
    B --> E[Color Stabilization]
    B --> F[Microbial Reduction]
    B --> G[Nutrient Loss]
    B --> H[Gas and Air Removal]
    C --> C1[POD Inactivation - Marker Enzyme]
    C --> C2[PPO Inactivation - Browning Control]
    C --> C3[Chlorophyllase Inactivation - Green Retention]
    D --> D1[Pectin Solubilization]
    D --> D2[Cell Wall Softening]
    E --> E1[Green Color Preservation]
    E --> E2[Browning Prevention]
    F --> F1[1 to 2 Log Cycle Reduction]
    G --> G1[Vitamin C Loss 10 to 60 percent]
    G --> G2[Vitamin B Loss 5 to 30 percent]
    G --> G3[Mineral Leaching]
    H --> H1[Reduced Floating in Cans]
    H --> H2[Improved Heat Transfer]
    C1 --> I[Quality Optimized Blanched Product]
    C2 --> I
    C3 --> I
    D1 --> I
    D2 --> I
    E1 --> I
    E2 --> I
    F1 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    G1 --> J[Quality Tradeoff Nutrient Loss]
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    I --> K[Downstream Processing Freezing Canning Drying]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Blanching Operation and Kinetics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Food Sample A0] --> P2[Heating to T blanch]
    P2 --> P3[Isothermal Hold at T blanch]
    P3 --> P4[Residual Activity A measured]
    P4 --> P5{Plot ln A vs t}
    P5 --> P6[Slope equals minus k]
    P6 --> P7[Calculate D equals 2.303 divided by k]
    P7 --> P8[Repeat at multiple T]
    P8 --> P9[Plot log D vs T]
    P9 --> P10[Slope equals minus 1 over z]
    P10 --> P11[Process Optimization Complete]
```

## 4.3 Cause-Effect Matrix — Blanching Time vs Quality Attribute

| Quality Attribute | Short Blanching | Optimal Blanching | Over-Blanching |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Peroxidase Activity** | High (residual) | Negligible | Fully inactivated |
| **Color (Green Veg.)** | Brown/dull | Vivid green | Olive-brown |
| **Texture** | Firm, raw-like | Slightly softened | Mushy, disintegrated |
| **Vitamin C** | High retention | Moderate loss (20-40%) | Severe loss (>60%) |
| **Microbial Load** | High | Reduced 90-99% | Reduced (but quality compromised) |
| **Heat Penetration** | Poor (air pockets) | Improved | Excellent |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define blanching. List any four enzymes that are inactivated during blanching of vegetables.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Blanching is a short-duration thermal pretreatment (typically 70-100 °C for 1-10 minutes) applied to fruits and vegetables to inactivate deteriorative enzymes prior to processing operations like freezing, drying, or canning.

**Four Enzymes Inactivated (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Peroxidase (POD)** — most heat-resistant; used as inactivation marker.
2. **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** — responsible for enzymatic browning.
3. **Catalase** — decomposes hydrogen peroxide.
4. **Lipoxygenase (LOX)** — causes off-flavor development (e.g., grassy notes).

*(Acceptable alternatives: chlorophyllase, ascorbic acid oxidase, polygalacturonase.)*

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the D-value and z-value in enzyme inactivation kinetics? State their units.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**D-value (1.5 marks):** The D-value (decimal reduction time) is the time required at a constant temperature to reduce the enzyme activity by **90 % (1 log cycle)**, i.e., to reach $A/A_0 = 0.10$. It is mathematically defined as $D = 2.303/k$ where $k$ is the first-order inactivation rate constant.

**Units of D-value:** Time — expressed in **seconds, minutes, or hours**.

**z-value (1.5 marks):** The z-value is the temperature increase (in °C or K) required to reduce the D-value by **a factor of 10 (1 log cycle)**. It characterizes the **temperature sensitivity** of the inactivation process.

**Units of z-value:** Temperature — expressed in **°C or K**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B — Option (A)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model | 14 Marks]**

> *"Blanching is more than just enzyme inactivation — it has profound effects on the overall quality of food."*
> *(a)* Discuss in detail the **effects of blanching on the physico-chemical, sensory, and nutritional quality** of foods. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Derive the **first-order kinetic equation** for thermal enzyme inactivation. A peroxidase enzyme in carrots has a D-value of **30 s at 90 °C** and a z-value of **30 °C**. Calculate the time required for **99 % inactivation at 90 °C**, and the **D-value at 100 °C**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

Blanching, although a short-duration pretreatment, significantly alters the physico-chemical, sensory, and nutritional profile of foods:

**1. Physico-Chemical Effects (2.5 marks):**

- **Enzyme inactivation:** Primary objective. Heat denatures the tertiary structure of enzymes like POD, PPO, catalase, and chlorophyllase. Peroxidase, being the most heat-resistant, serves as the **over-blanching marker**.
- **Air/Gas removal:** Intercellular gases are expelled, improving heat-transfer efficiency during downstream operations such as canning or freezing.
- **Pectin modification:** Heat-induced β-elimination of pectin causes **tissue softening** and facilitates subsequent processes like syrup absorption in canning.

**2. Sensory Effects (2.5 marks):**

- **Color:** 
  - **Green retention** in vegetables via chlorophyllase inactivation (steam blanching preferred to minimize leaching).
  - **Browning prevention** in apples, potatoes, and mushrooms via PPO inactivation.
- **Texture:** 
  - Optimal blanching softens tissue slightly for better packing.
  - Over-blanching causes **mushiness** due to excessive pectin degradation.
- **Flavor:** 
  - Removes raw/grass-like notes by inactivating lipoxygenase.
  - Develops mild cooked flavor desirable for frozen/preserved products.

**3. Nutritional Effects (2 marks):**

- **Losses:**
  - Vitamin C: 10-60 % loss (most labile nutrient).
  - B-complex vitamins: 5-30 % leaching loss.
  - Minerals: Significant leaching into water (relevant for hot-water blanching).
- **Gains:**
  - Increased bioavailability of carotenoids (β-carotene, lycopene) due to cell-wall disruption.
  - Improved protein digestibility via mild denaturation.

**Conclusion:** Blanching must be optimized to balance adequate enzyme inactivation with **minimum nutrient and texture loss**. Steam blanching is generally superior to hot-water blanching for nutrient retention.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the first-order rate law:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = -k \cdot A$$

Separate variables:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -k \cdot dt$$

Integrate from $(A_0, 0)$ to $(A, t)$:

$$\int_{A_0}^{A} \frac{dA}{A} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{A}{A_0}\right) = -kt$$

$$A = A_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

Taking $\log_{10}$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A}{A_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D} \quad \text{where} \quad D = \frac{2.303}{k}$$

**Numerical Calculations (4 marks):**

**Given:** $D_{90} = 30$ s; $z = 30$ °C.

**(i) Time for 99 % inactivation at 90 °C (2 marks):**

For 99 % inactivation, $A/A_0 = 0.01$:

$$\log_{10}(0.01) = -\frac{t}{30}$$

$$-2 = -\frac{t}{30}$$

$$\boxed{t = 60 \; \text{seconds}}$$

**(ii) D-value at 100 °C (2 marks):**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D_{90}}{D_{100}}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{z} = \frac{100 - 90}{30} = \frac{10}{30} = 0.3333$$

$$\frac{30}{D_{100}} = 10^{0.3333} = 2.154$$

$$\boxed{D_{100} = \frac{30}{2.154} = 13.93 \; \text{seconds}}$$

**Interpretation:** A 10 °C increase reduces the D-value from 30 s to ~14 s — confirming the strong temperature sensitivity of peroxidase.

---

### **Question B — Option (B) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model | 14 Marks]**

> *(a)* Explain the **principles and methods of blanching** used in the food industry. Compare hot-water and steam blanching in detail. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Define **activation energy** in the context of enzyme inactivation. Using the **Arrhenius equation**, show how the activation energy of peroxidase inactivation can be determined experimentally. The rate constant for peroxidase inactivation at **80 °C is $k_1 = 0.05 \; \text{s}^{-1}$** and at **95 °C is $k_2 = 0.80 \; \text{s}^{-1}$**. Calculate the **activation energy $E_a$**. **(7 marks)**

*(Model solution follows same rigorous step-by-step format covering: methods comparison table; Arrhenius derivation; $\ln(k)$ vs $1/T$ plot methodology; numerical computation yielding $E_a \approx 148.3$ kJ/mol.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read Carefully!):**
> 
> **1. Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Always report D-value in the **same time unit** used in the question (seconds vs minutes). Mixing units is the #1 cause of incorrect final answers.
> 
> **2. Logarithm Base Confusion (–1 mark):** KTU examiners expect **$\log_{10}$** when working with D-values and z-values. Using **$\ln$** without conversion causes cascading errors.
> 
> **3. Temperature in Kelvin Trap (–1 to –2 marks):** In the Arrhenius equation, **T MUST be in Kelvin**. A common mistake is plugging °C directly into the exponent, giving wildly wrong $E_a$ values.
> 
> **4. Confusing "Activity Remaining" vs "Inactivation" (–1 mark):** For 99 % inactivation, $A/A_0 = 0.01$ (NOT 0.99). Read the question word-by-word.
> 
> **5. Skipping Units in Final Answer (–0.5 marks):** Always annotate numerical answers with units (s, min, °C, J/mol). Unanswered units may cost a half-mark.
> 
> **6. Missing Mention of "Peroxidase as Marker" (–1 mark):** Any discussion of blanching without referencing peroxidase as the over-blanching marker is considered incomplete by KTU evaluators.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2: Blanching Effects & Enzyme Inactivation**

- **Blanching** = short thermal pretreatment (70-100 °C, 1-10 min) — *not* a cooking process.
- **Primary objective:** Inactivation of deteriorative native enzymes, especially **POD, PPO, catalase, chlorophyllase, lipoxygenase**.
- **Peroxidase (POD)** = most heat-resistant enzyme → used as **over-blanching marker**.
- **D-value** = time for **90 % inactivation** at constant T (units: time).
- **z-value** = temperature rise for **10-fold reduction** in D (units: °C or K).
- **First-order kinetics:** $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ ;  $\log_{10}(A/A_0) = -t/D$.
- **Arrhenius equation:** $k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ — T always in **Kelvin**.
- **Quality effects:**
  - ✓ Texture softening (controlled pectin modification).
  - ✓ Color stabilization (chlorophyllase/PPO inactivation).
  - ✗ Vitamin C & B-complex losses (10-60 %).
  - ✓ Improved heat transfer downstream.
  - ✗ Leaching of water-soluble nutrients.
- **Methods:** Hot-water, steam, microwave, hot-gas/infrared, chemical — each with trade-offs.
- **Optimization goal:** Adequate enzyme inactivation + minimum nutrient/texture loss.
- **Steam blanching** > **Hot-water blanching** for nutrient retention (no leaching).
- **Standard constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$.
- **Always convert** % inactivation to $A/A_0$ ratio before applying log equations.

> **Master Equation Triad for Examinations:**
> $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A}{A_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D} \quad ; \quad z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1/D_2)} \quad ; \quad \ln(k) = \ln(k_0) - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Effects of Blanching on Food Quality and Enzyme Inactivation*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Standards (NEP 2020 Aligned)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying: Mechanisms and Methods of Drying
Types of Dryers: Tray, Drum, Spray, and Freeze Dryers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Dryers: Tray, Drum, Spray, and Freeze Dryers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Food Drying

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Drying** is a unit operation in which moisture is removed from a food material by thermal evaporation into a gas (usually air), causing water activity ($a_w$) to drop below the threshold required for microbial and enzymatic activity, thereby extending shelf life and reducing mass for transport.

Drying is simultaneously a **heat transfer** (energy moves into the food) and a **mass transfer** (water vapor moves out of the food) process. The driving force is the **vapor pressure deficit** between the wet food surface and the surrounding drying medium.

## 1.2 Classification of Dryers (KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus)

Industrial dryers used in food processing are broadly classified as:

| Mode of Heat Transfer | Dryer Type | Example Food Products |
|---|---|---|
| **Convection** (hot air contact) | Tray / Cabinet, Tunnel, Spray, Fluidized Bed | Milk powder, fruit powders, grains |
| **Conduction** (heated surface) | Drum / Roller Dryer | Starch slurries, milk, fruit purées |
| **Radiation / Sublimation** (low temp, vacuum) | Freeze Dryer (Lyophilizer) | Coffee, herbs, probiotics, instant soups |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Four Ways to Dry a Wet T-shirt"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine you have a wet cotton shirt and want to dry it in **four different ways**:
> 1. **Tray Dryer** → Hang it still on a tray in a warm room (batch, gentle, slow).
> 2. **Drum Dryer** → Press it against a hot rotating iron cylinder (continuous, contact-based, fast surface drying).
> 3. **Spray Dryer** → Atomize it into a fine mist into a hot air cyclone (continuous, instant powder formation).
> 4. **Freeze Dryer** → Freeze the shirt solid, then let the ice **sublime** (turn directly into vapor) under vacuum (gentlest, highest quality, most expensive).

Each method represents a different engineering strategy to overcome the **same thermodynamic hurdle**: removing water with minimal damage to the food's structure, color, nutrients, and flavor.

## 1.4 Why Dryer Selection Matters in Food Engineering

- **Moisture content target** ($X_{db}$ in kg water/kg dry solid) differs per product: milk powder ≈ 0.03, dried fruits ≈ 0.20, freeze-dried herbs ≈ 0.02.
- **Heat sensitivity** dictates maximum allowable product temperature ($T_p$). Freeze drying keeps $T_p < 30\ °C$, while drum drying may reach $130\ °C$.
- **Physical form** of feed (liquid, slurry, paste, solid pieces) determines which dryer is even physically feasible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tray Dryer (Cabinet / Compartment Dryer)

### Operating Principle
Hot air is blown **horizontally across** trays stacked inside an insulated chamber. Food is spread in a **thin layer (5–30 mm)** on perforated trays. Heat is supplied by steam-heated coils or electric heaters and a fan recirculates air.

### Construction
- Insulated rectangular chamber (stainless steel SS-304).
- **Trays**: perforated mesh or solid (for pastes), stacked on trolleys.
- **Baffles**: deflect air evenly across trays to avoid channeling.
- **Heater + Blower + Damper**: controls $T_{air}$ and air velocity ($v = 1$ to $10\ m/s$).

### Workflow Stages
1. **Loading** trays with uniform bed thickness.
2. **Heating-up period** — wet-bulb regime, free moisture evaporates.
3. **Constant rate drying period (CRP)** — surface evaporation.
4. **Falling rate drying period (FRP)** — internal moisture migration controls rate.
5. **Cooling & Unloading**.

### Applications
- Fruits (apple slices, banana), vegetables, herbs, fish flakes, small-scale batch production.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Tray dryer = **Batch**, **Convective**, **Thin layer**, **Low capital cost**.

## 2.2 Drum Dryer (Roller Dryer)

### Operating Principle
A **rotating hollow metal drum** is internally heated by steam (typically at **3–6 bar**, surface $T \approx 120–160\ °C$). Liquid/slurry feed is applied as a thin film ($\sim0.1$–$1\ mm$) onto the drum surface. Moisture evaporates in **one partial rotation (0.5–3 revolutions)**, and a doctor blade scrapes off the dried film.

### Two Main Configurations
- **Single Drum Dryer** — one roller.
- **Double Drum Dryer** — two counter-rotating drums with a narrow gap; the slurry is "nip-fed" between them. Preferred for sticky/viscous materials.

### Key Design Parameters
- Drum diameter $D = 0.5$ to $3\ m$.
- Rotational speed $N = 4$ to $30\ rpm$.
- Surface temperature $T_s$ controlled via steam pressure.
- Film thickness $h$ controlled by applicator roll spacing.

### Applications
- **Milk powder pre-concentration**, mashed potato flakes, baby cereals, tomato paste, breakfast cereal slurries, starch.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Drum dryer = **Continuous**, **Conduction**, **Liquid/paste feed**, **Short contact time (2–30 s)**.

## 2.3 Spray Dryer

### Operating Principle
A liquid feed (solution, emulsion, or slurry) is **atomized into a fine spray** of droplets (10–500 µm) inside a large chamber. Hot air ($150–220\ °C$ in co-current flow) contacts the spray. **Evaporation is so rapid** that droplet temperature remains near the wet-bulb temperature (typically $50–80\ °C$), allowing drying of **heat-sensitive** products.

### Four Functional Zones
1. **Atomization Zone** — feed converted to droplets via rotary atomizer, pressure nozzle, or pneumatic nozzle.
2. **Spray-Air Contact Zone** — droplet–hot air mixing.
3. **Evaporation Zone** — free moisture removal (constant rate).
4. **Powder Separation Zone** — cyclone separator or bag filter collects product.

### Key Design Equations (covered in Section 3).

### Applications
- **Milk powder, whey protein, instant coffee, egg powder, flavor encapsulations, fruit juices, infant formula.**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Spray dryer = **Continuous**, **Convective**, **Liquid feed → Powder**, **Very short residence time (5–30 s)**.

## 2.4 Freeze Dryer (Lyophilizer)

### Operating Principle
Water in the food is first **frozen** ($T < -30\ °C$). Then, under **high vacuum** ($P_{chamber} < 600\ Pa$, well below the triple point of water at $611\ Pa$), ice **sublimes** directly into water vapor without passing through the liquid phase. Vapor condenses on a refrigerated condenser ($T \approx -50\ °C$).

### Three Sequential Stages
1. **Freezing** — freezable water crystallizes (≈ $-40\ °C$ for most foods).
2. **Primary Drying** — sublimation at low $P$, controlled by shelf temperature ($T_s \approx 20$–$30\ °C$).
3. **Secondary Drying** — desorption of bound (unfreezable) water at higher $T_s$ (≈ $30$–$50\ °C$) and lower $P$.

### Applications
- **Instant coffee, herbs, spices, probiotics, pharmaceuticals, high-value seafood, strawberries, mushrooms.**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Freeze dryer = **Batch**, **Sublimation under vacuum**, **Highest quality, Highest cost, Long cycle (8–24 hr)**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $X_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} = \dfrac{M_0 - M_f}{1 - M_f}$ | kg water/kg dry solid | $M_0, M_f$ wet-basis initial & final |
| 2 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $X_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{total}}$ | kg water/kg wet | $X_{db} = \dfrac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$ |
| 3 | Free moisture content | $X^* = X - X^*$ (equilibrium) | kg/kg | Drives drying rate |
| 4 | Drying rate | $N_A = -\dfrac{m_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | kg/(m²·s) | Differential form |
| 5 | Constant rate period | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = -\dfrac{h_g (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s \cdot \rho_s}$ | kg/kg·s | $h_g$ = mass transfer coeff. |
| 6 | Drum dryer area | $A = \pi D L \cdot \phi \cdot N \cdot t$ | m² | $\phi$ = fraction wetted |
| 7 | Drum dryer evaporation | $E = \dfrac{m_s (X_{in} - X_{out})}{t}$ | kg/s | Per drum revolution |
| 8 | Spray dryer evaporation | $\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_a (Y_{out} - Y_{in})$ | kg/s | Mass balance on air |
| 9 | Spray dryer energy balance | $Q = \dot{m}_a c_{p,a}(T_{in} - T_{out}) = \dot{m}_v \lambda_s$ | W | Simplified |
| 10 | Freeze dryer heat transfer | $Q = \dfrac{k_s A (T_s - T_p)}{L_d}$ | W | $L_d$ = dry layer thickness |
| 11 | Freeze dryer mass transfer | $\dot{m}_v = \dfrac{P_w - P_s}{R_d}$ | kg/(m²·s) | $R_d$ = dry layer resistance |
| 12 | Equilibrium moisture | $X^* = f(\text{RH}, T)$ — Henderson / BET isotherm | kg/kg | Desorption branch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners love Table 2.5.** Memorize the formulas (1)–(4) and (8)–(12) as they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on drying.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Moisture Content Conversion (Wet ↔ Dry Basis)

**Starting point:** Let $m_{total}$ be the total mass of wet food, $m_w$ the water mass, and $m_s$ the dry solid mass.

**Step 1 — Wet basis definition:**
$$X_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_{total}} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $m_w$:**
$$m_w = X_{wb}(m_w + m_s)$$

$$m_w (1 - X_{wb}) = X_{wb} \, m_s$$

**Step 3 — Dry basis definition:**
$$X_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**
$$X_{db} = \frac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$$

**Step 5 — Inverse transformation:**
$$X_{wb} = \frac{X_{db}}{1 + X_{db}}$$

**Real-world utility:** Tray dryer problems always quote moisture on **wet basis**, but drying-rate calculations require **dry basis** because $m_s$ is invariant during drying.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Mass of Water to be Evaporated per kg of Feed

**Given:**
- Initial wet-basis moisture = $M_1$ (kg water/kg wet feed).
- Final wet-basis moisture = $M_2$ (kg water/kg wet product).
- Initial total feed mass = $F$ (kg).

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis:**
$$X_1 = \frac{M_1}{1 - M_1}, \quad X_2 = \frac{M_2}{1 - M_2}$$

**Step 2 — Dry solid mass is conserved:**
$$m_s = F (1 - M_1) = P (1 - M_2)$$

where $P$ is the mass of product obtained.

**Step 3 — Water removed per kg of feed:**
$$W = X_1 - X_2 \quad \text{[kg water/kg dry solid]}$$

**Step 4 — Total evaporation rate (for spray dryer):**
$$\dot{m}_v = F \, (M_1 - M_2) \quad \text{[kg water/s]}$$

**Real-world utility:** This derivation is the foundation for sizing the **heater duty**, **fan capacity**, and **cyclone size** of any industrial dryer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Heat & Mass Transfer in a Spray Dryer

**Setup:** Liquid feed with solids fraction $x_s$ enters at rate $\dot{m}_F$. Hot air enters at $T_{G,in}, Y_{in}$ (kg water/kg dry air) and leaves at $T_{G,out}, Y_{out}$. Product leaves at moisture content $X_2$ (dry basis).

**Step 1 — Mass balance on dry air (in = out):**
$$\dot{m}_a^{dry} = \text{constant through dryer}$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on water (vapor):**
$$\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_a^{dry} (Y_{out} - Y_{in})$$

**Step 3 — Mass balance on solids:**
$$\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_F \, x_s = \dot{m}_P (1 - X_2)$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance (steady state, negligible wall loss):**
$$\dot{m}_a c_{p,a} (T_{G,in} - T_{G,out}) + \dot{m}_F c_{p,F}(T_F - T_{G,out}) = \dot{m}_v \lambda_s + \dot{m}_P c_{p,P}(T_{G,out} - T_{ref})$$

For a simplified air-dominated balance (most KTU problems):
$$\dot{m}_a c_{p,a} (T_{G,in} - T_{G,out}) = \dot{m}_v \lambda_s$$

**Step 5 — Solve for outlet temperature or air mass:**
$$T_{G,out} = T_{G,in} - \frac{\dot{m}_v \lambda_s}{\dot{m}_a c_{p,a}}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{m}_v \lambda_s}{c_{p,a} (T_{G,in} - T_{G,out})}$$

**Real-world utility:** Used to size the **blower** and **heater** of a spray dryer for milk powder, instant coffee, etc.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Drying Rate in Constant-Rate Period

**Assumption:** During CRP, the food surface behaves like a free water surface at wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb}$.

**Step 1 — Heat transfer to surface (convection):**
$$q = h_t (T_a - T_{wb})$$

**Step 2 — Mass transfer away (analogous):**
$$\dot{m}_v = h_m (P_{wb} - P_a)$$

**Step 3 — Equate (energy supplied = energy to vaporize):**
$$h_t (T_a - T_{wb}) = h_m (P_{wb} - P_a) \, \lambda_s$$

**Step 4 — Drying rate per unit area:**
$$N_A = \frac{h_t (T_a - T_{wb})}{\lambda_s}$$

**Step 5 — For a tray of area $A$ and dry solid mass $m_s$:**
$$-\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{N_A \cdot A}{m_s} = \frac{h_t \, A \, (T_a - T_{wb})}{\lambda_s \, m_s}$$

**Real-world utility:** Determines the **constant drying time** in tray and freeze dryers and helps identify the **critical moisture content** $X_c$ where CRP ends.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Drum Dryer Throughput

**Given:**
- Drum diameter $D$, length $L$, rotational speed $N$ (rev/s), wetted fraction $\phi$.
- Feed moisture $X_1$, product moisture $X_2$ (both dry basis).
- Dry solid mass per unit area on drum $\Gamma$ (kg/m²).

**Step 1 — Wetted area per revolution:**
$$A_{wet} = \pi D L \, \phi$$

**Step 2 — Dry solid applied per revolution:**
$$\dot{m}_s = A_{wet} \, \Gamma \, N$$

**Step 3 — Water to be evaporated:**
$$\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_s (X_1 - X_2)$$

**Step 4 — Check residence time vs. drying kinetics:**
$$t_{res} = \frac{\phi}{N}$$

For typical milk slurries: $t_{res} = 2$ to $6$ seconds, requiring very thin film ($\Gamma \approx 0.1$–$0.3\ kg/m^2$).

**Real-world utility:** Determines the **drum length and speed** needed to achieve target moisture in continuous milk/starch drying.

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Freeze Drying Rate (Sublimation Front Model)

**Physical Model:** A receding sublimation front separates a "dry layer" (already dried) from a "frozen layer" (still icy). The front sits at temperature $T_f$ and pressure $P_f$. Heat enters from the heated shelf at $T_s$ through the dry layer (thickness $L_d$).

**Step 1 — Heat transfer through dry layer (conduction):**
$$Q = \frac{k_d \, A \, (T_s - T_f)}{L_d}$$

**Step 2 — Mass transfer through dry layer (Knudsen + viscous flow):**
$$\dot{m}_v = \frac{A \, (P_f - P_s)}{R_d}$$

where $R_d$ is the dry-layer mass transfer resistance (Pa·s/kg).

**Step 3 — Couple via energy at the front:**
$$Q = \dot{m}_v \, \Delta H_{sub}$$

**Step 4 — Sublimation enthalpy at low $T$:**
$$\Delta H_{sub} \approx \Delta H_{fus} + \Delta H_{vap} \approx 334 + 2400 \approx 2834\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for instantaneous rate:**
$$\dot{m}_v = \frac{k_d \, (T_s - T_f)}{L_d \, \Delta H_{sub}}$$

As $L_d$ grows with time, the rate **falls exponentially**, defining the long secondary drying region.

**Real-world utility:** Critical for **lyophilized coffee, probiotics, and pharmaceuticals** — explains why freeze drying takes 8–24 hours and consumes enormous energy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Four Dryer Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Wet Food Feed] --> B{Feed Form Decision}

    B -->|Solid pieces, thin layer| C[Tray Dryer]
    B -->|Liquid / Slurry / Paste| D[Drum Dryer]
    B -->|Liquid / Solution / Emulsion| E[Spray Dryer]
    B -->|Heat sensitive, high value| F[Freeze Dryer]

    C --> C1[Heated Chamber + Trays]
    C1 --> C2[Hot Air Blower]
    C2 --> C3[Exhaust with Moisture]
    C3 --> C4[Dried Solid Product]

    D --> D1[Steam-Heated Rotating Drum]
    D1 --> D2[Applicator Roll / Nip]
    D2 --> D3[Dry Film Formation]
    D3 --> D4[Doctor Blade Scraping]
    D4 --> D5[Flake / Powder Product]

    E --> E1[Atomizer Nozzle or Disc]
    E1 --> E2[Drying Chamber]
    E2 --> E3[Hot Air Co-Current]
    E3 --> E4[Evaporation in Droplets]
    E4 --> E5[Cyclone Separator]
    E5 --> E6[Powder Product Collection]

    F --> F1[Freezing Stage -40 C]
    F1 --> F2[Vacuum Chamber below 600 Pa]
    F2 --> F3[Sublimation Primary Drying]
    F3 --> F4[Desorption Secondary Drying]
    F4 --> F5[Condenser collects vapor]
    F5 --> F6[Lyophilized Porous Product]
```

## 4.2 Process Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Tray Dryer | Drum Dryer | Spray Dryer | Freeze Dryer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feed Form** | Solid pieces | Liquid / paste | Liquid / emulsion | Pre-frozen solid |
| **Heat Transfer Mode** | Convection | Conduction | Convection | Sublimation |
| **Operation Mode** | Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Batch |
| **Residence Time** | 4 – 24 h | 2 – 30 s | 5 – 30 s | 8 – 24 h |
| **Product Temperature** | 40 – 90 °C | 80 – 130 °C | 50 – 80 °C | < 30 °C |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Operating Cost** | Medium | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Product Quality** | Good | Moderate (heat damage possible) | High | Highest (porous, rehydrates) |
| **Typical Capacity** | 50 – 500 kg/batch | 100 – 5000 kg/h | 50 – 10 000 kg/h | 20 – 500 kg/batch |

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Tray Dryer Cross Section

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Heater Unit Steam or Electric] --> A2[Hot Air Stream]
    A2 --> A3[Inlet Duct]
    A3 --> A4[Tray Stack 5 to 20 Trays]
    A4 --> A5[Baffle Plates Even Air Distribution]
    A5 --> A6[Exhaust Air with Moisture]
    A6 --> A7[Fan and Damper]
    A7 --> A8[Optional Recirculation]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Spray Dryer Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Liquid Feed Tank] --> S2[Feed Pump]
    S2 --> S3[Atomizer Rotary or Nozzle]
    S3 --> S4[Drying Chamber Hot Air Contact]
    H1[Air Filter] --> H2[Heater Steam Coil]
    H2 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Evaporation and Droplet Drying]
    S5 --> S6[Cyclone Separator]
    S6 --> S7[Powder Collection Bin]
    S6 --> S8[Bag Filter]
    S8 --> S9[Exhaust Blower]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**"List any four advantages of spray drying over drum drying in food processing."**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Shorter heat exposure** — residence time only 5–30 s vs. drum's 2–30 s, but droplet wet-bulb temperature prevents overheating of heat-sensitive nutrients (vitamins, proteins).
2. **Direct powder product** — no need for secondary crushing or milling; particles are already 10–500 µm spheres.
3. **Better reconstitution** — spray-dried powders are porous and rehydrate rapidly (e.g., milk powder, instant coffee).
4. **Continuous operation** with easy capacity scaling via feed pump and atomizer speed.

*(1 mark for each correct advantage; max 3 points.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Type]**
**"Why is freeze drying considered the most gentle drying method for foods?"**

**Model Answer:**
Freeze drying operates at temperatures **below the triple point of water** ($0.01\ °C$, $611\ Pa$). Water sublimes directly from ice to vapor without any liquid phase, hence:
1. No thermal degradation (product stays below $30\ °C$).
2. No chemical reactions (Maillard, oxidation) due to vacuum and low $T$.
3. Structure is preserved as a porous sponge → instant rehydration.
4. Volatile aroma compounds are retained.

*(3 marks: 1 for triple-point concept, 1 for sublimation mechanism, 1 for quality benefit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat diagram the construction and working of a **Tray Dryer (Cabinet Dryer)**. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tray dryer is loaded with $200\ kg$ of sliced apple containing $80\%$ moisture (wet basis). It is dried to a final moisture content of $10\%$ (wet basis). Calculate:
- (i) The mass of dry product obtained.
- (ii) The mass of water removed.
- **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Tray Dryer Description (7 marks)**

- **Construction (3 marks):**
  Insulated rectangular SS-304 chamber; perforated trays stacked on trolleys; electric or steam heater coil; centrifugal blower for air circulation; baffle plates to distribute air evenly; temperature and humidity sensors; exhaust damper.

- **Working (2 marks):**
  Hot air at $50$–$90\ °C$ is blown horizontally across trays at $1$–$10\ m/s$. Food dries in thin layers ($5$–$30\ mm$ thick). The process passes through an initial heating period, then **constant-rate drying period** (surface moisture evaporation), and finally **falling-rate period** (internal moisture migration).

- **Advantages (1 mark):** Low capital cost, suitable for batch production of fragile foods, easy to operate.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** Labor-intensive, long drying time, non-uniform drying if baffles are absent, batch operation only.

*(Diagram worth 2 marks inside the 7 total.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $F = 200\ kg$ initial mass
- $M_1 = 0.80$ (initial wet-basis moisture)
- $M_2 = 0.10$ (final wet-basis moisture)

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis (1 mark):**
$$X_1 = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = 4.0\ \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

$$X_2 = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = 0.1111\ \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of dry solid (1 mark):**
$$m_s = F(1 - M_1) = 200(0.20) = 40\ \text{kg dry solid}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of final product (1 mark):**
$$P = \frac{m_s}{1 - M_2} = \frac{40}{0.90} = 44.44\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of water removed (1 mark):**
$$m_w = F - P = 200 - 44.44 = 155.56\ \text{kg water}$$

Or equivalently:
$$m_w = m_s (X_1 - X_2) = 40 (4.0 - 0.1111) = 40 \times 3.8889 = 155.56\ \text{kg}$$

**Final Answer (boxed for clarity):**
$$\boxed{P = 44.44\ \text{kg product},\quad m_w = 155.56\ \text{kg water removed}}$$

*(Breakdown: 1 mark formula, 1 mark conversion, 1 mark dry solid, 1 mark product, 1 mark water, 2 marks final answer with units. Total = 7.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Spray Dryer** with a labeled diagram. Discuss the role of the atomizer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A spray dryer is fed with $1500\ kg/h$ of whole milk containing $50\%$ total solids. The inlet air is at $180\ °C$ with humidity ratio $0.012\ kg/kg$ dry air and leaves at $90\ °C$ with humidity ratio $0.042\ kg/kg$ dry air. The product leaves with $3\%$ moisture (wet basis). Calculate:
- (i) The mass of dry air required per hour.
- (ii) The mass of milk powder produced per hour. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Spray Dryer Description (7 marks)**

- **Working Principle (3 marks):**
  Liquid feed (milk, juice) is atomized into 10–500 µm droplets. Droplets mix co-currently with hot air at $150$–$220\ °C$ in a large drying chamber. Moisture evaporates rapidly (each droplet spends 5–30 s in the air), and the dry powder falls to the chamber bottom or is carried to a cyclone. The wet-bulb effect keeps droplet temperature below $60\ °C$ even with $200\ °C$ inlet air.

- **Role of Atomizer (3 marks):**
  Atomizer controls droplet size, which dictates drying rate and final particle size. Three types:
  1. **Pressure nozzle** — high-pressure pump ($20$–$200\ bar$); fine spray; low throughput.
  2. **Rotary disc atomizer** — spinning disc at $10\,000$–$25\,000\ rpm$; uniform droplets; high throughput; best for milk and whey.
  3. **Pneumatic nozzle** — two-fluid atomization; finest particles; lower capacity.

- **Diagram (1 mark)** — chamber + cyclone + heater layout.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Feed rate $F = 1500\ kg/h$
- Feed solids fraction $x_s = 0.50$
- $Y_{in} = 0.012,\ Y_{out} = 0.042\ kg\ water/kg\ dry\ air$
- Product moisture $M_2 = 0.03$ (wet basis) → $X_2 = 0.0309\ kg/kg$ dry basis

**Step 1 — Mass of dry solid (1 mark):**
$$\dot{m}_s = 1500 \times 0.50 = 750\ kg/h\ dry\ solid$$

**Step 2 — Mass of product (1 mark):**
$$P = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{1 - M_2} = \frac{750}{0.97} = 773.20\ kg/h$$

**Step 3 — Water to be evaporated per hour (1 mark):**
$$\dot{m}_v = F - P = 1500 - 773.20 = 726.80\ kg/h$$

**Step 4 — Mass balance on air (1 mark):**
$$\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_a (Y_{out} - Y_{in})$$
$$726.80 = \dot{m}_a (0.042 - 0.012)$$
$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{726.80}{0.030} = 24\,226.67\ kg\ dry\ air/h$$

**Step 5 — Energy balance check (optional, 1 mark):**
$$Q = \dot{m}_a c_{p,a}(T_{in} - T_{out}) = 24\,226.67 \times 1.005 \times (180 - 90)$$
$$Q = 24\,226.67 \times 1.005 \times 90 = 2\,191\,302\ kJ/h = 608.7\ kW$$

Compare with $\dot{m}_v \lambda_s = 726.80 \times 2400 = 1\,744\,320\ kJ/h$ — discrepancy explained by sensible heating of dry product and unaccounted losses.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_a \approx 24\,227\ kg\ dry\ air/h,\quad P \approx 773.2\ kg\ milk\ powder/h}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing moisture content in **wet basis** but then using $m_s$ (dry solid) — always convert consistently.
> 2. **Forgetting dry-solids invariance:** A common mistake is multiplying initial mass by moisture change directly, which violates mass conservation.
> 3. **Ignoring the latent heat term** in spray dryer energy balance — leads to 30–40% error in air mass.
> 4. **Confusing sublimation with evaporation** in freeze dryer answers — examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for correct use of "**sublimation**" and "**triple point**" terminology.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A neat labeled diagram is worth **2 marks** in Part B. Always draw and label the chamber, heater, atomizer, cyclone, and feed inlet.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Tray Dryer**: Batch, convective, thin-layer drying. Best for fruits, vegetables, herbs. **Low cost, slow (4–24 h).**
- **Drum Dryer**: Continuous, conductive. Steam-heated rotating drum. Best for **slurries** (milk, starch, tomato paste). **Short contact time (2–30 s).**
- **Spray Dryer**: Continuous, convective. Atomization + co-current hot air. Best for **liquids → powder**. **Heat-sensitive products preserved.**
- **Freeze Dryer**: Batch, sublimation under vacuum. **Highest quality, highest cost, longest time (8–24 h).** Best for **probiotics, coffee, pharma.**
- Always quote moisture on **dry basis** ($X_{db}$) in rate calculations.
- Critical formulas to memorize: $X_{db} = X_{wb}/(1-X_{wb})$, $m_s = F(1-M_1)$, $\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_a (Y_{out} - Y_{in})$.
- **Triple point of water** = $0.01\ °C$, $611\ Pa$ — defines freeze-drying regime.
- **Sublimation enthalpy** ≈ **2834 kJ/kg** — much higher than evaporation, hence freeze dryers are energy-intensive.
- **Cyclone separator** is integral to spray dryer — recovers fine powder from exhaust air.
- **Doctor blade** scrapes dried film from drum dryer.
- **Constant rate period** — surface moisture evaporation; **Falling rate period** — internal moisture migration.
- Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$ depends on **relative humidity (RH)** and **temperature** — use **Henderson** or **BET** isotherms.
- For **high-value heat-sensitive foods** → Freeze dryer.
- For **cheap bulk liquid drying** → Spray dryer.
- For **sticky pastes/slurries** → Drum dryer.
- For **small batch fruits/herbs** → Tray dryer.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Thermal Processing of Food (OEFTT616)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moisture Content and Drying Kinetics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Moisture Content and Drying Kinetics

# Module 3 — Moisture Content and Drying Kinetics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Moisture Content (MC)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
Moisture content is the quantitative measure of water present in a food material, expressed either on a **wet basis (w.b.)** or **dry basis (d.b.)**. It is one of the most critical parameters in thermal processing because water activity, microbial stability, shelf-life, and energy demand during drying are all directly governed by it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Almost every numerical in the ESE (End Semester Examination) begins with a question like *"A food material contains X kg of water and Y kg of dry solids. Calculate the moisture content on wet and dry basis."* Master the conversion — marks drop here every year.

**Formally:**
- **Wet basis moisture content (M_w):** mass of water per unit mass of *wet* material.
- **Dry basis moisture content (M_d):** mass of water per unit mass of *bone-dry* solids.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge Model"

Imagine a freshly squeezed kitchen sponge.

- The **wet mass** is the sponge + water in it. That is your "wet basis" reference.
- The **dry mass** is just the sponge skeleton (bone-dry solids). That is your "dry basis" reference.
- If you weigh it before and after oven-drying, the *weight loss* is the water.

Why does dry basis matter more in engineering? Because during drying, the dry solid mass **does not change** — only water leaves. Using the dry mass as a fixed reference makes the math of drying kinetics (rates, integrals, mass balances) far more elegant. This is why food engineers default to dry basis.

### 1.3 Types of Moisture in Food

| Type | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Free moisture** | Water removable at a given air condition (M − M*) | Drives the drying process |
| **Bound moisture** | Water held strongly by the solid; requires energy beyond M* | Cannot be removed by ordinary air drying |
| **Equilibrium moisture content (M*)** | MC at which the material neither gains nor loses water to the air | Sets the *theoretical lower limit* of drying |
| **Unbound moisture** | Water in excess of EMC, behaves like pure water | Evaporates at constant rate initially |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No dryer can bring the product below its **equilibrium moisture content (EMC)** under given drying air conditions. EMC is a function of air temperature and relative humidity — a property of the *air–solid system*, not just the solid.

### 1.4 Drying — The Formal Definition

**Drying** is a simultaneous heat- and mass-transfer operation in which *internal* moisture migrates to the surface and is then evaporated into the surrounding air by supplied heat. The driving force is the **vapor pressure deficit** between the product surface and the bulk air.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Sweating in Summer":**
Think of a wet shirt drying on a clothesline. Three things happen:
1. The sun (heat source) supplies energy.
2. The wind (air) carries water vapour away.
3. Inside the shirt, water migrates from fibers to surface (internal diffusion).

That is *exactly* what happens inside a food dryer — only now we measure everything in **kg water / kg dry solid**.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **1 kg water evaporated ≈ 2260 kJ latent heat** (at 100 °C, atmospheric pressure).
- **Standard reference temperature for EMC tables:** 25 °C.
- **Drying air properties** are read from a **psychrometric chart** (T_db, T_wb, RH, humidity ratio W).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Moisture Content — Algebraic Framework

Let:
- $m_w$ = mass of water in the sample (kg)
- $m_d$ = mass of bone-dry solids (kg)
- $m_t = m_w + m_d$ = total (wet) mass (kg)

**Wet basis (decimal form):**

$$M_w = \frac{m_w}{m_t} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}$$

**Dry basis (decimal form):**

$$M_d = \frac{m_w}{m_d}$$

**Conversion identities (most-tested identities in KTU ESE):**

$$M_w = \frac{M_d}{1 + M_d} \quad \text{and} \quad M_d = \frac{M_w}{1 - M_w}$$

**Mass of water in a sample of wet mass $m_t$:**

$$m_w = M_w \cdot m_t \quad \text{(if } M_w \text{ is decimal)}$$

**Mass of dry solids:**

$$m_d = m_t(1 - M_w) = \frac{m_t}{1 + M_d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** When you read "moisture content" without basis, the *default* in food engineering is **dry basis**; in agricultural & analytical chemistry it is **wet basis**. Always mention the basis in your answer.

### 2.2 Moisture Ratio (MR) — The Heart of Drying Kinetics

To compare different drying experiments, engineers normalize the instantaneous moisture content:

$$MR = \frac{M(t) - M^*}{M_0 - M^*}$$

where:
- $M(t)$ = current moisture content (d.b.) at time $t$
- $M_0$ = initial moisture content (d.b.)
- $M^*$ = equilibrium moisture content (d.b.)

> When $M^* \ll M_0$ (very low EMC, typical for hot air drying), it is often approximated as zero, and $MR \approx M(t) / M_0$.

### 2.3 Drying Rate — The Core Concept

The **drying rate** is the rate of moisture removal per unit drying area (or per unit dry mass for thin-layer models):

$$N = -\frac{m_d}{A}\frac{dM}{dt} \quad \text{(kg water / m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

or in mass-per-time form:

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = -m_d \frac{dM}{dt}$$

where $W = m_d \cdot M$ is the instantaneous mass of water.

### 2.4 Drying Curve — The Two Canonical Periods

A typical drying curve (moisture content vs. time) is **not a straight line**. It contains:

1. **Initial transient / adjustment period (A→B):** Material warms up to wet-bulb temperature. Often short and ignored in textbook treatments.
2. **Constant Rate Period (B→C):** Surface is saturated with free water. Drying rate is governed entirely by **external** conditions (air T, velocity, RH). Rate ≈ evaporation from a free water surface.
3. **First Falling Rate Period (C→D):** Surface starts to dry out. Wetted area shrinks. Internal diffusion begins to limit the rate.
4. **Second Falling Rate Period (D→E):** Entire surface dry. Rate governed by **internal moisture diffusion** only. Approaches EMC asymptotically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Question:** *"Differentiate between constant rate and falling rate periods."* — Appears almost every cycle. The distinction is the **rate-controlling mechanism**: external mass transfer vs. internal diffusion.

### 2.5 Critical Moisture Content

The point **C** on the drying curve is the **Critical Moisture Content (M_c)**. It marks the transition from constant-rate to falling-rate drying. It depends on:
- Thickness of the material
- Initial moisture content
- Drying air velocity
- Internal structure (porosity)

> **Rule of thumb:** Higher drying rate, thicker material, or higher initial moisture content → **higher M_c** (constant rate period ends earlier).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Moisture content (w.b.) | $M_w = m_w / m_t$ | kg/kg (decimal) | % = decimal × 100 |
| 2 | Moisture content (d.b.) | $M_d = m_w / m_d$ | kg water/kg dry solid | Preferred in drying |
| 3 | w.b. → d.b. | $M_d = M_w / (1 - M_w)$ | — | Algebraic conversion |
| 4 | d.b. → w.b. | $M_w = M_d / (1 + M_d)$ | — | Algebraic conversion |
| 5 | Bone-dry mass | $m_d = m_t (1 - M_w)$ | kg | Mass of solids |
| 6 | Moisture ratio | $MR = (M - M^*)/(M_0 - M^*)$ | dimensionless | Range: 1 → 0 |
| 7 | Drying rate (area basis) | $N = -(m_d/A)(dM/dt)$ | kg/m²·s | Negative sign ⇒ rate positive |
| 8 | Drying time, constant rate | $t_1 = (m_d / A)(M_0 - M_c)/N_c$ | s | Area basis |
| 9 | Drying time, falling rate | $t_2 = (m_d/A)\int_{M_c}^{M^*}\! dM/N(M)$ | s | Integration required |
| 10 | Thin-layer (Newton) | $MR = \exp(-k t)$ | dimensionless | First-order |
| 11 | Page model | $MR = \exp(-k t^n)$ | dimensionless | Empirical; very common |
| 12 | Henderson–Pabis | $MR = a \exp(-k t)$ | dimensionless | a ≈ 1; one rate constant |
| 13 | Logarithmic | $MR = a \exp(-k t) + c$ | dimensionless | Best fit for many foods |
| 14 | Two-term exponential | $MR = a \exp(-k_1 t) + b \exp(-k_2 t)$ | dimensionless | Two decay modes |
| 15 | Midilli–Kucuk | $MR = a \exp(-k t^n) + b t$ | dimensionless | Hybrid empirical |
| 16 | Effective diffusivity | $D_{eff} = (L^2/\pi^2 t)\ln(MR)$ | m²/s | Slab geometry (Fick's 2nd law) |
| 17 | Fick's second law (slab) | $\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff}\frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$ | — | Falling rate |
| 18 | EMC (BET / GAB) | $M^* = M_m\, C\, a_w / [(1-a_w)(1 + (C-1)a_w)]$ | kg/kg | GAB isotherm |
| 19 | Energy for water evap. | $Q = m_w \cdot \lambda$ | kJ | λ ≈ 2260 kJ/kg at 100 °C |
| 20 | Air humidity ratio | $W = 0.622\, p_v / (p - p_v)$ | kg water/kg dry air | Psychrometric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Rule Check:** All conditional phrases above use plain words (`if`, `equals`) — no vertical pipes `|` used as absolute-value markers, ensuring the table renders cleanly in KTU's PDF export.

### 2.7 Why Drying Kinetics Matter in Engineering

- **Dryer sizing:** Knowing $N_c$ (constant rate) and $D_{eff}$ (falling rate) lets you size tray area, residence time, and air flow.
- **Energy auditing:** Most plant energy goes to latent heat of evaporation → drives heat-recovery design.
- **Product quality:** Over-drying cracks the surface; under-drying invites mould. Kinetics predict the optimum stop-point.
- **Process control:** Real-time MR monitoring ties back to first-order model parameters for PLC feedback loops.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wet-Basis ↔ Dry-Basis Conversion

**Given:**
- $M_w = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}$
- $M_d = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}$

**Goal:** Express $M_d$ in terms of $M_w$.

**Step 1.** Start from the definition of $M_w$:

$$M_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}$$

**Step 2.** Divide numerator and denominator by $m_d$:

$$M_w = \frac{\dfrac{m_w}{m_d}}{\dfrac{m_w}{m_d} + 1}$$

**Step 3.** Recognise that $\dfrac{m_w}{m_d} = M_d$:

$$M_w = \frac{M_d}{M_d + 1}$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $M_d$:

$$M_w (M_d + 1) = M_d$$
$$M_w M_d + M_w = M_d$$
$$M_w = M_d - M_w M_d = M_d(1 - M_w)$$

$$\boxed{\,M_d = \frac{M_w}{1 - M_w}\,}$$

**Step 5.** Inverse — solve for $M_w$ in terms of $M_d$:

$$M_w (1 + M_d) = M_d \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\,M_w = \frac{M_d}{1 + M_d}\,}$$

**Sanity check (KTU favourite):** $M_w = 0.80$ (80% w.b.) → $M_d = 0.80 / 0.20 = 4.0$ kg/kg (400% d.b.). The d.b. value > 1 is perfectly valid — it simply means the material has 4 kg water per 1 kg solid.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drying Time in the Constant-Rate Period

**Given:** A material is drying at a constant rate $N_c$ (kg water / m²·s) from initial moisture $M_0$ to critical moisture $M_c$ (both d.b.), with dry mass per unit area $m_d / A$ (kg dry solid / m²).

**Step 1.** Instantaneous water mass per unit area is $w = m_d \cdot M / A$.

**Step 2.** Differentiate: $\dfrac{dw}{dt} = \dfrac{m_d}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{dt}$.

**Step 3.** Drying rate by definition: $N = -\dfrac{dw}{dt}$.

**Step 4.** Set $N = N_c$ (constant) and rearrange:

$$\frac{m_d}{A} \frac{dM}{dt} = -N_c \quad\Rightarrow\quad dM = -\frac{N_c A}{m_d}\, dt$$

**Step 5.** Integrate from $M_0$ to $M_c$ and $t = 0$ to $t = t_1$:

$$\int_{M_0}^{M_c} dM = -\frac{N_c A}{m_d} \int_0^{t_1} dt$$

$$M_c - M_0 = -\frac{N_c A}{m_d}\, t_1$$

**Step 6.** Solve for the time:

$$\boxed{\,t_1 = \frac{m_d}{N_c A} (M_0 - M_c)\,}$$

**Alternative form (mass basis, no area):** if the total dry mass in the dryer is $m_d$ (kg) and total water evaporated during constant-rate is $W$ (kg), then $N_c = W / (A \cdot t_1)$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Falling-Rate Drying (Linear Approximation)

In the **first falling rate period**, the rate is assumed proportional to the remaining free moisture:

$$N = N_c \cdot \frac{M - M^*}{M_c - M^*}$$

**Step 1.** Substitute into the rate equation:

$$-\frac{m_d}{A} \frac{dM}{dt} = N_c \cdot \frac{M - M^*}{M_c - M^*}$$

**Step 2.** Separate variables:

$$dM = -\frac{N_c A}{m_d} \cdot \frac{M - M^*}{M_c - M^*}\, dt$$

**Step 3.** Integrate from $M_c$ (at $t = t_1$) to $M$ (at $t = t_1 + t_2$):

$$\int_{M_c}^{M} \frac{dM}{M - M^*} = -\frac{N_c A}{m_d (M_c - M^*)} \int_{t_1}^{t_1 + t_2} dt$$

**Step 4.** Left side yields a logarithm:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M - M^*}{M_c - M^*}\right) = -\frac{N_c A}{m_d (M_c - M^*)}\, t_2$$

**Step 5.** Solve for $t_2$:

$$\boxed{\,t_2 = \frac{m_d (M_c - M^*)}{N_c A} \ln\!\left(\frac{M_c - M^*}{M - M^*}\right)\,}$$

**Total drying time:**

$$t_{total} = t_1 + t_2$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fickian Diffusion & Effective Diffusivity (Thin-Layer Slab)

In the falling-rate period, transport is governed by Fick's second law. For an infinite slab of half-thickness $L$ (full thickness $= 2L$), with uniform initial moisture and negligible surface resistance, the analytical solution (Crank, 1975) is:

$$MR = \frac{M - M^*}{M_0 - M^*} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\!\left[-(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{eff} t / 4 L^2 \right]$$

**Step 1.** For long drying times (large $t$), only the first term ($n = 0$) survives:

$$MR \approx \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Take natural log of both sides:

$$\ln(MR) = \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2}\, t$$

**Step 3.** Plot $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$. The slope gives the effective diffusivity:

$$\boxed{\,D_{eff} = -\frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2} \cdot \text{slope of } \ln(MR) \text{ vs. } t\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marker:** $D_{eff}$ for foods typically lies in the range $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-8}$ m²/s. Below $10^{-11}$ indicates case-hardening (surface crust).

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Newton (Lewis) Thin-Layer Model

**Assumption:** Drying rate is first-order with respect to remaining free moisture.

**Step 1.** Rate equation:

$$-\frac{dM}{dt} = k (M - M^*)$$

**Step 2.** Define $MR$:

$$MR = \frac{M - M^*}{M_0 - M^*} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; M - M^* = (M_0 - M^*)\, MR$$

**Step 3.** Differentiate: $dM = (M_0 - M^*)\, dMR$.

**Step 4.** Substitute and cancel $(M_0 - M^*)$:

$$-\frac{dMR}{dt} = k\, MR$$

**Step 5.** Separate and integrate (at $t = 0$, $MR = 1$):

$$\int_1^{MR} \frac{dMR}{MR} = -k \int_0^t dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln(MR) = -k t$$

$$\boxed{\,MR = e^{-k t}\,}$$

**Step 6.** Linearisation: $\ln(MR) = -k t$ — slope gives drying constant $k$ (per second).

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** The Newton model under-predicts drying time for many foods (e.g., fruits) because it assumes a single exponential decay. The **Page model** ($MR = \exp(-k t^n)$) introduces an empirical exponent $n$ that corrects the curvature.

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

> **Problem:** 100 kg of wet food contains 65 kg of water. Calculate (a) moisture content on wet basis, (b) moisture content on dry basis, (c) mass of water to be removed to reach 10% (w.b.) final moisture. [KTU 2024 Pattern, 5 marks]

**Solution:**

(a) Wet basis:

$$M_w = \frac{65}{100} = 0.65 = 65\% \text{ w.b.}$$

(b) Dry basis:

$$M_d = \frac{65}{100 - 65} = \frac{65}{35} = 1.857 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

(c) Bone-dry mass is constant: $m_d = 35$ kg.
Final wet-basis = 0.10, so:

$$M_{d,final} = \frac{0.10}{0.90} = 0.1111 \text{ kg/kg}$$

Final water mass: $m_{w,f} = 0.1111 \times 35 = 3.889$ kg.

**Water to remove:** $\Delta m_w = 65 - 3.889 = \boxed{61.11 \text{ kg}}$

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Thin-Layer Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix to pick the right model for a 14-mark KTU question.**

| Model | Equation | # of Constants | Best Suited For | Limitation |
|-------|----------|----------------|------------------|------------|
| Newton (Lewis) | $MR = e^{-kt}$ | 1 | Initial stages, low-MR range | Linear on semi-log |
| Page | $MR = e^{-kt^n}$ | 2 | Most fruits, grains, vegetables | Empirical |
| Henderson–Pabis | $MR = a\,e^{-kt}$ | 2 | Slight improvement on Newton | $a$ not always 1 |
| Logarithmic | $MR = a\,e^{-kt} + c$ | 3 | Foods with long tail | Three-parameter fit |
| Two-term | $MR = a\,e^{-k_1 t} + b\,e^{-k_2 t}$ | 4 | Bimodal drying (e.g., porous + bound) | Over-parameterised |
| Midilli–Kucuk | $MR = a\,e^{-kt^n} + bt$ | 4 | Best statistical fit in many studies | Hard to interpret physically |
| Verma et al. | $MR = a\,e^{-kt} + (1-a)\,e^{-gt}$ | 3 | Combined transport regimes | Needs both constants |

**Goodness-of-fit metrics KTU expects you to mention:**
- $R^2$ (coefficient of determination) — closer to 1 = better.
- $\chi^2$ (reduced chi-square) — closer to 0 = better.
- RMSE (root mean square error) — closer to 0 = better.
- **MBE** (mean bias error) — closer to 0 = better.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the following with one example each:**
(i) Free moisture content
(ii) Equilibrium moisture content
(iii) Bound moisture content

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Free moisture content:** The amount of water in a food material *in excess of* its equilibrium moisture content at given drying conditions. It is the moisture that *can* be removed by the prevailing air. *Example:* The water removable from wet rice at 60 °C, 30 % RH air.
- **(ii) Equilibrium moisture content (EMC, M\*):** The moisture content at which the food material neither gains nor loses moisture to the surrounding air — i.e., the partial pressure of water at the solid surface equals the partial pressure in the air. *Example:* Raisins at 25 °C, 60 % RH have EMC ≈ 0.20 kg/kg d.b.
- **(iii) Bound moisture content:** The moisture held by the solid through physico-chemical forces (capillarity, adsorption, solution) such that its vapour pressure is *less* than that of pure water at the same temperature. *Example:* Water of hydration in starch or protein matrices.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between constant rate drying period and falling rate drying period in food materials.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Constant Rate Period | Falling Rate Period |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Rate-controlling step | External mass/heat transfer | Internal moisture diffusion |
| Surface condition | Fully wetted (saturated) | Partially or fully dry |
| Product temperature | Equal to wet-bulb temperature | Rises above wet-bulb toward air temperature |
| Mechanism | Surface evaporation, like free water | Moisture migrates from interior to surface |
| Duration | Short for thick materials | Dominates total drying time |
| Mathematical model | $N = N_c = \text{const}$ | $N = f(M)$ — e.g., linear in $M - M^*$ |
| Controlling variables | Air velocity, T, RH, exposed area | $D_{eff}$, thickness, structure |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice)

> **ESE Pattern Note (KTU 2024):** Each Part B question is 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer ONE of the two choices.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** A wet food material contains 80 kg water and 20 kg bone-dry solids. It is to be dried to a final moisture content of 5 % (w.b.) in a tray dryer. Calculate:
(i) Initial moisture content on dry basis.
(ii) Final moisture content on dry basis.
(iii) Mass of water to be removed per kg of initial product.
(iv) Mass of product obtained at the end of drying.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the drying time in the **linear falling rate period** assuming the rate is directly proportional to the moisture content above the equilibrium value. State clearly all assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**
(i) Initial MC (d.b.):

$$M_{d,0} = \frac{80}{20} = 4.0 \text{ kg/kg d.b.}$$

(ii) Final MC (d.b.):

$$M_{d,f} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg d.b.}$$

(iii) Initial wet mass = 100 kg. Final wet mass = $m_d(1 + M_{d,f}) = 20 \times 1.0526 = 21.05$ kg.

Water removed = $100 - 21.05 = 78.95$ kg.
Per kg of initial product: $0.7895$ kg water.

(iv) Final product mass = **21.05 kg**.

**(b) Derivation of linear falling-rate drying time:**

**Assumption:** $N = k (M - M^*)$ where $k = \dfrac{N_c}{M_c - M^*}$ (proportionality from continuity at point C).

**Step 1.** Rate equation:

$$-\frac{m_d}{A} \frac{dM}{dt} = k (M - M^*)$$

**Step 2.** Separate:

$$-\frac{dM}{M - M^*} = \frac{k A}{m_d} dt$$

**Step 3.** Integrate from $t_1$ (where $M = M_c$) to $t = t_1 + t_2$ (where $M = M$):

$$-\int_{M_c}^{M} \frac{dM}{M - M^*} = \frac{k A}{m_d} \int_{t_1}^{t_1 + t_2} dt$$

$$-\ln\!\left(\frac{M - M^*}{M_c - M^*}\right) = \frac{k A}{m_d} t_2$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $k = N_c / (M_c - M^*)$:

$$\boxed{\,t_2 = \frac{m_d (M_c - M^*)}{N_c A}\, \ln\!\left(\frac{M_c - M^*}{M - M^*}\right)\,}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Drying is uniform across the tray area.
2. No heat losses to surroundings.
3. Material is hygroscopic and obeys linear falling rate.
4. EMC is constant (steady air conditions).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **constant rate and falling rate drying periods** in a typical food drying curve. Indicate the critical moisture content and equilibrium moisture content on the curve.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a food product drying in a thin layer, the following data are recorded:

| Time (min) | 0 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 |
|------------|---|----|----|----|-----|-----|-----|
| MR | 1.000 | 0.812 | 0.660 | 0.535 | 0.435 | 0.353 | 0.286 |

Fit a **Newton (Lewis) model** $MR = e^{-kt}$ and determine the drying constant $k$ in min⁻¹. Comment on the goodness of fit.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Diagram (use Figure 4.2 from Section 4 above). Labelled points A, B (start of constant rate), C (critical point $M_c$), D (end of first falling rate), E (end of second falling rate), F ($M = M^*$, drying stops).

**Description (7 marks breakdown):**
- *Initial adjustment (A→B):* 1 mark — product warms up.
- *Constant rate (B→C):* 2 marks — surface saturated, $N$ constant, governed by external conditions.
- *Critical point (C):* 1 mark — first dry spot appears, $M = M_c$.
- *First falling rate (C→D):* 1.5 marks — wetted area shrinks, internal diffusion.
- *Second falling rate (D→E):* 1 mark — surface fully dry, $D_{eff}$ controls.
- *Equilibrium (E→F):* 0.5 mark — $M \to M^*$, drying stops.

**(b) Newton model fitting:**

Take $\ln(MR)$ for each point:

| $t$ (min) | MR | $\ln(MR)$ |
|-----------|-----|-----------|
| 0 | 1.000 | 0.000 |
| 30 | 0.812 | −0.2083 |
| 60 | 0.660 | −0.4155 |
| 90 | 0.535 | −0.6259 |
| 120 | 0.435 | −0.8329 |
| 150 | 0.353 | −1.0406 |
| 180 | 0.286 | −1.2518 |

**Linear regression** of $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$:

- Slope $\approx -0.00695$ min⁻¹
- Intercept $\approx -0.0002$ ≈ 0

**Drying constant:**

$$\boxed{\,k = 0.00695 \text{ min}^{-1} \approx 6.95 \times 10^{-3} \text{ min}^{-1}\,}$$

**Comment on fit:**
- $R^2$ from regression ≈ 0.9999 → excellent linear fit.
- The Newton model is adequate for this product in this MR range.
- For higher accuracy over the entire range, a **Page model** with exponent $n \ne 1$ may be tried.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this module — common pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert basis** — quoting 65 % as "dry basis" when it was given on wet basis. Always state the basis and convert explicitly.
> 2. **Sign error in drying rate** — the rate of moisture *loss* is positive; the rate of *change* of MC is negative. Use $N = -\dfrac{m_d}{A}\dfrac{dM}{dt}$.
> 3. **Mixing % and decimal** — 0.65 vs 65 %. Be consistent. If the formula expects decimal, use decimal.
> 4. **Ignoring EMC** — at high RH, $M^*$ is not negligible. Setting $MR = M/M_0$ without justification loses a mark.
> 5. **Choosing the wrong model** — fitting Newton and concluding "drying is first-order" without checking Page or Henderson–Pabis. Always justify model choice with $R^2$ and RMSE.
> 6. **Slab vs. cylinder vs. sphere** — Crank's solution depends on geometry. State it explicitly.
> 7. **No units in final answer** — KTU deducts ½ mark per numerical question lacking SI units.
> 8. **Mis-spelling and wrong symbol** — "HenderSon-Paibis" — autocorrect trap. Use exact symbols: $D_{eff}$, $M^*$, $M_c$, $N_c$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before the exam — 60-second sweep.**

- [x] **Moisture content** is reported in **% w.b.** (per wet mass) or **% d.b.** (per dry solid). **Dry basis** is the engineer's default.
- [x] Conversion identities: $M_d = M_w / (1 - M_w)$ and $M_w = M_d / (1 + M_d)$.
- [x] **EMC ($M^*$)** is a property of the **air–solid** system — depends on RH and T of air, not the dryer size.
- [x] **Free moisture** = $M - M^*$; **Bound moisture** = portion with $M < M^*$.
- [x] **Critical moisture content ($M_c$)** is the pivot between constant-rate and falling-rate periods.
- [x] **Drying curve** = M vs. t; **Drying rate curve** = N vs. M.
- [x] **Constant rate period** → controlled by *external* conditions (air velocity, T, RH).
- [x] **Falling rate period** → controlled by *internal* diffusion ($D_{eff}$).
- [x] **Drying time formulas**:
  - Constant rate: $t_1 = \dfrac{m_d}{N_c A}(M_0 - M_c)$
  - Linear falling rate: $t_2 = \dfrac{m_d (M_c - M^*)}{N_c A}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{M_c - M^*}{M - M^*}\right)$
- [x] **Moisture ratio:** $MR = (M - M^*)/(M_0 - M^*)$.
- [x] **Thin-layer models** (must be able to write & linearise):
  - Newton: $MR = e^{-kt}$ → $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ linear
  - Page: $MR = e^{-kt^n}$ → $\ln[-\ln(MR)]$ vs. $\ln(t)$ linear
  - Henderson–Pabis: $MR = a\,e^{-kt}$
  - Logarithmic: $MR = a\,e^{-kt} + c$
- [x] **Fick's second law** for slab (long times): $D_{eff} = -\dfrac{4L^2}{\pi^2}\cdot \text{slope of } \ln(MR)$ vs. $t$.
- [x] **Effective diffusivity** for foods: $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-8}$ m²/s.
- [x] **Goodness of fit** parameters: $R^2$, $\chi^2$, RMSE, MBE.
- [x] **EMC isotherms**: Henderson, Chung–Pfost, BET, **GAB** (most common for foods).
- [x] **Latent heat** of water vaporisation ≈ 2260 kJ/kg at 100 °C.
- [x] **Psychrometric basics:** $W = 0.622\,p_v / (p - p_v)$; drying proceeds as long as $W_{air} < W_{sat}(T_{wb})$.
- [x] Always mention **assumptions** (isothermal, uniform thickness, negligible shrinkage) in derivations.
- [x] Always carry **units** through the calculation — partial credit evaporates without them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freeze Drying: Principles and Stages of Freeze Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Freeze Drying: Principles and Stages of Freeze Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Freeze Drying** (scientifically termed **Lyophilization** or **Cryodesiccation**) is an advanced, low-temperature dehydration process in which water is removed from a frozen food (or biological) matrix by **sublimation** — the direct phase transition of water from the **solid state (ice)** to the **vapor state (water vapor)** — under carefully controlled conditions of low pressure (high vacuum) and low temperature, completely bypassing the **liquid phase**.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme definition (aligned with the OEFTT616 – Thermal Processing of Food syllabus):
> *"Freeze drying is a unit operation in which the moisture content of a heat-sensitive food product is reduced by freezing the water and subsequently subliming the ice under high vacuum, preserving the structural, nutritional, and sensorial integrity of the product."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
