# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Freezing Point of Food" and explain why it is always below 0 °C.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Freezing Point of Food** is the temperature at which the free water present in the food begins to undergo phase transition from liquid to solid, initiating ice crystal formation (1 mark).

It is **always below 0 °C** because food contains dissolved solutes such as salts, sugars, organic acids, and other colloidal substances (1 mark). These solutes cause **freezing point depression** (colligative property), described by the equation:
$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$$
For example, beef freezes at **−1.7 °C**, milk at **−0.55 °C**, and orange juice at **−1.3 °C** (1 mark).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between "Free water" and "Bound water" in foods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Free Water | Bound Water |
|---|---|---|
| **Binding** | Loosely held, easily removed | Tightly bound to proteins/carbohydrates |
| **Quantity** | 85–90% of total water | 10–15% of total water |
| **Freezability** | Freezes at 0 to −5 °C | Does not freeze even at −40 °C |
| **Role** | Available for reactions, microbial growth | Unavailable, structural |
| **Example** | Juices of fruits, meat exudate | Hydration shell of proteins |

Bound water cannot form ice crystals because the binding energy exceeds the energy of the water-water hydrogen bonds (1 mark each point, complete table = 3 marks).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **freezing curve** of a food product, explaining each zone. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fish fillet of thickness **5 cm**, density **1080 kg/m³**, freezing point **−1.5 °C**, and latent heat **240 kJ/kg** is to be frozen in an air blast freezer at **−35 °C** with $\theta = 25$ W/m²·K. The thermal conductivity of frozen fish is **1.8 W/m·K**. Estimate the freezing time using **Plank's equation** (assume infinite slab geometry). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q5A(a) — Freezing Curve (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A freezing curve is a plot of temperature at the thermal center of a food product versus time, depicting the various stages of heat removal during the freezing process.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
The curve shows 5 zones — refer to the freezing curve diagram in Section 4.1.

**Zone Description (4 marks):**

1. **Zone A — Pre-cooling (1 mark):** Food cools from initial temperature (T₀) to freezing point (T_f) by removal of **sensible heat**. Duration is short; food matrix is cooling.

2. **Zone B — Supercooling (1 mark):** Temperature drops 2–5 °C below T_f momentarily due to lack of nucleation sites. No ice is formed yet; system is metastable.

3. **Zone C — Plateau / Phase Change (1 mark):** Temperature remains constant as ~80% of water converts to ice. Latent heat (334 kJ/kg) is removed. This is the **longest zone** and represents the bulk of freezing time.

4. **Zone D — Sub-cooling (1 mark):** Remaining freezable water (in concentrated solution) freezes. Temperature drops from T_f to final storage T_s. Sensible heat of ice and concentrated solution is removed.

#### Model Solution for Q5A(b) — Plank's Equation Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
- $\rho = 1080$ kg/m³
- $L_f = 240{,}000$ J/kg
- $T_f = -1.5\,°C$
- $T_a = -35\,°C$
- $\theta = 25$ W/m²·K
- $k = 1.8$ W/m·K
- Infinite slab: $P = 1/2$, $R = 1/8$

**Step 1: Plank's equation (1 mark)**
$$t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\theta(T_f - T_a)} \left[ \frac{PD}{v} + \frac{RD^2}{4k} \right]$$

**Step 2: Surface term (1 mark)**
$$\frac{PD}{v} = 1 \text{ (for infinite slab double-sided cooling)}$$

**Step 3: Internal term (1 mark)**
$$\frac{RD^2}{4k} = \frac{(1/8)(0.05)^2}{4(1.8)} = \frac{0.0003125}{7.2} = 4.34 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 4: Temperature driving force (1 mark)**
$$T_f - T_a = -1.5 - (-35) = 33.5 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 5: Numerator of prefactor (1 mark)**
$$\rho L_f = 1080 \times 240{,}000 = 2.592 \times 10^8 \, \text{J/m}^3$$

**Step 6: Denominator and bracket (1 mark)**
$$\theta(T_f - T_a) \times [\ldots] = 25 \times 33.5 \times [1 + 4.34 \times 10^{-5}] \approx 837.5 \times 1.00004 \approx 837.53$$

**Step 7: Final calculation (1 mark)**
$$t_f = \frac{2.592 \times 10^8}{837.53} = 309{,}498 \, \text{s}$$
$$t_f = \frac{309{,}498}{3600} = 85.97 \, \text{hours} \approx 86 \, \text{hours}$$

**Answer: $t_f \approx 86$ hours ($\approx 3.6$ days)**

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **principles of thawing** and discuss the various methods of thawing with their relative merits and demerits. (7 marks)

**(b)** A frozen meat product is stored at **−20 °C** with initial freezing point **−2 °C**. Calculate the **fraction of water frozen** at the storage temperature. Take specific heat of water as 4.18 kJ/kg·K and latent heat of fusion as 334 kJ/kg. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q5B(a) — Thawing Principles (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Thawing is the reverse of freezing — the controlled re-conversion of ice crystals in a frozen food to liquid water by supplying heat.

**Principles (3 marks):**
1. **Heat must be supplied** to the frozen product to overcome both sensible and latent heat.
2. The process is **thermodynamically asymmetric** to freezing (ice has lower thermal conductivity, ~2.2 W/m·K vs water at 0.6 W/m·K — note: ice conducts better, but surface melt-water film insulates).
3. **Drip loss** is the main quality concern — cell walls ruptured during freezing release intracellular water.
4. **Microbial reactivation** is critical — surface layer enters the danger zone (5–60 °C) before core thaws.
5. **Recrystallization** of small ice crystals into large ones occurs during temperature fluctuations in storage.

**Methods (3 marks):**

| Method | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|
| Air (still) | Simple, low cost | Slow, uneven |
| Air (forced) | Faster, uniform | Drying of surface |
| Water immersion | Very fast, uniform | Requires waterproof pkg |
| Vacuum | Fastest, no oxidation | High capital cost |
| Microwave | Very fast | Edge overheating, non-uniform |
| RF/Dielectric | Uniform, fast | Expensive, complex |

**Recommended**: Forced air or vacuum for industrial use; **never use room-temperature thawing for meat/fish**.

#### Model Solution for Q5B(b) — Ice Fraction (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $T_f = -2\,°C$
- $T_s = -20\,°C$
- $c_w = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K
- $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg

**Step 1: Formula (1 mark)**
$$X_{ice} = \frac{c_w (T_f - T_s)}{L_f}$$

**Step 2: Temperature difference (1 mark)**
$$T_f - T_s = -2 - (-20) = 18\,°C = 18\,K$$

**Step 3: Numerator (1 mark)**
$$c_w (T_f - T_s) = 4.18 \times 18 = 75.24 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Denominator (1 mark)**
$$L_f = 334 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5: Compute ratio (1 mark)**
$$X_{ice} = \frac{75.24}{334} = 0.2253$$

**Step 6: Convert to percent (1 mark)**
$$X_{ice} = 22.53\%$$

**Step 7: Interpretation (1 mark)**
**Only 22.53% of the total water content is in the form of ice** at −20 °C storage. The remaining 77.47% is liquid (concentrated solution + bound water), which means **microorganisms can still survive** in the unfrozen fraction. This is why **storage temperature must be as low as practical** (e.g., −30 °C for long-term storage of sensitive products).

**Answer: $X_{ice} \approx 0.225$ or 22.5%**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Sign errors in temperature differences**: Always compute $T_f - T_a$ carefully; both are typically negative, and the difference is positive.
> 2. **Unit consistency**: Plank's equation requires SI units (W, J, m, K). Convert all quantities BEFORE substituting.
> 3. **Geometry selection**: Forgetting to apply the right Plank's constants ($P$ and $R$) for the given shape is the most common mistake — lose **2–3 marks instantly**.
> 4. **Ice fraction confusion**: Students often confuse $X_{ice}$ (fraction of water frozen) with the fraction of total mass that is ice. $X_{ice}$ is based on **water weight only**.
> 5. **Thawing vs Freezing reversibility**: Don't write that thawing is a perfect reverse of freezing — state the asymmetries (drip loss, recrystallization, microbial risk).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### 🧊 Key Concepts to Remember

- **Freezing Point Depression**: $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ — explains why food freezes below 0 °C.
- **Three Types of Water**: Free (freezable), Bound (non-freezable), Capillary (partially).
- **Five-Zone Freezing Curve**: A-Pre-cooling → B-Supercooling → C-Plateau → D-Sub-cooling → E-Storage.
- **Zone C is the longest** — dominated by latent heat removal (≈ 80% of water freezes here).
- **Fraction of Ice Formed**: $X_{ice} = c_w (T_f - T_s) / L_f \approx 0.0125 (T_f - T_s)$.
- **Plank's Equation**: $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L_f}{\theta (T_f - T_a)} \left[ \dfrac{PD}{v} + \dfrac{RD^2}{4k} \right]$.
- **Plank's Constants**: Slab (1/2, 1/8); Cylinder (1/4, 1/16); Sphere (1/6, 1/24).
- **Freezing time $\propto D^2$** — halving thickness reduces time by 4×.
- **Fast freezing = small intracellular crystals = minimal drip loss**.
- **Slow freezing = large extracellular crystals = severe cell damage**.

### 🔬 Glass Transition ($T_g$)

- Below $T_g$: molecular mobility → 0; **maximal stability**.
- Storage temp should be **well below $T_g'$** (glass transition of maximally freeze-concentrated solution, typically **−32 to −42 °C** for foods).
- $T_g$ decreases with increasing water content (plasticization).

### ❄️ Thawing Quick-Facts

- Thawing is **slower** than freezing due to lower thermal conductivity of ice.
- **Vacuum thawing** is the fastest industrial method.
- **Drip loss** is the chief quality defect on thawing.
- **Recrystallization** is a serious issue in fluctuating cold-chain storage.
- **Never** thaw meat/fish at room temperature (microbial danger zone).

### 📋 Important Numerical Constants (MEMORIZE)

| Constant | Value |
|---|---|
| Latent heat of fusion ($L_f$ for water) | **334 kJ/kg** (80 kcal/kg) |
| Specific heat of water | **4.18 kJ/kg·K** |
| Specific heat of ice | **2.1 kJ/kg·K** |
| Cryoscopic constant ($K_f$ for water) | **1.86 °C·kg/mol** |
| Freezing point of pure water | **0 °C** |
| Standard cold storage | **−18 °C** |
| Thermal conductivity of ice | **~2.2 W/m·K** |
| Thermal conductivity of water | **~0.6 W/m·K** |

### ⚡ KTU 2024 High-Yield Keywords

- **Plank's equation, Zone of Maximum Ice Formation, Glass Transition Temperature, Freezing Point Depression, Bound vs Free Water, Drip Loss, Recrystallization, Supercooling, Latent Heat, Plank's Constants, Fast vs Slow Freezing, Vacuum Thawing, Eutectic Point, Freeze-Concentration, Water Activity ($a_w$).**

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip**: For numerical problems, always draw a **schematic**, **list givens**, **state the formula**, **show unit conversions**, then substitute. KTU examiners award **step-marks** generously if your approach is correct even if the final number has a minor arithmetic slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freezing Rate and Its Impact on Food Quality
Types of Freezers: Blast, Plate, and Cryogenic Freezers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Freezers: Blast, Plate, and Cryogenic Freezers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Freezing** is a preservation method in which the temperature of food is reduced below its freezing point (typically below **−18 °C / 0 °F**), converting most of the water content into ice crystals and thereby arresting microbial, enzymatic, and chemical deterioration. A **freezer** is an engineered thermal processing unit designed to extract sensible and latent heat from a food product at a controlled rate using a deliberately chosen heat-transfer medium.

The three principal industrial freezer classes mandated in the KTU 2024 Food Technology syllabus are:

| Freezer Class | Heat Transfer Medium | Typical Operating Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Blast Freezer** | Cold air (forced convection) | **−35 °C to −40 °C** |
| **Plate Freezer** | Refrigerated metal plates (conduction) | **−30 °C to −40 °C** |
| **Cryogenic Freezer** | Liquid N₂ or Liquid CO₂ | **−60 °C to −196 °C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The choice of freezer dictates the *rate of ice nucleation* and *ice crystal size* — two parameters that directly control drip loss, texture degradation, and rehydration capacity upon thawing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine three ways to chill a hot cup of tea rapidly:

- **Blast Freezer** = Pointing a **high-velocity desk fan blowing ice-cold air** at the cup. The cold air scrubs heat from the surface. Slow at the core, but very gentle on the product.
- **Plate Freezer** = **Squeezing the cup between two frozen metal ice-trays**. Direct contact → super-fast heat conduction through the metal. Best for thin, uniformly shaped products.
- **Cryogenic Freezer** = **Dunking the cup into liquid nitrogen (–196 °C)**. The temperature gradient is enormous, so heat exits almost explosively. Ultra-fast, ultra-expensive, ultra-delicate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember**
> - Latent heat of fusion of water: **λfus ≈ 334 kJ/kg** (or 80 kcal/kg)
> - Specific heat of ice: **c_ice ≈ 2.1 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Specific heat of water: **c_w ≈ 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Boiling point of liquid N₂ at 1 atm: **−195.79 °C**
> - Sublimation point of solid CO₂ at 1 atm: **−78.5 °C**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

### A. Blast Freezer (Air-Blast)
1. **Refrigeration circuit** compresses and expands a refrigerant (NH₃ or HFC) inside **evaporator coils** lining the freezer walls.
2. A **high-velocity fan (typically 2–6 m/s)** circulates the chilled air across the product stack.
3. Heat is removed primarily by **forced convection** governed by Newton's Law of Cooling:
$$q'' = h_c \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$
where `h_c` is the convective heat-transfer coefficient (typically **10–30 W/(m²·K)** for air blast).
4. Variants: **Tunnel**, **Spiral belt**, **Fluidised bed** (for small particulates), and **Cabinet batch** types.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why air?** Air is cheap, non-toxic, non-corrosive, and food-safe — but it has a *low thermal conductivity* (≈ 0.025 W/m·K), so freezing is comparatively slow.

### B. Plate Freezer (Contact / Conduction)
1. Food is placed between **hollow, refrigerated metal plates** (aluminium or stainless steel).
2. Refrigerant or a secondary coolant (e.g., **calcium chloride brine, ethylene glycol**) circulates inside the plates.
3. Heat flows by **conduction** through the plate–product interface (Fourier's Law).
4. Variants: **Horizontal plate** (for blocks of fish/meat) and **Vertical plate** (for free-flowing products like peas, berries).
5. Contact pressure (typically **0.5–2 kPa**) is applied to minimise the air-gap resistance at the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why plates?** Contact freezing gives `h_c,eff` values of **500–1500 W/(m²·K)** — an order of magnitude higher than air-blast — yielding much faster freezing for thin geometries.

### C. Cryogenic Freezer
1. **Liquid nitrogen (LN₂)** or **liquid CO₂** is sprayed or dripped directly onto the product inside an insulated tunnel.
2. The cryogen **boils at the product surface**, absorbing its **latent heat of vaporisation** (LN₂: 199 J/g; CO₂: 571 J/g at sublimation).
3. The cold vapour continues to **pre-chill incoming product** → cascading heat recovery.
4. Freezing time can be **seconds to a few minutes** (vs. hours in air-blast).

> [!WARNING]
> Cryogenic freezing must be **tuned**: excessive speed causes *thermal shock cracking* (e.g., on strawberries, fish fillets). Operators must match product residence time to the cryogen flow rate.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = m \, c \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat removal | J, kJ |
| 2 | $Q_{lat} = m \, \lambda_{fus}$ | Latent heat removal (water→ice) | J, kJ |
| 3 | $t_f = \dfrac{\lambda_{fus} \, \rho \, L}{T_f - T_a}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{h_c} + \dfrac{R \, L}{k}\right)$ | **Plank's Equation** (freezing time for a slab) | seconds |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm}$ | Overall heat transfer (log-mean ΔT) | W |
| 5 | $Nu = \dfrac{h_c \, L}{k} = f(Re, Pr)$ | Nusselt number (forced convection) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (air velocity) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu \, c_p}{k}$ | Prandtl number (air ≈ 0.71) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{LN_2} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{h_{fg,N_2}}$ | Required cryogen mass flow | kg/s |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\delta_{plate}}{k_{plate}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Total thermal resistance (plate freezer) | m²·K/W |

**Where Plank's constants are:**
- `P` and `R` are geometric constants of Plank's equation (depend on product shape: slab = 1/2, cylinder = 1/4, sphere = 1/6)
- `L` = characteristic thickness (m); `ρ` = density (kg/m³); `k` = thermal conductivity of frozen food (≈ 1.5 W/m·K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Plank's equation (1913) is the *single most-tested formula* in KTU food engineering papers. Memorise the structure, not just the symbols — examiners often re-write the equation with renamed variables.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Plank's Equation for Freezing Time of a Slab

We derive the freezing time of a finite slab of thickness `2L` initially at temperature `T_i`, exposed on both faces to an ambient at `T_a` (with `T_a < T_f` = freezing point). The frozen layer grows inward from both faces.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the freezing front**

Let `x` be the half-thickness of the *unfrozen* core at time `t`. The latent heat released when the front advances by `dx` must equal the heat conducted away through the frozen crust:

$$m \, \lambda_{fus} = \rho \, A \, \lambda_{fus} \, dx = \frac{\lambda_{fus} \, \rho \, A \, (L - x)}{1/h_c} \, (T_f - T_a) \, d\!t$$

We approximate the **temperature distribution in the frozen layer as linear** (steady-state quasi-plug assumption), giving the conductive resistance:

**Step 2 — Thermal resistance of the frozen layer**

By Fourier's law through the crust of instantaneous thickness `(L − x)`:

$$q = \frac{k \, A \, (T_f - T_s)}{L - x} = \frac{A \, (T_f - T_a)}{\dfrac{1}{h_c} + \dfrac{L - x}{k}}$$

**Step 3 — Rate of latent-heat release at the front**

Differentiating: the mass frozen per unit time is `ρ A (−dx/dt)`. The energy balance is:

$$\rho \, \lambda_{fus} \, A \, \left(-\frac{dx}{d\!t}\right) = \frac{A \, (T_f - T_a)}{\dfrac{1}{h_c} + \dfrac{L - x}{k}}$$

**Step 4 — Variable separation**

$$-\rho \, \lambda_{fus} \left(\frac{1}{h_c} + \frac{L - x}{k}\right) d\!x = (T_f - T_a) \, d\!t$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from x = L (start, nothing frozen) to x = 0 (fully frozen)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{L}^{0} -\rho \, \lambda_{fus} \left(\frac{1}{h_c} + \frac{L - x}{k}\right) d\!x &= \int_{0}^{t_f} (T_f - T_a) \, d\!t \\
\rho \, \lambda_{fus} \int_{0}^{L} \left(\frac{1}{h_c} + \frac{L - x}{k}\right) d\!x &= (T_f - T_a) \, t_f \\
\rho \, \lambda_{fus} \left[\frac{L}{h_c} + \frac{1}{k}\!\left(Lx - \frac{x^2}{2}\right)\bigg|_{0}^{L}\right] &= (T_f - T_a) \, t_f \\
\rho \, \lambda_{fus} \left[\frac{L}{h_c} + \frac{L^2}{2k}\right] &= (T_f - T_a) \, t_f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Plank's Equation for a Slab (both sides cooled)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{\rho \, \lambda_{fus}}{(T_f - T_a)} \left(\frac{L}{h_c} + \frac{L^2}{2k}\right) \\
&= \frac{\lambda_{fus} \, \rho \, L}{(T_f - T_a)} \left(\frac{1}{h_c} + \frac{L}{2k}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Plank's equation** with `P = 1/2` and `R = 1/2`, exactly as listed in the formula sheet.

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

> A 4 cm thick fish fillet (initial temp 10 °C, freezing point −2 °C, λfus = 235 kJ/kg, ρ = 1050 kg/m³, k_frozen = 1.6 W/m·K) is frozen in a blast freezer with air at −30 °C, `h_c = 20 W/(m²·K)`. Estimate the freezing time. Take `L = 0.02 m`, `(T_f − T_a) = (−2 − (−30)) = 28 K`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{(235 \times 10^3)(1050)(0.02)}{28} \left(\frac{1}{20} + \frac{0.02}{2 \times 1.6}\right) \\
&= \frac{4.935 \times 10^6}{28} \left(0.0500 + 0.00625\right) \\
&= 176{,}250 \;\times \; 0.05625 \\
&\approx 9{,}914 \;\text{seconds} \\
&\approx 2.75 \;\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** An equivalent plate freezer with `h_c,eff = 1000 W/(m²·K)` would give `t_f ≈ 47 minutes` — a 3.5× speed-up. This is *exactly* why plate freezers dominate fish and meat industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Air-Blast (Tunnel) Freezer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Product Inlet] --> A2[Conveyor Belt]
    A2 --> A3[Zone 1 Pre-cool -10 C]
    A3 --> A4[Zone 2 Surface Freeze -25 C]
    A4 --> A5[Zone 3 Through Freeze -35 C]
    A5 --> A6[Zone 4 Equilibration -25 C]
    A6 --> A7[Product Outlet Packaged]
    F1[Axial Fan Bank] -->|Cold Air -35 C| A3
    F1 --> A4
    F1 --> A5
    F1 --> A6
    E1[Evaporator Coils NH3] --> F1
    COMP[Compressor + Condenser] --> E1
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Horizontal Plate Freezer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Refrigerant In -35 C] --> P2[Top Plate Hollow]
    P2 --> P3[Product Layer 2 cm]
    P3 --> P4[Middle Plate Hollow]
    P4 --> P5[Product Layer 2 cm]
    P5 --> P6[Bottom Plate Hollow]
    P6 --> P7[Refrigerant Out -30 C]
    H1[Hydraulic Ram 0.5-2 kPa] --> P3
    H1 --> P5
    P3 --> FT1[Frozen Block Ejected]
    P5 --> FT1
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Cryogenic (LN₂) Tunnel Freezer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Product Inlet] --> C2[Conveyor Mesh Belt]
    C2 --> C3[LN2 Spray Header -196 C]
    C3 --> C4[Cold N2 Gas Recirculation -50 C]
    C4 --> C5[Insulated Tunnel Exit]
    C5 --> C6[Product Outlet]
    LN2[Liquid N2 Storage Tank] -->|Pressurised 3 bar| C3
    C4 --> EXH[Exhaust Vent to Atmosphere]
    VENT[Pressure Relief Valve] --> C4
    C3 --> C2
```

## 4.4 Comparative Decision Matrix

| Parameter | Blast Freezer | Plate Freezer | Cryogenic Freezer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat-transfer mechanism** | Forced convection (air) | Conduction (contact) | Boiling + convection |
| **Surface `h_c` (W/m²·K)** | **10–30** | **500–1500** | **500–2000** |
| **Freezing speed** | Slow (hours) | Fast (min–h) | Ultra-fast (sec–min) |
| **Capital cost** | Low–Medium | High | Medium (LN₂ cost dominates) |
| **Operating cost** | Low | Low | **High** (cryogen recurring) |
| **Best suited for** | Irregular shapes, large pieces | Uniform blocks, fish, patties | Premium berries, IQF items |
| **Examples in KTU syllabus** | Chicken carcass, peas | Fish blocks, meat patties | Strawberries, shrimps, herbs |
| **Texture damage** | Moderate | Low | Minimal if tuned |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between blast freezing and cryogenic freezing with respect to heat-transfer mechanism and product suitability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Heat transfer:* Blast freezing uses **forced convection with cold air** (h ≈ 10–30 W/m²·K), while cryogenic freezing uses **boiling of liquid N₂ or sublimation of solid CO₂** at the product surface, achieving h ≈ 500–2000 W/m²·K. **(1.5 marks)**
- *Product suitability:* Blast freezers suit **large, irregularly shaped, bulk products** (carcasses, fish, vegetables in cartons) where slow, uniform freezing is acceptable. Cryogenic freezers suit **delicate, high-value, small, or individually quick-frozen (IQF) products** (berries, herbs, shrimps) where rapid freezing preserves cellular integrity. **(1.5 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages of plate freezers over air-blast freezers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher heat-transfer coefficient** (500–1500 vs 10–30 W/m²·K) → faster freezing. **(1 mark)**
2. **Lower energy consumption per kg frozen** because there is no fan power and no heat carried away by exhaust air. **(1 mark)**
3. **Smaller floor space and better hygiene** (closed system, no airborne cross-contamination). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Note for Students:** In KTU 2024 ESE, every Module 3 question carries the choice pattern *(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks*. We provide TWO full alternative questions for parallel practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a cryogenic (liquid nitrogen) tunnel freezer**. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** for IQF fruits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6 cm thick meat block (`ρ = 1050 kg/m³`, `λfus = 235 kJ/kg`, `k_frozen = 1.4 W/m·K`, `T_f = −2 °C`, initial temperature `+5 °C`) is to be frozen in a **plate freezer** with an effective surface heat-transfer coefficient `h_c = 800 W/(m²·K)` and refrigerant temperature `−35 °C`. Using **Plank's equation for a slab**, calculate the **time required for complete freezing**. Compare this with the time that would be needed in a blast freezer with `h_c = 25 W/(m²·K)`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Construction (3 marks):** A cryogenic tunnel freezer consists of:
   - An **insulated stainless-steel tunnel** (typically 4–12 m long).
   - A **perforated LN₂ spray header** at the inlet zone, fed from a vacuum-insulated cryogenic storage tank.
   - A **mesh or flighted conveyor belt** carrying product through the tunnel.
   - A **recirculating cold-N₂ gas curtain** in the middle and exit zones.
   - A **pressure-relief vent** and **oxygen-deficiency alarm** for safety.
   - An optional **exhaust heat exchanger** that recovers cold N₂ vapour to pre-chill incoming product.

2. **Working (2 marks):** Liquid N₂ at −196 °C is sprayed onto the product. The N₂ **boils on contact** (latent heat 199 J/g), removing the product's sensible + latent heat within seconds. The cold N₂ gas continues to extract residual heat by convection as the product travels downstream, then exits via the vent.

3. **Advantages (1 mark):** Ultra-rapid freezing → small intracellular ice crystals → minimal drip loss, superior texture, premium IQF product quality.

4. **Limitations (1 mark):** High recurring cost of LN₂; risk of **thermal shock cracking** if residence time is mismatched; tank logistics; cannot economically handle bulk commodities.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** `L = 0.03 m` (half-thickness of slab = 6/2 cm), `ρ = 1050 kg/m³`, `λfus = 235 × 10³ J/kg`, `k = 1.4 W/(m·K)`, `T_f = −2 °C`, `T_a = −35 °C`, `h_c,plate = 800 W/(m²·K)`, `h_c,blast = 25 W/(m²·K)`.

**Plank's equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{\lambda_{fus} \, \rho \, L}{(T_f - T_a)} \left(\frac{1}{h_c} + \frac{L}{2k}\right) \\
(T_f - T_a) &= (-2) - (-35) = 33 \;\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 1 — Plate freezer (h_c = 800):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{f,\text{plate}} &= \frac{(235 \times 10^3)(1050)(0.03)}{33} \left(\frac{1}{800} + \frac{0.03}{2 \times 1.4}\right) \\
&= \frac{7.4025 \times 10^6}{33} \left(0.00125 + 0.010714\right) \\
&= 224{,}318 \times 0.011964 \\
&\approx 2{,}684 \;\text{s} \;\approx\; 44.7 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2 — Blast freezer (h_c = 25):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{f,\text{blast}} &= 224{,}318 \times \left(\frac{1}{25} + 0.010714\right) \\
&= 224{,}318 \times (0.04000 + 0.010714) \\
&= 224{,}318 \times 0.050714 \\
&\approx 11{,}376 \;\text{s} \;\approx\; 3.16 \;\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** The plate freezer is **≈ 4.24× faster** than the blast freezer for this geometry. **(7 marks total — 3 marks plate calc, 3 marks blast calc, 1 mark comparison)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **construction, working principle, and typical applications of a horizontal plate freezer**. How does it differ from a **vertical plate freezer**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fluidised-bed IQF freezer handles green peas at 5 t/h, freezing them from +5 °C to −18 °C. The specific heat above freezing is `3.5 kJ/(kg·K)`, below freezing is `1.8 kJ/(kg·K)`, latent heat `280 kJ/kg`, and freezing point is `−1 °C`. Calculate the **total refrigeration load (kW)** and the **required LN₂ flow rate** if 90 % of the heat is removed by LN₂ (latent heat of vaporisation of N₂ = 199 kJ/kg). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (2 marks):** A horizontal plate freezer consists of a stack of hollow aluminium/stainless-steel plates, each internally baffled, with refrigerant channels. Plates are mounted on a hydraulic ram that can close the stack to apply a uniform contact pressure of 0.5–2 kPa on the product (fish blocks, meat cuts) sandwiched between them.

**Working (2 marks):** Refrigerant (NH₃ or brine) at −35 to −40 °C circulates inside the plates. Heat conducts from the product through the metal plate (k ≈ 200 W/m·K for Al). After freezing, the ram releases, plates separate, and the frozen blocks are ejected onto a discharge conveyor.

**Applications (1 mark):** Fish blocks, meat patties, butter, cheese blocks — any product that can be moulded into a regular slab.

**Difference from vertical plate (2 marks):**
- **Vertical plates** stand upright; product is loaded from the top into rectangular moulds between plates. Suits free-flowing items like peas, berries, diced vegetables. The frozen product is discharged as a single rectangular block and then broken/cut.
- **Horizontal plates** lie flat; product is pre-placed; freezing is more uniform per unit mass; better for thick, dense blocks (up to 10–12 cm).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Mass flow rate:** `m = 5000 kg/h = 1.389 kg/s`.

**Heat loads (per kg):**
- Sensible (cool from +5 to −1 °C): `3.5 × 6 = 21 kJ/kg`
- Latent (−1 °C): `280 kJ/kg`
- Sensible (cool from −1 to −18 °C): `1.8 × 17 = 30.6 kJ/kg`
- **Total:** `21 + 280 + 30.6 = 331.6 kJ/kg`

**Total refrigeration load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{total} &= \dot{m} \times q = 1.389 \times 331.6 \\
&\approx 460.6 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**LN₂ flow rate (90 % of load):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{LN_2} &= \frac{0.90 \times \dot{Q}_{total}}{h_{fg,N_2}} = \frac{0.90 \times 460.6}{199} \\
&\approx 2.08 \;\text{kg/s} \approx 7.5 \;\text{t/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ⚠️ **Mass balance check:** `7.5 t/h LN₂` ≈ `1.5 kg LN₂ per kg peas` — consistent with industry benchmarks of 1.0–1.5 kg LN₂/kg product for IQF vegetables. **(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Examiner's Eye-View**
> 1. **Mixing up `L` in Plank's equation**: it is the *half-thickness* for a slab, not the full thickness. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the geometric constant** `P` and `R`: a *cylinder* uses `P = 1/4, R = 1/4`; a *sphere* uses `P = 1/6, R = 1/6`. The KTU 2024 paper is *known* to test this. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Using the wrong temperature difference**: always use `(T_f − T_a)`, the difference between the *freezing point* and the *cooling-medium* temperature, **not** (T_initial − T_a). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Ignoring units** in the worked numerical: kJ vs J mismatch is a classic –1 mark penalty.
> 5. **No block diagram** in cryogenic/plate freezer questions: KTU mandates a sketch → 1–2 marks reserved.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Three freezer classes** in syllabus: **Blast (air convection)**, **Plate (conduction)**, **Cryogenic (boiling LN₂/CO₂)**.
- ✅ **Plank's equation** for slab: `t_f = (λρL / ΔT) · (1/h_c + L/(2k))` — learn the **form**, not just symbols.
- ✅ **Geometric constants** Plank (slab): `P = 1/2, R = 1/2`; cylinder: `1/4`; sphere: `1/6`.
- ✅ **Surface `h_c` hierarchy:** blast (10–30) < plate (500–1500) ≈ cryogenic (500–2000) W/m²·K.
- ✅ **Typical air-blast temperature:** **−35 to −40 °C**; **LN₂:** **−196 °C**.
- ✅ **Latent heat values to memorise:** water fusion 334 kJ/kg; LN₂ vaporisation 199 kJ/kg; CO₂ sublimation 571 kJ/kg.
- ✅ **IQF** = *Individually Quick Frozen* — cryogenic and fluidised-bed freezers are the canonical examples.
- ✅ **Plate freezer pressure** to reduce contact resistance: **0.5–2 kPa**.
- ✅ **Trade-off triangle:** Cost ↓ ↔ Speed ↑ ↔ Quality ↑ — you can only pick two.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "BPC"** → **B**last (air, slow, cheap), **P**late (contact, medium, blocks), **C**ryogenic (LN₂, ultra-fast, premium).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Thermal Processing Techniques and Its Principle, Application, and Impact on Food Quality and Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advanced Thermal Processing Techniques and Its Principle, Application, and Impact on Food Quality and Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Advanced Thermal Processing Techniques?

**Advanced Thermal Processing Techniques** are non-conventional, rapid, and volumetric heating methods that deliver thermal energy *internally* to the food matrix (or through highly efficient surface radiation), in contrast to the slow, surface-to-center conductive/convective heat transfer seen in conventional retort, blanching, or steam-cooking operations. They rely on direct interaction between an electromagnetic field, electric current, or radiation wave and the food's intrinsic dielectric, electrical, or radiant properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):** *Advanced thermal processing encompasses any thermal preservation or pasteurization method in which the heat-generation mechanism occurs within the food itself (volumetric heating) or through high-intensity radiant energy transfer, achieving uniform and rapid temperature rise, minimized thermal damage to nutrients, and superior retention of sensory/functional quality.*

The principal techniques covered under the OEFTT616 Module 3 syllabus are:

| # | Technique | Energy Domain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ohmic Heating** (Joule Heating) | Electrical conduction (50–60 Hz AC) |
| 2 | **Microwave Heating** | Dielectric (915 MHz / 2.45 GHz) |
| 3 | **Radio-Frequency (RF) Heating** | Dielectric (13.56 / 27.12 / 40.68 MHz) |
| 4 | **Infrared (IR) Heating** | Electromagnetic radiation (0.78–1000 µm) |
| 5 | **Induction Heating** | Magnetic induction (typical 25–50 kHz) |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Volumetric vs. Surface Heating:**
> Imagine heating a thick chicken leg piece.
> - **Conventional boiling/steaming** = warming your hands by holding them over a campfire — heat must *creep* inward from the surface. The outside overcooks while the center is barely warm.
> - **Microwave / Ohmic heating** = your hands warming from *inside out* — the energy molecules of the food itself absorb the field and turn into heat simultaneously throughout the bulk. No "waiting" for the surface to conduct heat inward.

| Technique | Everyday Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Ohmic Heating** | Current flowing through the water-heater rod — the rod *itself* gets hot because electricity is forced through a resistive medium. |
| **Microwave Heating** | Popcorn kernel — water molecules inside rotate billions of times per second, friction → heat. |
| **RF Heating** | Like microwaves but with longer wavelength, deeper penetration (used in drying thick wood/bulk grain). |
| **Infrared Heating** | Standing under a glowing quartz heater or the Sun — radiant energy strikes the surface directly, no air heating needed. |
| **Induction Heating** | Induction cooktop — eddy currents form inside the metal pan, heating it directly without flame. |

---

## 1.3 Why These Techniques Matter (KTU Industrial Significance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Benchmarks Used in Industry:**
> - Conventional retort: **D-value reduction of 12-log** in *Clostridium botulinum* takes ~40–90 min (121 °C).
> - Ohmic / Microwave: Achieve the same lethality in **seconds to a few minutes** with **30–50 % less nutrient degradation**.
> - Volumetric heating uniformity index: **λ₁ ≤ 0.5** (well-designed ohmic system), versus λ₁ > 1.5 for a static retort.

---

## 1.4 Standard Operating Constants (Authoritative Reference Values)

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| Vacuum permeability | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m |
| Vacuum permittivity | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/m²·K⁴ |
| Planck constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| ISM Microwave frequency (domestic) | $f$ | **2450** | MHz |
| ISM Microwave frequency (industrial) | $f$ | **915** | MHz |
| Standard RF industrial band | $f$ | **27.12** | MHz |

---

## 1.5 Quick Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Penetration Categorization:**
> - **Surface-Dominant Radiative:** Infrared (penetration < 1 mm typically).
> - **Mid-Penetration:** Radio Frequency (~few cm, depends on moisture).
> - **Volumetric Deep:** Microwave and Ohmic (heat generated throughout bulk).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OHMIC HEATING (Joule/Electro-Conductive Heating)

### 2.1.1 Principle (Operational Logic)
An alternating electrical current (50 Hz in India / 60 Hz USA) is passed *directly through* the food, which behaves as a **resistive medium**. Ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, etc.) migrate with the field; collisions with neighboring molecules convert kinetic energy into **Joule heat (I²R losses)** uniformly within the product.

**Why it works step-by-step:**
1. Food placed between two inert electrodes (Titanium, graphite, or stainless steel).
2. AC voltage applied — ions oscillate rather than undergo bulk electrolysis (this is why *AC*, not DC, is mandatory).
3. Local electrical energy dissipation → bulk heating at the rate proportional to the **square of the local field strength**.

### 2.1.2 Governing Formulas

The volumetric heat generation rate (W/m³):

$$ \dot{q}_{gen} = \nabla \cdot (\sigma_{e} \, \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{E}^{*}) $$

In scalar form (uniform field approximation):

$$ \dot{q}_{gen} \;=\; \sigma_{e} \, E^{2} \;=\; \frac{E^{2}}{\rho_{e}} $$

where
- $\sigma_{e}$ = electrical conductivity of food (S/m),
- $E$ = electric field intensity (V/m),
- $\rho_{e} = 1/\sigma_{e}$ = resistivity (Ω·m).

Total power dissipated in the load:

$$ P \;=\; V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \;\approx\; V \cdot I $$

(φ ≈ 0 for resistive food loads → power factor ≈ 1.)

Temperature rise of the food mass:

$$ \Delta T \;=\; \frac{P \, t}{m \, C_{p}} \;=\; \frac{V I t}{m C_{p}} $$

### 2.1.3 Critical Parameter — Electrical Conductivity of Food

| Food | σₑ (S/m) at 25 °C |
|---|---|
| Distilled water | 0.00005 |
| Milk | 0.40 – 0.55 |
| Orange juice | 0.40 – 0.45 |
| Tomato juice | 0.16 – 0.23 |
| Soy milk | 0.18 – 0.25 |
| Solid meat (beef) | 0.30 – 0.55 |
| Bread (low moisture) | < 0.01 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum conductivity criterion:** For ohmic heating to be commercially viable, food should have $\sigma_{e} \geq 0.05$ S/m. Products with very low moisture/fat (e.g., dry powders) cannot be ohmically heated because they are nearly insulators.

### 2.1.4 Heating Uniformity Index (KTU standard — Laplace equation solution)

$$ \lambda_{1} = \frac{\sigma_{e, \, \text{centre}}}{\sigma_{e, \, \text{surface}}} $$

- $\lambda_{1} \to 1.0$ → perfectly uniform heating.
- $\lambda_{1} \to 0$ → severe cold-spot formation at center.

### 2.1.5 Applications
- Continuous pasteurization of liquid eggs, milk, fruit juices.
- Sterilization of particulate foods (soups, stews) — the solid heats at the *same rate* as the liquid if conductivities match.
- Blanching of vegetables (peppers, potatoes).
- Pre-cooking of meats for ready-meals.

### 2.1.6 Impact on Quality
- **Positive:** 80–90 % retention of Vitamin C (vs. 50–60 % in retort); minimal Maillard browning due to absence of hot surface.
- **Negative:** Electrolysis risk at electrode–food interface if system is poorly designed (causing pH drift ±0.2 units near electrodes).

---

## 2.2 MICROWAVE HEATING

### 2.2.1 Principle
Microwaves are **non-ionizing electromagnetic waves** that interact with polar molecules (chiefly **water**) and ionic species via two mechanisms:
1. **Dipolar rotation** — water dipoles try to align with the alternating E-field; lag causes frictional heating.
2. **Ionic conduction** — dissolved ions accelerate back and forth, colliding with molecules.

### 2.2.2 Governing Formula — Power Dissipation per Unit Volume

$$ \dot{q}_{MW} \;=\; 2 \pi f \, \varepsilon_{0} \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{2} $$

where $\varepsilon''$ = **dielectric loss factor** (dimensionless) of the food.

Equivalently:

$$ \dot{q}_{MW} \;=\; 55.61 \times 10^{-12} \, f \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{2} $$

with $f$ in Hz, $E_{rms}$ in V/m, output in W/m³.

### 2.2.3 Penetration Depth (the single most-tested formula in KTU)

$$ d_{p} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{0}}{2\pi \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2}} \;-\; 1}} $$

For **low-loss foods** ($\varepsilon'' \ll \varepsilon'$), this simplifies to:

$$ d_{p} \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda_{0} \, \sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2 \pi \, \varepsilon''} $$

| Food | Penetration Depth @ 2.45 GHz |
|---|---|
| Water (25 °C) | ~ 1.4 cm |
| Beef (raw) | 0.9 – 1.1 cm |
| Bread | 1.5 – 2.0 cm |
| Butter | ~ 6.0 cm (very low loss) |
| Frozen meat | 5 – 8 cm (ice, very low loss) |

### 2.2.4 Applications
- Domestic reheating, tempering of frozen foods.
- Industrial continuous microwave pasteurization (sauces, ready-meals).
- Microwave-vacuum drying (MVB).
- Microwave-assisted extraction of bioactives.

### 2.2.5 Quality Impact
- Rapid inactivation of enzymes & microbes (D-values reduce by 60–80 %).
- **Negative:** Run-away heating at edges/corners (focused hot spots), "bumping" of liquids, textural collapse in starch gels if not moisture-controlled.

---

## 2.3 RADIO-FREQUENCY (RF) HEATING

### 2.3.1 Principle
Same physics as microwave heating, but at **lower frequencies (13.56, 27.12, 40.68 MHz)**. Because the wavelength is much longer (≈ 11 m at 27 MHz), RF waves penetrate **tens of centimeters into bulk products** — making it ideal for drying thick pastes, grains, and biscuits.

### 2.3.2 Governing Formula

$$ d_{p, RF} \;=\; \frac{c}{2\pi f \, \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2}} \;-\; 1}} $$

For 27 MHz vs 2.45 GHz → **penetration is ~ 90× greater** in RF (since $d_p \propto 1/f$ for low-loss media).

### 2.3.3 Applications
- Post-bake drying of biscuits (replacing hot-air, saving ~ 30 % energy).
- Disinfestation of grain, pulses, nuts (non-chemical disinfestation).
- Bulk heating of packaged foods.

---

## 2.4 INFRARED (IR) HEATING

### 2.4.1 Principle
IR radiation (wavelength 0.78 – 1000 µm) is emitted by a hot body and absorbed by the food surface, causing rapid molecular vibrational excitation → heat. Three sub-bands:

| Sub-band | λ range | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| Near IR (NIR) | 0.78 – 1.4 µm | Surface heating, baking, crusting |
| Short-wave IR (SWIR) | 1.4 – 3 µm | Industrial drying |
| Far IR (FIR) | 3 – 1000 µm | Deep surface penetration (paper, paint, films) |

### 2.4.2 Governing Law — Stefan-Boltzmann (with emissivity correction)

$$ q \;=\; \varepsilon \, \sigma \left( T_{s}^{4} - T_{f}^{4} \right) $$

For a gray body food surface:

$$ \varepsilon \;=\; \frac{\text{Emitted radiation}}{\text{Blackbody radiation at same T}} $$

Most foods: $\varepsilon \approx 0.90$ – $0.97$.

### 2.4.3 Beer-Lambert Absorption Within Food

$$ I(x) \;=\; I_{0} \, e^{-\alpha_{IR} \, x} $$

where $\alpha_{IR}$ = IR absorption coefficient (m⁻¹). Typical foods: $\alpha_{IR} \approx 100$ – $10\,000$ m⁻¹.

### 2.4.4 Applications
- Surface pasteurization of bread crust, bakery products.
- Infrared dehydration of fruits (banana chips).
- Roasting of coffee, nuts.
- Combined IR + hot air (IR-MAX) dryers.

---

## 2.5 INDUCTION HEATING

### 2.5.1 Principle
A high-frequency alternating current in a coil generates a **rapidly alternating magnetic field**, which induces **eddy currents** in any electrically conductive body placed in the field. Eddy currents dissipate as Joule heat.

### 2.5.2 Governing Formula

$$ P_{ind} \;=\; k \cdot B^{2} \cdot f^{2} \; \cdot \; \text{(geometry factor)} $$

For a semi-infinite slab, **skin depth** (δ) where current density drops to 37 %:

$$ \delta \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho_{e}}{\mu \, \omega}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{e}}{\pi \, \mu \, f}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Application Note:** Induction heating is *not* used directly on foods (which are poor conductors) — but is used to **heat the packaging/sealing layer** (e.g., induction-sealed caps, induction-welded films).

---

## 2.6 Consolidated KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least 70 % of KTU numericals on this module use one of these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohmic heat generation | $\dot{q} = \sigma_{e} E^{2}$ | J/m³·s |
| 2 | Temperature rise (ohmic) | $\Delta T = \dfrac{V I t}{m C_{p}}$ | K |
| 3 | Microwave heating rate | $\dot{q}_{MW} = 2\pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^{2}$ | W/m³ |
| 4 | Penetration depth (general) | $d_{p} = \dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{2\pi\sqrt{2\varepsilon'}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1+(\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^2}-1}}$ | m |
| 5 | Penetration depth (low-loss) | $d_{p} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{0}\sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi \varepsilon''}$ | m |
| 6 | Free-space wavelength | $\lambda_{0} = c / f$ | m |
| 7 | Stefan-Boltzmann (IR) | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_{s}^{4} - T_{f}^{4})$ | W/m² |
| 8 | Beer-Lambert (IR) | $I(x) = I_{0} e^{-\alpha x}$ | W/m² |
| 9 | Induction skin depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{e}}{\pi \mu f}}$ | m |
| 10 | Heating uniformity | $\lambda_{1} = \sigma_{e,c}/\sigma_{e,s}$ | – |

> [!WARNING]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\mid` in case of confusion — the markdown renderer in some KTU e-textbooks mishandles the vertical bar in `|x|`. Substitute `|x|` → `\vert x \vert`.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Penetration Depth of EM Waves into a Food Material

**Starting premise:** When an EM wave enters a lossy dielectric (food), the field decays exponentially with distance from the surface.

The complex propagation constant for a lossy dielectric is:

$$ \gamma \;=\; \alpha_{att} \;+\; j \beta_{ph} $$

where
- $\alpha_{att}$ = attenuation constant (Np/m),
- $\beta_{ph}$ = phase constant (rad/m).

For a plane wave in a good but lossy dielectric:

$$ \alpha_{att} \;=\; \omega \sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon'}{2}} \; \sqrt{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2}} - 1} $$

$$ \beta_{ph} \;=\; \omega \sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon'}{2}} \; \sqrt{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2}} + 1} $$

The penetration depth is conventionally defined as the distance at which the **power density** has fallen to $1/e$ of its surface value. Since power ∝ (field)², this corresponds to a field attenuation of $1/\sqrt{e}$:

$$ d_{p} \;=\; \frac{1}{2 \, \alpha_{att}} $$

Substituting the expression for $\alpha_{att}$:

$$ d_{p} \;=\; \frac{1}{2 \, \omega \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu \varepsilon'}{2}} \, \sqrt{\sqrt{1 + (\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^{2}} - 1}} $$

Since $\omega = 2\pi f$ and free-space wavelength $\lambda_0 = c/f = 1/(f\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0})$:

$$ \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu \varepsilon'}{2}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon'} \cdot 2\pi f}{2 \pi \sqrt{2} \, c} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{\lambda_{0} \sqrt{2}} $$

Therefore:

$$ d_{p} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{0}}{2\pi \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + (\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^{2}} - 1}} $$

This is the **KTU standard penetration-depth formula**. In the limit $\varepsilon'' \ll \varepsilon'$ (low-loss foods like frozen materials, butter), expand the inner square root:

$$ \sqrt{1 + (\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^2} \;\approx\; 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}(\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^2 $$

$$ \sqrt{\sqrt{1+(\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^2} - 1} \;\approx\; \frac{\varepsilon''}{\sqrt{2}\,\varepsilon'} $$

Therefore, **for low-loss materials**:

$$ d_{p} \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda_{0}}{2\pi \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}\,\varepsilon'}{\varepsilon''} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{0} \sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi \varepsilon''} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ohmic Heating Temperature Rise from Energy Balance

**Step 1.** Total electrical energy input:

$$ W_{e} \;=\; P \, \Delta t \;=\; V \, I \, \Delta t $$

**Step 2.** All this energy is converted to thermal energy (Joule heating, ideal case):

$$ Q \;=\; W_{e} \;=\; V \, I \, \Delta t $$

**Step 3.** The food absorbs this heat and its temperature rises by $\Delta T$:

$$ Q \;=\; m \, C_{p} \, \Delta T $$

**Step 4.** Equate:

$$ V \, I \, \Delta t \;=\; m \, C_{p} \, \Delta T $$

**Step 5.** Solve for temperature rise:

$$ \Delta T \;=\; \frac{V \, I \, \Delta t}{m \, C_{p}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Note:** This equation holds only when (a) no heat is lost to the surroundings, (b) the food has uniform $\sigma_e$, (c) phase change does not occur. Real systems require correction factor $\eta = 0.85$–$0.95$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Blackbody Equation

Planck's spectral radiance is:

$$ B_{\lambda}(T) \;=\; \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_{B} T}\right) - 1} $$

Integrating over all wavelengths gives the total emitted power per unit area of a blackbody:

$$ E_{bb}(T) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} B_{\lambda}(T) \, \pi \, d\lambda $$

After integration (using substitution $u = hc/\lambda k_B T$):

$$ E_{bb}(T) \;=\; \sigma \, T^{4} $$

where $\sigma = 2 \pi^{5} k_{B}^{4} / (15 h^{3} c^{2}) = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴.

For a real food surface (emissivity $\varepsilon < 1$):

$$ E(T) \;=\; \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4} $$

Net heat flux between food surface (T_f) and IR heater surface (T_s):

$$ q \;=\; \varepsilon \, \sigma \left( T_{s}^{4} - T_{f}^{4} \right) $$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 5 kg batch of orange juice ($\sigma_e = 0.40$ S/m, $C_p = 3800$ J/kg·K) is ohmically heated between two parallel plate electrodes of effective spacing 6 cm. A voltage of 220 V (rms) is applied for 60 s. Determine:
> (a) the volumetric heat generation rate,
> (b) the temperature rise (neglecting heat losses),
> (c) the current drawn.
> Take electrode area $A = 0.025$ m².

**Solution:**

**(a)** Electric field:

$$ E \;=\; \frac{V}{d} \;=\; \frac{220}{0.06} \;=\; 3666.67 \;\; \text{V/m} $$

Volumetric heat generation:

$$ \dot{q} \;=\; \sigma_{e} E^{2} \;=\; 0.40 \times (3666.67)^{2} \;\approx\; 5.378 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{W/m}^{3} $$

**(b)** Volume of juice between plates:

$$ V_{\text{vol}} \;=\; A \cdot d \;=\; 0.025 \times 0.06 \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^{3} $$

Total power:

$$ P \;=\; \dot{q} \cdot V_{\text{vol}} \;=\; 5.378 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 8066.7 \;\; \text{W} $$

Total energy in 60 s:

$$ W \;=\; P \cdot t \;=\; 8066.7 \times 60 \;\approx\; 4.84 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{J} $$

Temperature rise:

$$ \Delta T \;=\; \frac{W}{m C_{p}} \;=\; \frac{4.84 \times 10^{5}}{5 \times 3800} \;\approx\; 25.5 \;\; \text{K} $$

**(c)** Current drawn:

$$ I \;=\; \frac{P}{V} \;=\; \frac{8066.7}{220} \;\approx\; 36.7 \;\; \text{A} $$

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define **ohmic heating**. List any two industrial applications of ohmic heating in food processing.

**Model Answer:**
Ohmic heating, also known as **Joule heating** or **electro-conductive heating**, is an advanced thermal processing technique in which an alternating electrical current (typically 50–60 Hz) is passed *directly through* the food, which acts as a resistive element. The heat is generated *within* the food by ionic conduction as the dissolved salts and ions oscillate with the field; this is the *volumetric* heating that distinguishes it from conventional conductive heating.

**Two industrial applications:**
1. Continuous pasteurization of **liquid whole egg** and fruit juices (e.g., orange, apple) where the product is heat-sensitive.
2. Aseptic sterilization of **particulate foods** such as vegetable-in-soup preparations, where matching electrical conductivities of solid and liquid phases eliminate the cold spot inside the particles.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
What is **penetration depth** in the context of microwave heating? State its significance.

**Model Answer:**
**Penetration depth ($d_p$)** is defined as the distance from the surface of a food at which the **microwave power density has decreased to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8 %) of its surface value**. It is given by:

$$ d_{p} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{0}}{2\pi \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + (\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^{2}} - 1}} $$

**Significance:**
- It determines the **maximum thickness** of food that can be uniformly microwave-heated in a single pass.
- It governs the **choice of frequency**: at 915 MHz, $d_p$ is roughly 2–3 times greater than at 2.45 GHz, making 915 MHz the preferred industrial frequency for bulk products.
- Foods with high moisture (high $\varepsilon''$) have *small* penetration depth, requiring thinner pieces; frozen foods (low $\varepsilon''$) have very large $d_p$ — this is why *microwave tempering* of frozen meat is highly efficient.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, working mechanism and industrial applications of ohmic heating** in food processing. Discuss its advantages over conventional thermal processing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous ohmic heater processes **tomato juice** at a flow rate of **500 kg/h**. The juice has electrical conductivity $\sigma_e = 0.20$ S/m, density $\rho = 1040$ kg/m³, and $C_p = 3900$ J/kg·K. The juice enters at **30 °C** and must reach **95 °C**. The heater uses an applied voltage of **4000 V (rms)** across electrodes spaced **4 cm apart**. Determine:
(i) the electric field strength,
(ii) the volumetric heat generation rate,
(iii) the total power required,
(iv) the **current** drawn.
Neglect heat losses. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER (Question A)

**(a) Principle and working mechanism of ohmic heating**

**Definition:** Ohmic heating is an *electro-thermal* process in which an alternating electrical current is passed through a food that behaves as a **resistive (ohmic) medium**. The ionic species dissolved in the food (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, etc.) are accelerated by the electric field; their collisions with neighbouring molecules convert electrical energy directly into **Joule heat ($I^2R$ heating)** within the bulk of the food.

**Why AC and not DC:**
A direct current would cause electrolysis at the electrodes — generating H₂, O₂, Cl₂, and producing off-flavours and pH changes. Using **50 Hz / 60 Hz AC** causes ions to oscillate rather than undergo bulk transport, eliminating electrolysis.

**Key governing equation:**

$$ \dot{q}_{gen} \;=\; \sigma_{e} \, E^{2} \;=\; \frac{V^{2}}{R \, V_{\text{vol}}} $$

**Critical design requirements (KTU high-yield points):**
1. The food must have $\sigma_e \geq 0.05$ S/m.
2. Electrodes must be **inert** (Titanium grade 1, graphite, or stainless steel coated with platinum) to avoid corrosion.
3. Frequency must be ≥ 50 Hz to avoid electrolysis; some commercial units operate up to 100 kHz.
4. The **heating uniformity index $\lambda_1 = \sigma_{e,c}/\sigma_{e,s}$** must be maintained close to 1 by designing the geometry and matching solid/liquid conductivities in particulate foods.

**Industrial applications:**
- Pasteurization of milk, fruit juices, liquid egg.
- Sterilization of ready-meals with particulates (e.g., meat + vegetable + sauce).
- Pre-cooking / blanching of vegetables.
- Heat treatment of tomato concentrates, baby foods.
- Continuous aseptic processing.

**Advantages over conventional thermal processing:**
1. **Volumetric heating** — eliminates surface overcooking.
2. **Heating rates 30–100 °C/min** vs. 1–3 °C/min in retort.
3. **Energy efficiency 85–95 %** (heat generated inside product, no furnace or steam losses).
4. **Excellent nutrient retention** (Vit C, thiamine) and sensory quality.
5. **No fouling on heating surfaces** (since surfaces are not hot), enabling longer run times.
6. **Uniform heating of particulates + carrier fluid** when conductivities are matched — solves a major problem in aseptic processing.

**Limitations:**
- Not suitable for foods with very low $\sigma_e$ (fats, dry powders, frozen foods).
- Electrode corrosion and pH drift possible in poorly designed systems.
- Capital cost higher than retorts.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m} = 500$ kg/h $= 500/3600 = 0.1389$ kg/s
- $\sigma_e = 0.20$ S/m
- $\rho = 1040$ kg/m³
- $C_p = 3900$ J/kg·K
- $T_{in} = 30$ °C, $T_{out} = 95$ °C → $\Delta T = 65$ K
- $V = 4000$ V rms
- $d = 0.04$ m

**(i) Electric field strength:**

$$ E \;=\; \frac{V}{d} \;=\; \frac{4000}{0.04} \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{V/m} $$

**(ii) Volumetric heat generation rate:**

$$ \dot{q}_{gen} \;=\; \sigma_{e} \, E^{2} \;=\; 0.20 \times (1.0 \times 10^{5})^{2} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{9} \;\; \text{W/m}^{3} $$

**(iii) Total power required:**
First, the heat load:

$$ \dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m} \, C_{p} \, \Delta T \;=\; 0.1389 \times 3900 \times 65 $$

$$ \dot{Q} \;\approx\; 3.522 \times 10^{4} \;\; \text{W} \;\approx\; 35.2 \;\; \text{kW} $$

Since heat losses are neglected, **P ≈ 35.2 kW** is the required total electrical power input.

**(iv) Current drawn:**

$$ I \;=\; \frac{P}{V} \;=\; \frac{3.522 \times 10^{4}}{4000} \;\approx\; 8.81 \;\; \text{A} $$

**Optional sanity check — confirm $d_p$ consistency:**

Volumetric flow rate:

$$ \dot{V}_{vol} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} \;=\; \frac{0.1389}{1040} \;\approx\; 1.336 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} $$

Required heater volume:

$$ V_{\text{heater}} \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{q}_{gen}} \;=\; \frac{3.522 \times 10^{4}}{2.0 \times 10^{9}} \;\approx\; 1.76 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{m}^{3} $$

Heater volume is small → very compact design, characteristic of ohmic systems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Choice 2]**

**(a)** Compare **microwave heating and radio-frequency heating** with respect to their physical principles, frequency, penetration depth, and industrial applications in food processing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microwave pasteurization system operates at **915 MHz** to heat a **ready-meal sauce** ($\varepsilon' = 65$, $\varepsilon'' = 17$ at 915 MHz). Calculate the **penetration depth** of the microwave in this sauce. If the same product is processed at **2.45 GHz** (with $\varepsilon' = 60$, $\varepsilon'' = 22$), what is the new penetration depth? Comment on the implications for industrial process design. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER (Question B)

**(a) Comparison: Microwave vs Radio-Frequency Heating**

| Aspect | Microwave (MW) | Radio-Frequency (RF) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency bands (ISM) | 915 MHz, 2.45 GHz | 13.56, 27.12, 40.68 MHz |
| Free-space wavelength (λ₀) | 33 cm (915 MHz); 12.2 cm (2.45 GHz) | ≈ 22 m (13.56 MHz); ≈ 11 m (27.12 MHz) |
| Penetration depth | 1 – 10 cm in water-rich foods | 10 – 100 cm — penetrates deep into bulk |
| Heating mechanism | Dipolar rotation + ionic conduction | Same physical mechanism as MW |
| Applicator type | Waveguide / resonant cavity | Capacitive plate (between electrodes) |
| Coupling to food | Tight (small applicator) | Loose (large uniform field) |
| Industrial applications | Pasteurization of sauces, tempering frozen meat, MVB drying | Bulk drying of biscuits, post-bake drying, grain disinfestation, packaged food heating |
| Limitation | Limited penetration → thin products; non-uniform in thick pieces | Larger capital cost; tuning is more critical |

**Summary statement:** Microwave heating is preferred for **fast, controlled heating of moderate-thickness foods** (up to ~5 cm), while RF heating is the choice for **bulk, deep-penetration heating of large or thick products** where uniform temperatures throughout are required.

---

**(b) Penetration Depth Calculation**

**General penetration depth formula:**

$$ d_{p} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{0}}{2\pi \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + (\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^{2}} - 1}} $$

**Case 1 — f = 915 MHz, $\varepsilon' = 65$, $\varepsilon'' = 17$:**

$$ \lambda_{0,1} \;=\; \frac{c}{f} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{915 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; 0.3279 \;\; \text{m} $$

Loss tangent ratio:

$$ \frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'} \;=\; \frac{17}{65} \;\approx\; 0.2615 $$

$$ \left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2} \;\approx\; 0.0684 $$

$$ \sqrt{1 + 0.0684} \;\approx\; 1.0334 $$

$$ \sqrt{1.0334 - 1} \;=\; \sqrt{0.0334} \;\approx\; 0.1827 $$

$$ \sqrt{2\varepsilon'} \;=\; \sqrt{130} \;\approx\; 11.40 $$

$$ d_{p,1} \;=\; \frac{0.3279}{2\pi \times 11.40 \times 0.1827} \;\approx\; \frac{0.3279}{13.08} \;\approx\; 0.0251 \;\; \text{m} \;\approx\; 2.51 \;\; \text{cm} $$

**Case 2 — f = 2.45 GHz, $\varepsilon' = 60$, $\varepsilon'' = 22$:**

$$ \lambda_{0,2} \;=\; \frac{c}{f} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.45 \times 10^{9}} \;\approx\; 0.1224 \;\; \text{m} $$

$$ \frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'} \;=\; \frac{22}{60} \;\approx\; 0.3667 $$

$$ \left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2} \;\approx\; 0.1344 $$

$$ \sqrt{1.1344} \;\approx\; 1.0651 $$

$$ \sqrt{1.0651 - 1} \;=\; \sqrt{0.0651} \;\approx\; 0.2552 $$

$$ \sqrt{2 \times 60} \;=\; \sqrt{120} \;\approx\; 10.954 $$

$$ d_{p,2} \;=\; \frac{0.1224}{2\pi \times 10.954 \times 0.2552} \;\approx\; \frac{0.1224}{17.55} \;\approx\; 0.00697 \;\; \text{m} \;\approx\; 6.97 \;\; \text{mm} $$

**Comment on industrial design implications:**

$$ \frac{d_{p,1}}{d_{p,2}} \;=\; \frac{2.51}{0.697} \;\approx\; 3.6 $$

- At **915 MHz**, the penetration depth is **~ 3.6 × larger** than at 2.45 GHz.
- For the same ready-meal sauce in **retort-pouch thickness of 1.5 cm**, the 915 MHz process heats uniformly across the depth; the 2.45 GHz process would develop surface heating with a cold center.
- Therefore, **915 MHz** is mandated for **bulk industrial MW pasteurization**, while **2.45 GHz** is reserved for **domestic ovens and thin-product surface drying**.
- Lower frequency → longer wavelength → lower energy per photon (still non-ionizing, $hf \approx 10^{-5}$ eV vs. ionization threshold of ~10 eV), confirming biological safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up penetration depth definition:** Some students write "depth at which the *field* falls to $1/e$" — wrong. It is the depth at which **power density** falls to $1/e$, i.e. field falls to $1/\sqrt{e}$. This single error costs 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\cos\phi$ in ohmic heating:** Power factor is ~1 for resistive food, but always write $P = VI\cos\phi$ formally in derivations to score full marks.
> 3. **Confusing frequency vs wavelength in penetration formula:** Substituting $f$ where $\lambda_0$ should go (or vice versa) — re-derive $d_p$ once from $\alpha_{att}$ to eliminate this error.
> 4. **Assuming IR heating is volumetric:** It is *surface radiative* — overstating penetration depth of IR will lose marks.
> 5. **Forgetting unit conversions in numericals:** mW vs W, m vs cm, MHz vs Hz — KTU markers routinely deduct 0.5 marks for unit errors.

---
