2. **Protein denaturation:** Disruption of hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions leads to unfolding of cytoplasmic and membrane-bound enzymes → loss of metabolic activity.
3. **Ribosome dissociation:** Subunits of 70S ribosomes separate → halted protein synthesis.
4. **DNA/RNA effects:** Generally minor at commercial pressures (<700 MPa); notable only at very high pressures.

**Step 3: Effect on food components (2 marks)**

| Component | Effect | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Vitamins (C, B) | Preserved | Orange juice retains >95% vitamin C after HPP |
| Pigments | Preserved | Anthocyanins in berries remain stable |
| Flavor volatiles | Preserved | Fresh orange flavor maintained |
| Proteins | Denatured | Whey protein gelation |
| Starch | Gelatinized at >600 MPa | Tapioca starch modification |
| Enzymes | Partially inactivated | Polyphenol oxidase in mushroom |

#### Part (b) — A fruit juice sample contains an initial microbial load of $10^7$ CFU/mL. After HPP treatment at 550 MPa, the pressure D-value for the target organism is 3.8 minutes. **[7 Marks]**
- **(i)** Calculate the holding time required for a **6-log reduction**.
- **(ii)** If the pressure is increased to 600 MPa, the new $D_P$ becomes 1.9 minutes. Calculate the **pressure z-value**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Holding time for 6-log reduction:**

Using the first-order kinetic equation:

$$
\log \left( \dfrac{N_0}{N} \right) = \dfrac{t}{D_P}
$$

Substituting $N_0 = 10^7$, $N = 10^1$, and $D_P = 3.8$ min:

$$
\log \left( \dfrac{10^7}{10^1} \right) = \dfrac{t}{3.8}
$$

$$
6 = \dfrac{t}{3.8}
$$

$$
\boxed{t = 22.8 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**(ii) Pressure z-value:**

Using the formula:

$$
z_P = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\log \left( \dfrac{D_{P_1}}{D_{P_2}} \right)}
$$

Substituting $\Delta P = 600 - 550 = 50$ MPa, $D_{P_1} = 3.8$ min, $D_{P_2} = 1.9$ min:

$$
z_P = \dfrac{50}{\log (3.8/1.9)} = \dfrac{50}{\log (2)} = \dfrac{50}{0.3010}
$$

$$
\boxed{z_P \approx 166.1 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly write $z_P = \Delta P / (\log D_1 - \log D_2)$ with sign confusion or use the wrong $D_P$ in numerator. Always remember: $z_P$ is the **pressure increase** needed for a **10-fold decrease** in $D_P$. Re-check units: $\Delta P$ must be in MPa, not bar or psi.

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice Question on HPP **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Describe the industrial equipment and process flow for High-Pressure Processing of packaged food. Mention key design considerations. **[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Major equipment components (3 marks)**

1. **Pressure vessel** — Forged high-tensile steel cylinder with threaded or yoke closure; rated for 600–700 MPa.
2. **Pressure generation system** — A low-pressure pump (~30 MPa) feeding an **intensifier** that multiplies pressure to operating level.
3. **Pressure-transmitting fluid** — Typically water with 5–20% propylene glycol for corrosion inhibition and lubricant properties.
4. **Temperature control system** — Heat exchangers to maintain desired processing temperature.
5. **Safety devices** — Burst discs, relief valves, mechanical interlocks.
6. **Control and monitoring** — PLC with pressure transducers, thermocouples, and SCADA interface.

**Step 2: Process flow (2 marks)**

Loading → Closure → Pressurization (ramp-up) → Pressure hold → Decompression (controlled release) → Opening → Quality check → Storage.

**Step 3: Design considerations (2 marks)**

- **Vessel fatigue life** must accommodate cyclic pressure loading.
- **Food-contact fluids** must be food-grade.
- **Package flexibility** is essential — rigid glass or metal containers will fracture.
- **Head space** in packages should be minimized to avoid compression-induced deformation.

#### Part (b) — Discuss the applications and limitations of HPP in the food industry. Compare HPP with High-Pressure Homogenization (HPH). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Applications of HPP (3 marks)**

1. **Juices and beverages** — HPP pasteurized orange juice, apple juice, and smoothies with extended refrigerated shelf-life.
2. **Meat and seafood** — Ready-to-eat (RTE) meats, oysters, sliced ham with reduced *Listeria* risk.
3. **Dairy products** — Cheese, yogurt with improved texture and extended freshness.
4. **Plant-based products** — Guacamole, hummus, salsa (preserving fresh color and flavor).
5. **Rice and grain products** — Pre-cooked rice with reduced microbial load.

**Step 2: Limitations of HPP (2 marks)**

1. **Spore resistance** — cannot inactivate bacterial spores alone.
2. **High capital cost** — pressure vessels and intensifiers are expensive.
3. **Batch processing** — limits throughput compared to continuous thermal systems.
4. **Package constraints** — only flexible, air-tight packages are suitable.
5. **Texture modifications** — protein denaturation may alter mouthfeel in some products.

**Step 3: Comparison with High-Pressure Homogenization (2 marks)**

| Feature | HPP | HPH |
|---|---|---|
| Operating pressure | 400–600 MPa | Up to 400 MPa |
| Application mode | Batch | Continuous |
| Mechanism | Isostatic compression | Shear, cavitation, turbulence |
| Primary use | Pasteurization of packaged food | Emulsification, particle size reduction |
| Microbial inactivation | Bulk pasteurization | Surface sterilization, structural modification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse HPP (isostatic/batch) with HPH (dynamic shear/continuous). KTU valuation key penalizes this interchange. Memorize: **HPP = Pascal's Law (uniform pressure); HPH = shear + turbulence (continuous flow).**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [x] HPP = **400–600 MPa** at ambient/mild temperature for **1–20 min**.
- [x] Governing principles: **Pascal's Law** + **Le Chatelier's Principle**.
- [x] Pressure is transmitted **instantaneously and uniformly** (isostatic).
- [x] **Vegetative cells** inactivated; **spores** resistant.
- [x] **Non-covalent bonds** disrupted; **covalent bonds** preserved.
- [x] Small molecules (vitamins, flavor, color) are **preserved**.
- [x] First-order inactivation: $\ln(N/N_0) = -k_P \cdot t$.
- [x] $D_P = \ln 10 / k_P$ — **pressure D-value (minutes)**.
- [x] $z_P = \Delta P / \log(D_{P_1}/D_{P_2})$ — **pressure z-value (MPa)**.
- [x] **PATP** (Pressure-Assisted Thermal Processing) overcomes spore resistance.
- [x] Pressurizing fluid = water + propylene glycol.
- [x] **1 MPa = 10 bar = 145.038 psi**.
- [x] Negative **activation volume** $\Delta V^{\#}$ → pressure accelerates inactivation.
- [x] HPP ≠ HPH (HPP is isostatic/batch; HPH is shear/continuous).
- [x] Commercial products: juices, RTE meats, guacamole, oysters.
- [x] KTU exam tip: Always state the **assumptions** (constant T, isostatic P) when solving kinetic problems.

> **Final Quick-Glance Mnemonic:** **"PASCAL"**
> - **P**ressure **400–600 MPa**
> - **A**mbient/mild **T**emperature
> - **S**pores survive (need PATP)
> - **C**ovalent bonds **A**re **L**eft intact
- **Alternative Mnemonic for principles:** **"PIE-LE"** → **P**ascal's Law, **I**sostatic, **E**yring → **LE** Chatelier.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Novel Thermal Processing Techniques: Infrared Heating
Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** is a **non-thermal food preservation technology** that applies **short-duration high-voltage pulses** (microseconds to milliseconds) to a food material placed between two electrodes, generating an electric field typically in the range of **20–80 kV/cm**. The applied field induces **electroporation** of microbial and plant cell membranes, leading to loss of membrane integrity and subsequent cell inactivation, while preserving the fresh-like nutritional, sensory, and functional qualities of the food.

PEF is classified under **emerging non-thermal processing technologies** alongside High Pressure Processing (HPP), Ultrasound, UV-C, and Cold Plasma. It is sometimes referred to as **Electroporation Processing** or **High Intensity Pulsed Electric Field (HIPEF)** in advanced literature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a room full of inflated balloons (representing microbial cells) and you want to pop only the red ones (harmful bacteria) without disturbing the blue ones (food cells). 

In **conventional thermal processing** (boiling/sterilization), you would heat the entire room — this pops the balloons but also melts any chocolate bars (vitamins, flavors, textures) lying around.

In **PEF**, you instead send thousands of precisely aimed, super-fast tiny **"electric bullets"** (high-voltage pulses) that specifically pierce the red balloons' outer skin (cell membrane) by exploiting the fact that microbial membranes have a **critical breakdown voltage** different from food tissue. The pulses are so brief (**1–100 microseconds**) that the food barely warms up — only **1–5 °C rise** — preserving the chocolate bars (vitamins, color, flavor) completely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Approved Values):**
> - **Electric Field Strength (E):** 20–80 kV/cm
> - **Pulse Duration (tₚ):** 1–100 μs (typically 1–5 μs)
> - **Number of Pulses (n):** 1–1000
> - **Pulse Polarity:** Monopolar or Bipolar
> - **Treatment Temperature:** Below 40 °C (non-thermal regime)
> - **Pulse Waveform:** Exponential decay or Square wave

### 1.3 Why PEF? — Motivation in Food Processing

| Problem in Conventional Thermal Processing | How PEF Solves It |
|---|---|
| Loss of heat-sensitive vitamins (Vitamin C, B-complex) | Minimal temperature rise preserves heat-labile nutrients |
| Cooked flavor development in juices | Cold process retains "fresh-like" sensory profile |
| Texture softening in fruits/vegetables | Cellular integrity of plant tissue preserved (controlled) |
| High energy consumption for heating/cooling | Energy efficient for liquid foods |
| Color degradation (Maillard reactions) | No browning reactions occur |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of Operation — Structured Logic Steps

**Step 1: Generation of High-Voltage Pulse**
A high-voltage DC power supply charges a bank of capacitors (energy storage). When discharged through a switch (thyristor, MOSFET, or spark gap), it produces a high-voltage pulse across the electrodes of the treatment chamber.

**Step 2: Application of Electric Field**
The food product, flowing or static between two electrodes, is subjected to the electric field. The field strength depends on the voltage applied and the gap between electrodes:
$$E = \frac{V}{d}$$

where V is the voltage (kV) and d is the electrode gap (cm).

**Step 3: Electroporation of Cell Membranes**
The trans-membrane potential across the lipid bilayer of microbial cells exceeds a **critical value (≈1 V)**. This causes:
- Formation of hydrophilic pores in the membrane
- Increased membrane permeability
- Loss of cellular homeostasis
- Osmotic swelling and eventual cell lysis

**Step 4: Microbial Inactivation**
The inactivated cells cannot repair their membranes under optimal conditions, leading to irreversible damage and death. Spores are more resistant than vegetative cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Electric Field Strength (E_crit):**
> - Vegetative bacteria: **4–10 kV/cm**
> - Yeasts: **3–5 kV/cm**
> - Bacterial spores: **15–25 kV/cm** (much higher)
> - Plant cell membrane: **1–3 kV/cm** (used for enhancing extraction)

### 2.2 Classification of Electroporation

| Type | Reversibility | Field Strength | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reversible Electroporation** | Pores seal after pulse | Below critical field | Gene transfer, drug delivery |
| **Irreversible Electroporation** | Permanent membrane damage | Above critical field | Microbial inactivation (PEF food processing) |

### 2.3 Pulse Waveforms Used in PEF

| Waveform | Description | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Exponential Decay** | Capacitor discharge waveform | Simple, low cost |
| **Square Wave** | Constant voltage during pulse | More uniform treatment, higher efficiency |
| **Bipolar Pulses** | Alternating +/− polarity | Reduces electrolytic deposition on electrodes |
| **Monopolar Pulses** | Single polarity | Simpler design |

### 2.4 Factors Affecting PEF Efficiency

1. **Electric Field Strength (E):** Higher E → greater inactivation, but risk of dielectric breakdown
2. **Pulse Duration (tₚ):** Longer pulses → more energy deposition
3. **Number of Pulses (n):** More pulses → cumulative damage to cells
4. **Pulse Shape:** Square wave > Exponential decay for microbial inactivation
5. **Product Conductivity (σ):** Affects current flow and heating
6. **Treatment Temperature:** Higher T (sub-lethal) enhances inactivation
7. **pH of medium:** Acidic conditions synergize with PEF
8. **Microbial type, size, and growth phase:** Larger cells more susceptible; log-phase cells most sensitive

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electric field strength | $E = V/d$ | kV/cm | V = voltage, d = gap |
| Current density | $J = \sigma \cdot E$ | A/m² | σ = electrical conductivity |
| Power density | $P = E \cdot J = \sigma \cdot E^2$ | W/m³ | Energy per unit volume per second |
| Energy per pulse | $W_p = C \cdot V^2 / 2 = \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot v \cdot t_p$ | J | C = capacitance, v = chamber volume |
| Specific energy | $W_s = W_p / m$ | kJ/kg | Energy per kg of product |
| Total energy input | $W_t = n \cdot W_p$ | J | n = number of pulses |
| Temperature rise | $\Delta T = W_s / c_p$ | °C | cₚ = specific heat (≈3.8–4.0 kJ/kg·K for juices) |
| Inactivation kinetics | $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -k \cdot E^n \cdot t_p^n$ | log reduction | Hülsheger model |
| Weibull model | $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -(t/b)^n$ | log reduction | Empirical fit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The specific energy formula is critical for **industrial scale-up**. Food manufacturers use it to size capacitors, calculate power supply ratings, and design cooling systems. Typical industrial PEF systems operate at **100–500 kJ/kg** for juice pasteurization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Energy Input in a PEF Treatment Chamber

**Problem Context:** Derive the expression for specific energy input (Ws) in terms of electric field strength (E), pulse duration (tp), product conductivity (σ), and product density (ρ).

**Step 1: Define the current density through the food product.**

When an electric field E is applied across a conductive food medium of conductivity σ, the resulting current density J (current per unit area) follows Ohm's law in microscopic form:

$$J = \sigma \cdot E$$

**Step 2: Calculate the power dissipated per unit volume (volumetric power density).**

The power density P (power per unit volume of product) is the product of the electric field and current density (Joule heating analogy):

$$P = E \cdot J$$

Substituting J from Step 1:

$$P = E \cdot (\sigma \cdot E)$$

$$P = \sigma \cdot E^2$$

This represents the instantaneous power dissipated per unit volume of the food product (in W/m³) due to ionic conduction.

**Step 3: Multiply by pulse duration to obtain energy per unit volume.**

For a single pulse of duration tp, the energy dissipated per unit volume of product is:

$$W_v = P \cdot t_p$$

Substituting the expression for P:

$$W_v = \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p$$

This is the energy per unit volume (in J/m³) delivered per pulse.

**Step 4: Multiply by treatment chamber volume to obtain energy per pulse.**

If the treatment chamber has an effective volume v (in m³), then the total energy per pulse is:

$$W_p = W_v \cdot v$$

$$W_p = \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p \cdot v$$

**Step 5: Divide by mass of product to obtain specific energy.**

The mass of product in the chamber is m = ρ · v, where ρ is the density of the food (kg/m³). Therefore, the specific energy (energy per unit mass, in J/kg or kJ/kg) is:

$$W_s = \frac{W_p}{m} = \frac{\sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p \cdot v}{\rho \cdot v}$$

$$\boxed{W_s = \frac{\sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p}{\rho}}$$

**Step 6: Engineering interpretation.**

This equation shows that specific energy scales with the **square of the electric field** — meaning doubling the field quadruples the energy input. This is why PEF systems are designed at the **lowest practical field** combined with **multiple pulses** rather than extreme single-pulse conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Temperature Rise During PEF Treatment

**Step 1: Use energy balance principle.**

The total energy absorbed by the food product is converted almost entirely into heat (since PEF is predominantly ohmic/conductive heating):

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

where Q is the heat absorbed (J), cₚ is the specific heat capacity (J/kg·K), and ΔT is the temperature rise (K or °C).

**Step 2: Equate heat to total PEF energy input.**

For n pulses, the total energy absorbed is:

$$Q = n \cdot W_p = n \cdot \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p \cdot v$$

Alternatively, using specific energy:

$$Q = m \cdot W_s$$

**Step 3: Solve for temperature rise.**

$$m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = m \cdot W_s$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T = \frac{W_s}{c_p} = \frac{\sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p}{\rho \cdot c_p}}$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example.**

Consider apple juice processing: E = 35 kV/cm = 350,000 V/m, tp = 5 μs = 5 × 10⁻⁶ s, σ = 0.4 S/m, ρ = 1050 kg/m³, cₚ = 3800 J/kg·K.

$$W_s = \frac{0.4 \times (350000)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{1050}$$

$$W_s = \frac{0.4 \times 1.225 \times 10^{11} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{1050}$$

$$W_s = \frac{245,000}{1050} \approx 233.3 \text{ J/kg} = 0.233 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{0.233 \times 1000}{3800} \approx 0.06 \text{ °C per pulse}$$

For 10 pulses: ΔT ≈ 0.6 °C. This confirms the **non-thermal** nature of PEF processing.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hülsheger Microbial Inactivation Model

**Step 1: Empirical relationship.**

The log reduction of microbial population in PEF follows:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot E^n \cdot t_p^m$$

where N is the surviving count, N₀ is the initial count, k is the inactivation rate constant, and n, m are empirical exponents.

**Step 2: Taking logarithm of both sides.**

$$\log_{10}\left[-\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right)\right] = \log_{10}(k) + n \cdot \log_{10}(E) + m \cdot \log_{10}(t_p)$$

**Step 3: Linear regression analysis.**

This linearized form allows determination of k, n, and m from experimental data using multiple regression analysis. For *E. coli* in apple juice, typical values are n ≈ 2, m ≈ 0.7, k ≈ 10⁻⁹.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | PEF | Thermal Pasteurization | HPP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature rise | **1–5 °C** | 70–95 °C | 2–5 °C |
| Vitamin C retention | **>90%** | 50–70% | >85% |
| Flavor | Fresh-like | Cooked | Fresh-like |
| Spore inactivation | **No** | Yes | No |
| Energy for juices | 100–500 kJ/kg | 1500–2500 kJ/kg | 400–800 kJ/kg |
| Continuous processing | **Yes** | Yes | No (batch) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PEF System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HVPS[HV DC Power Supply]
    CB[Capacitor Bank]
    SW[High Voltage Switch]
    TC[Treatment Chamber with Electrodes]
    FP[Food Product Inlet and Outlet]
    CS[Cooling Heat Exchanger]
    CTRL[Microcontroller Based Control Unit]
    VM[Voltage and Current Sensor]
    TM[Temperature Sensor]
    DLOG[Data Logger and HMI]

    HVPS --> CB
    CB --> SW
    SW --> TC
    FP --> TC
    TC --> CS
    CS --> FP
    CTRL --> SW
    VM --> TC
    TM --> TC
    VM --> CTRL
    TM --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DLOG
```

### 4.2 Electroporation Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Healthy Microbial Cell]
    B[PEF Pulse Applied 20 to 80 kV per cm]
    C[Trans Membrane Potential Exceeds 1 V]
    D[Hydrophilic Pore Formation]
    E[Reversible Electroporation]
    F[Irreversible Electroporation]
    G[Loss of Membrane Integrity]
    H[Osmotic Swelling and Lysis]
    I[Cell Death and Inactivation]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    E -->|Below critical field| A
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 Treatment Chamber Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONFIG1[Parallel Plate Configuration]
        E1[High Voltage Electrode]
        P1[Food Product Flow]
        E2[Ground Electrode]
        E1 --- P1
        P1 --- E2
    end

    subgraph CONFIG2[Coaxial Configuration]
        IO[Inlet]
        CO[Central Electrode]
        CO2[Outer Electrode]
        OUT[Outlet]
        IO --> CO
        CO --> CO2
        CO2 --> OUT
    end

    subgraph CONFIG3[Collinear Configuration]
        IN1[Inlet]
        EL1[Electrode 1]
        EL2[Electrode 2]
        OUT1[Outlet]
        IN1 --> EL1
        EL1 --> EL2
        EL2 --> OUT1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Scale Reference:** The Ohio State University and Diversified Technologies Inc. (DTI) have commercialized continuous-flow PEF systems with capacities of **500–2000 L/h** for juice pasteurization, operating at **E = 30–50 kV/cm**, **tₚ = 2–4 μs**, and **n = 20–50 pulses**.

### 4.4 Functional Architecture of PEF Process Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[System Initialization]
    SETP[Operator Sets E, tp, n, T, Flow Rate]
    CHARGE[Capacitor Charging Phase]
    CHK[Check Voltage Reached Setpoint]
    PULSE[Trigger Pulse to Chamber]
    MON[Monitor Current Waveform]
    COUNT[Increment Pulse Counter]
    DEC{All Pulses Delivered?}
    COOL[Activate Cooling System]
    SAMPLE[Collect Outlet Sample for Quality Check]
    STOP[Shutdown and CIP]
    DONE[Process Complete]

    START --> SETP
    SETP --> CHARGE
    CHARGE --> CHK
    CHK -->|No| CHARGE
    CHK -->|Yes| PULSE
    PULSE --> MON
    MON --> COUNT
    COUNT --> DEC
    DEC -->|No| CHARGE
    DEC -->|Yes| COOL
    COOL --> SAMPLE
    SAMPLE --> STOP
    STOP --> DONE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) processing and state its principle of microbial inactivation.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Structured):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) processing is a non-thermal food preservation method in which short-duration high-voltage pulses (typically 20–80 kV/cm for 1–100 μs) are applied to food placed between two electrodes.

**Principle (2 Marks):**
The applied electric field induces **electroporation** — formation of pores in microbial cell membranes. When the trans-membrane potential exceeds a critical value (≈1 V), the lipid bilayer becomes permeable, leading to loss of cellular contents, osmotic imbalance, and eventual cell death. The process preserves heat-sensitive nutrients and sensory attributes because the temperature rise is only 1–5 °C.

---

#### Question 2: List any four advantages of PEF over conventional thermal pasteurization.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Four points, 0.75 each):**

1. **Retention of Fresh-like Quality:** Vitamin C, flavor compounds, and color are preserved since processing temperature remains below 40 °C.
2. **Short Treatment Time:** Total residence time is milliseconds to seconds, compared to several minutes in thermal processing.
3. **Energy Efficiency:** Specific energy for juice pasteurization is 100–500 kJ/kg versus 1500–2500 kJ/kg for thermal methods.
4. **No Cooked Flavor:** Absence of Maillard and caramelization reactions maintains the natural organoleptic profile.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[Tag: KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**"Explain in detail the principle, equipment, and process parameters of Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) processing. Discuss its major applications in the food industry."**

**Sub-part (a): Principle and Equipment — 7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Principle (3 Marks):**
PEF works on the principle of **electroporation** of biological membranes. When a sufficiently high electric field is applied across a microbial cell, the induced trans-membrane potential causes the phospholipid bilayer to rupture and form hydrophilic pores. The critical field for irreversible pore formation is typically 4–10 kV/cm for vegetative bacteria.

**(ii) Equipment Components (4 Marks):**
The major components of a PEF system are:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| High-voltage DC power supply | Converts AC mains to high DC voltage |
| Energy storage capacitor bank | Stores energy for pulse generation |
| High-voltage switch (spark gap/IGBT) | Releases stored energy as pulse |
| Treatment chamber | Holds food between electrodes (parallel-plate/coaxial) |
| Cooling system | Removes ohmic heat |
| Control and monitoring unit | Sets parameters, monitors waveforms |
| Flow system (pumps, pipes) | Continuous product handling |
| Cleaning-in-Place (CIP) system | Hygiene maintenance |

**Sub-part (b): Process Parameters and Applications — 7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Key Process Parameters (3 Marks):**
- **Electric field strength (E):** 20–80 kV/cm
- **Pulse duration (tₚ):** 1–5 μs typically
- **Number of pulses (n):** 5–100
- **Pulse waveform:** Square wave preferred
- **Treatment temperature:** Below 40 °C
- **Product conductivity (σ):** Affects energy deposition

**(ii) Applications in Food Industry (4 Marks):**

1. **Juice Pasteurization:** Apple, orange, cranberry juices — commercialized by Genesis Juice Corp., USA.
2. **Milk and Dairy:** Cold pasteurization retaining immunoglobulins.
3. **Liquid Egg Processing:** Pasteurization without protein coagulation.
4. **Enhancing Extraction:** Sugar beet, apple pressing — improves yield by 10–30%.
5. **Drying Enhancement:** Pre-treatment for fruits and vegetables reduces drying time.
6. **Wine and Beer Processing:** Control of spoilage microorganisms without heat damage.
7. **Biomass and Algae:** Disruption of microalgae for biofuel extraction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[Tag: KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**"Discuss the factors affecting microbial inactivation by PEF. Derive the expression for specific energy input and temperature rise during PEF treatment."**

**Sub-part (a): Factors Affecting Inactivation — 7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Electric Field Strength (E) — 2 Marks:**
Higher field strength causes greater membrane disruption. Inactivation increases non-linearly with E (Hülsheger model shows E² to E³ dependence). Typical threshold for *E. coli*: 10–15 kV/cm.

**(2) Pulse Duration and Number — 2 Marks:**
Longer pulses and more pulses cause cumulative membrane damage. Square-wave pulses are more efficient than exponential decay.

**(3) Product and Microbial Properties — 2 Marks:**
- Conductivity: Higher σ → more heating, different field distribution
- pH: Acidic conditions synergize with PEF
- Microbial type: Gram-negative bacteria > Gram-positive > Spores (in sensitivity)
- Cell size: Larger cells easier to inactivate
- Growth phase: Log-phase cells most sensitive

**(4) Treatment Temperature — 1 Mark:**
Sub-lethal pre-heating (40–50 °C) enhances PEF inactivation synergistically. Combined with mild thermal pre-treatment, log reduction improves significantly.

**Sub-part (b): Derivation of Specific Energy and Temperature Rise — 7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define current density by Ohm's law:
$$J = \sigma \cdot E$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Power density is:
$$P = E \cdot J = \sigma \cdot E^2$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Energy per unit volume for one pulse:
$$W_v = P \cdot t_p = \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Energy per pulse for chamber volume v:
$$W_p = \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p \cdot v$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Specific energy (Ws = Wₚ/m, m = ρv):
$$W_s = \frac{\sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p}{\rho}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Temperature rise from heat balance Q = m·cₚ·ΔT = m·Ws:
$$\Delta T = \frac{W_s}{c_p} = \frac{\sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_p}{\rho \cdot c_p}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Numerical substitution for apple juice (E=35 kV/cm, tp=5 μs, σ=0.4 S/m, ρ=1050 kg/m³, cₚ=3800 J/kg·K):
$$\Delta T \approx 0.06 \text{ °C per pulse (confirms non-thermal nature)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing E with V:** Students often write "E = V × d" instead of "E = V/d". **Marks deducted for sign errors.**
> 2. **Skipping unit consistency:** In derivation questions, KTU examiners **specifically check** whether units of σ (S/m), E (V/m), tₚ (s), and ρ (kg/m³) are converted correctly. E must be in V/m, not kV/cm, when using SI units.
> 3. **Missing the "non-thermal" justification:** When asked about PEF advantages, students must **explicitly state the temperature rise value** (1–5 °C) and compare with thermal pasteurization (70–95 °C) for full marks.
> 4. **Confusing reversible and irreversible electroporation:** PEF food processing uses **irreversible** electroporation. Marks lost if this distinction is missing.
> 5. **Formula derivation in energy question:** Examiners expect **7–8 explicit steps** with proper algebraic manipulation. Jumping directly to the final formula without intermediate steps results in **2–3 marks deduction**.
> 6. **Spelling errors:** "Electrodeporation" / "Electroporetion" — write **"Electroporation"** correctly.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PEF = Non-thermal processing** using high-voltage pulses (**20–80 kV/cm**, **1–100 μs**)
- ✅ **Principle:** Electroporation of microbial cell membranes
- ✅ **Critical trans-membrane potential:** ≈ 1 V
- ✅ **Electric field formula:** $E = V/d$
- ✅ **Specific energy:** $W_s = \sigma E^2 t_p / \rho$
- ✅ **Temperature rise:** $\Delta T = W_s / c_p$ — confirms non-thermal character (1–5 °C only)
- ✅ **Pulse types:** Exponential decay, Square wave, Monopolar, Bipolar
- ✅ **Square wave > Exponential decay** in microbial inactivation efficiency
- ✅ **Bipolar pulses** reduce electrode corrosion/electrolytic deposition
- ✅ **Spores are highly resistant** — PEF alone cannot inactivate bacterial spores
- ✅ **Industrial uses:** Juice pasteurization, milk, liquid egg, extraction enhancement
- ✅ **Hülsheger model:** $\log(N/N_0) = -k E^n t_p^m$
- ✅ **Gram-negative > Gram-positive > Spores** in susceptibility order
- ✅ **Advantages:** Fresh-like quality, nutrient retention, short time, energy efficient
- ✅ **Limitations:** No spore inactivation, limited to liquid/pumpable foods, electrode corrosion
- ✅ **Major components:** HV power supply, capacitor bank, switch, treatment chamber, control unit

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality and Safety of Thermally Processed Foods: Impact of Thermal Processing on Food Quality Attributes (Colour, Texture, Flavour)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality and Safety of Thermally Processed Foods: Impact of Thermal Processing on Food Quality Attributes (Colour, Texture, Flavour)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Quality of Thermally Processed Foods?

**Quality** of a thermally processed food refers to the composite set of sensory, nutritional, and physico-chemical attributes that determine consumer acceptability and market value. The three most critical sensory attributes that are *most susceptible* to thermal degradation are:

1. **Colour** (visual perception)
2. **Texture** (mouthfeel / mechanical property)
3. **Flavour** (taste + aroma chemistry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "Thermal processing is the application of heat to achieve microbiological safety, but every unit of heat delivered simultaneously drives deteriorative chemical reactions that degrade the quality attributes of the food — colour, texture, and flavour."

## 1.2 The Quality–Safety Conflict

Thermal processing presents a fundamental paradox:

- We **need** heat → to kill pathogens (safety)
- Heat **damages** → colour, texture, flavour (quality)

The engineer's job is to deliver the **minimum thermal load** (F₀ or integrated lethality) that ensures microbial inactivation while keeping quality degradation below the consumer rejection threshold.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Cooking Your Favourite Shirt"**
> Imagine your favourite cotton shirt has dirt (microorganisms). You want to wash it (kill microbes). But if you put it in *boiling water for 3 hours*, the shirt becomes stiff, faded (loss of colour), torn (loss of texture), and smells burnt (off-flavour). The same physics happens to food inside a retort! The shorter the wash time and lower the temperature, the better it survives — but dirtier it stays. This **time-temperature trade-off** is the heart of thermal process optimization.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decimal reduction time | **D** | minutes | Time to reduce microbial population by 90% (1 log) |
| Z-value | **z** | °C | Temperature rise needed to reduce D by 90% |
| Thermal death time | **F** | minutes | Equivalent minutes at reference temperature |
| Reaction rate constant | **k** | min⁻¹ | Rate of quality degradation |
| Activation energy | **Ea** | kJ/mol | Energy barrier for quality loss reaction |
| Reference temperature | **T_ref** | °C | Usually 121.1 °C for sterilization |
| Texture firmness parameter | **F_f** | minutes | Process value for texture degradation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Quality Degradation Kinetics

Quality loss (whether colour fading, texture softening, or flavour destruction) is most often described by **first-order chemical kinetics**:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$$

where:
- $C$ = quality attribute concentration (e.g., pigment concentration, firmness, aroma compound)
- $t$ = time (minutes)
- $k$ = reaction rate constant (min⁻¹)

Integrating gives the classic exponential decay form:

$$C = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

where $C_0$ = initial value of the quality attribute at $t = 0$.

## 2.2 Temperature Dependence — Arrhenius Equation

The rate constant $k$ is *not* constant — it increases with temperature following the **Arrhenius relationship**:

$$k = k_0 \cdot e^{\left(\frac{-E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)}$$

where:
- $k_0$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol or kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Quality reactions typically have **lower activation energies (E_a = 40–120 kJ/mol)** than microbial inactivation (**E_a ≈ 250–350 kJ/mol**). This is the *thermodynamic basis* for the HTST (High Temperature Short Time) advantage — by raising temperature, we disproportionately accelerate microbe kill while only mildly accelerating quality loss.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| First-order quality decay | $C = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$ | Remaining quality fraction |
| Arrhenius rate constant | $k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | How $k$ scales with $T$ |
| Decimal reduction (quality) | $D_{quality} = \frac{2.303}{k}$ | Time for 90% quality loss |
| Z-value (quality) | $z = \frac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T_{ref}^2}{E_a}$ | °C needed to change $k$ by 10× |
| Cook value integration | $C_v = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\frac{T(t)-T_{ref}}{z}} dt$ | Cumulative thermal effect on quality |
| Quality retention | $Q_{ret} = e^{-k_{ref} \cdot C_v}$ | Fraction of quality remaining |
| Texture softening (Arrhenius) | $\ln \frac{F_0}{F_1} = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Texture loss vs temperature |
| Maillard browning rate | $\text{Browning Index} = k_{M} \cdot t$ at constant T | Colour loss |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In tables, *never* write $|x|$ using the `|` symbol — it breaks markdown parsing. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX form.

## 2.4 The Process Value Concept (Cook Value $C_v$)

For a variable retort temperature profile $T(t)$, the **cook value** integrates the thermal damage experienced by quality:

$$C_v = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\left(\frac{T(t) - T_{ref}}{z_{quality}}\right)} dt$$

This is the *quality-side* equivalent of the lethality $F_0$. By choosing $z_{quality}$ (typically 25–35 °C for most quality reactions) versus $z_{microbe}$ (≈ 10 °C), we quantify the differential damage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aseptic processing of milk**: Uses HTST to preserve whiteness and fresh-milk flavour while ensuring safety.
- **UHT vs in-container sterilization**: UHT gives 6 months shelf life with far better colour/flavour.
- **Sous-vide cooking**: Operates precisely at the boundary where pathogens are killed but texture/colour remain "restaurant-fresh."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Quality Loss and D-value Relationship

We start from the differential rate law for first-order quality loss:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$$

**Step 1** — Separate variables:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 2** — Integrate from initial concentration $C_0$ to final $C$, and from time $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3** — Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln C - \ln C_0 = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 4** — Exponentiate both sides:

$$C = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

**Step 5** — Take natural log form:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 6** — For the D-value definition (time to achieve $C/C_0 = 0.1$, i.e., 90% loss):

$$\ln(0.1) = -k \cdot D_{quality}$$

$$\text{Since } \ln(0.1) = -2.303$$

$$-2.303 = -k \cdot D_{quality}$$

$$\boxed{D_{quality} = \frac{2.303}{k}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Z-value from Arrhenius

**Step 1** — Take natural log of Arrhenius:

$$\ln k = \ln k_0 - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2** — Write for two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\ln k_1 = \ln k_0 - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T_1}$$

$$\ln k_2 = \ln k_0 - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T_2}$$

**Step 3** — Subtract to eliminate $\ln k_0$:

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{(T_2 - T_1)}{T_1 \cdot T_2}$$

**Step 4** — Z-value is defined by $k_2/k_1 = 10$ when $T_2 - T_1 = z$. Substitute:

$$\ln(10) = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{z}{T_{ref}^2}$$

**Step 5** — Use $\ln(10) = 2.303$:

$$2.303 = \frac{E_a \cdot z}{R \cdot T_{ref}^2}$$

$$\boxed{z = \frac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T_{ref}^2}{E_a}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Higher $E_a$ ⇒ smaller $z$ ⇒ reaction is more temperature-sensitive. Microbial death has high $E_a$ → small $z$ ≈ 10 °C. Quality loss has lower $E_a$ → larger $z$ ≈ 25–35 °C. The gap is your optimization window.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Texture Softening with Temperature

For pectin/structural softening in vegetables, the firmness loss follows first-order kinetics with Arrhenius temperature dependence.

**Step 1** — Rate constant at two temperatures:

$$k_1 = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT_1}, \quad k_2 = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT_2}$$

**Step 2** — Ratio:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3** — Substitute into $C = C_0 e^{-kt}$ and equate quality remaining $C/C_0$ at two process temperatures:

$$\frac{k_2 \cdot t_2}{k_1 \cdot t_1} = 1 \text{ (same quality remaining)}$$

**Step 4** — Therefore:

$$\ln\left(\frac{t_1}{t_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{F_0}{F_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

This is the **process-value ratio** used to compute equivalent texture damage at two retort temperatures.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Heat Effect on Each Quality Attribute

| Quality Attribute | Major Reaction | Typical E_a (kJ/mol) | Typical z (°C) | Visual / Sensory Manifestation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Colour** (green vegetables) | Chlorophyll → Pheophytin | 70–90 | 28–35 | Olive-drab / dull brown |
| **Colour** (red fruits) | Anthocyanin hydrolysis | 50–70 | 30–40 | Purple → brown |
| **Colour** (Maillard browning) | Maillard reaction | 100–160 | 22–28 | Golden → dark brown |
| **Texture** (vegetables) | Pectin β-elimination | 60–90 | 25–35 | Crisp → mushy |
| **Texture** (meat) | Collagen → gelatin | 80–120 | 25–30 | Tough → tender |
| **Flavour** (volatiles) | Loss of esters, aldehydes | 30–60 | 25–40 | "Cooked" off-notes |
| **Flavour** (off-flavour formation) | Lipid oxidation, Strecker | 80–140 | 20–28 | Rancid / cardboard |
| **Nutrients** (Vitamin C) | Ascorbic acid oxidation | 80–110 | 25–30 | Reduced nutritional value |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Cook Value Calculation

**Problem:** A thermal process holds the product at 110 °C for 35 min and at 115 °C for 15 min. Reference temperature $T_{ref} = 100$ °C. Quality z-value = 28 °C. Find the cook value $C_v$.

**Step 1** — Compute temperature differences:

$$\Delta T_1 = 110 - 100 = 10\ °C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = 115 - 100 = 15\ °C$$

**Step 2** — Compute exponential factors:

$$10^{10/28} = 10^{0.3571} = 2.276$$

$$10^{15/28} = 10^{0.5357} = 3.434$$

**Step 3** — Sum contributions:

$$C_v = (2.276)(35) + (3.434)(15)$$

$$C_v = 79.66 + 51.51$$

$$\boxed{C_v = 131.17 \text{ min}}$$

This means the product has experienced the equivalent of **131.17 minutes at 100 °C** in terms of quality degradation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Heat-Induced Quality Degradation Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Food] --> B[Thermal Processing]
    B --> C{Quality Attribute}
    C --> D1[COLOUR LOSS]
    C --> D2[TEXTURE LOSS]
    C --> D3[FLAVOUR LOSS]
    D1 --> E1[Chlorophyll to Pheophytin]
    D1 --> E2[Maillard Browning]
    D1 --> E3[Anthocyanin Breakdown]
    D2 --> F1[Pectin Beta-Elimination]
    D2 --> F2[Collagen Gelatinisation]
    D2 --> F3[Starch Gelatinisation]
    D3 --> G1[Volatile Stripping]
    D3 --> G2[Lipid Oxidation]
    D3 --> G3[Maillard Strecker Aldehydes]
    E1 --> H1[Olive Drab Veg]
    E2 --> H2[Brown Crust]
    E3 --> H3[Bleached Reds]
    F1 --> I1[Mushy Texture]
    F2 --> I2[Tender Meat]
    F3 --> I3[Thickened Sauce]
    G1 --> J1[Cooked Cabbage Odour]
    G2 --> J2[Rancid Notes]
    G3 --> J3[Malty Off-Flavours]
    H1 --> K[Final Sensory Quality]
    H2 --> K
    H3 --> K
    I1 --> K
    I2 --> K
    I3 --> K
    J1 --> K
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The Quality-Safety Optimization Window

```mermaid
    A --> C[Quality Loss Rate]
    B --> D[High Ea ~ 300 kJ/mol]

    D --> F[Small z ~ 10 °C]
    E --> G[Larger z ~ 30 °C]
    F --> H[HTST Advantage Zone]
    G --> H
    H --> I[High T, Short t]
    I --> J[Max Safety + Min Quality Loss]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Thermal Process Time-Temperature Trade-off

```mermaid

    A[Start: Cold Pack Food] --> B[Come-up Time]
    B --> C[Holding Phase at T_proc]
    C --> D{F0 achieved?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Cool-down]
    E --> F[Final Quality State]
    F --> G{Quality Acceptable?}
    G -- No --> H[Reject Batch or Re-engineer]
    G -- Yes --> I[Release to Market]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Colour Degradation Pathway Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Green Chlorophyll] -->|H+ and heat| B[Pheophytin olive]
    A -->|Mg2+ replaced by Zn2+| C[Pheophytin a]
    B --> D[Brown Pheophytin]
    C --> D
    E[Red Anthocyanin] -->|Hydrolysis| F[Brown Sugar Complex]
    G[Reducing Sugar] -->|Amino acid| H[Maillard Intermediates]
    H --> I[Melanoidins Dark Brown]
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture — Thermal Quality Impact Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input: Raw Food Material] --> P2[Heat Transfer Module]
    P2 --> P3[Microbial Inactivation Engine]
    P2 --> P4[Quality Degradation Engine]
    P3 --> P5[Safety Outcome F0]
    P4 --> P6[Quality Outcome Cv]
    P5 --> P7[Decision Matrix]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 -->|Accept| P8[Release]
    P7 -->|Reject| P9[Process Redesign]
    P4 --> Q1[Colour Sensor Channel]
    P4 --> Q2[Texture Sensor Channel]
    P4 --> Q3[Flavour Sensor Channel]
    Q1 --> R1[Chlorophyll Loss Tracker]
    Q2 --> R2[Firmness Kinetic Tracker]
    Q3 --> R3[Volatile Loss Tracker]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Cook Value ($C_v$). Why is it used in thermal processing instead of lethality $F_0$?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cook Value ($C_v$)** is the integrated time-temperature exposure of a food product, expressed as equivalent minutes at a reference temperature $T_{ref}$, calculated with the quality-attribute z-value (typically 25–35 °C):
>
> $$C_v = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\left(\frac{T(t) - T_{ref}}{z_{quality}}\right)} dt$$
>
> **Why used instead of $F_0$?**
> 1. $F_0$ uses $z = 10$ °C, optimized for microbial kill.
> 2. Quality reactions have $z \approx 25$–35 °C.
> 3. $C_v$ quantifies *cooking damage* — the real driver of consumer-acceptable quality.
> 4. It enables HTST process optimization: maximize $F_0$ while minimizing $C_v$.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why does pasteurized milk retain colour and flavour better than in-bottle sterilized milk even though both are thermally processed?**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Lower temperature, shorter time:** Pasteurization uses 72 °C × 15 s (HTST) vs sterilization at 121 °C × 15 min.
> 2. **Lower cook value ($C_v$):** Pasteurization gives $C_v$ of < 1 min equivalent; sterilization gives $C_v$ > 60 min.
> 3. **Reduced Maillard browning:** Lower temperature → less lysine–lactose reaction → no brown colour.
> 4. **Less volatile stripping:** Lower temperature → fresh-milk flavour compounds (aldehydes, methyl ketones) preserved.
> 5. **Same safety outcome:** Both achieve 5-log reduction of pathogens.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the kinetics of colour degradation in thermally processed green vegetables. Discuss the role of pH and metal ions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A green pea puree is processed at 121 °C for 6 minutes. The chlorophyll degradation follows first-order kinetics with $k = 0.18 \text{ min}^{-1}$ at 121 °C and $E_a = 75 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Calculate the equivalent processing time at 115 °C that would produce the same chlorophyll loss. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Pigment chemistry:**
The green colour of vegetables is from **chlorophyll a** and **chlorophyll b**, both containing a central **Mg²⁺ ion** coordinated within a porphyrin ring.

**Step 2 — Primary degradation reaction:**
During thermal processing, heat + H⁺ (from any acidification) displaces Mg²⁺ from the porphyrin ring:

$$\text{Chlorophyll} \xrightarrow{H^+,\ \Delta} \text{Pheophytin (olive-brown)} + \text{Mg}^{2+}$$

This reaction is **first-order** in chlorophyll concentration:

$$\frac{d[\text{Chl}]}{dt} = -k[\text{Chl}]$$

**Step 3 — Role of pH:**
- **Low pH (< 5):** Pheophytin formation is fast → olive/brown discolouration.
- **Neutral pH (6.5–7):** Magnesium is retained longer → green colour preserved.
- **Alkaline pH (> 8):** Chlorophyll converts to **chlorophyllin** (bright green, water-soluble) — used commercially in pea processing.

**Step 4 — Role of metal ions:**
- **Zn²⁺ or Cu²⁺ ions** can replace Mg²⁺ → form **Zn-pheophytin** or **Cu-pheophytin** which are *more colour-stable* (used in canning industry).
- High concentrations of Na⁺, K⁺ accelerate Mg²⁺ displacement.

**Step 5 — Industrial mitigation:**
- Blanching before canning destroys the cell-wall-bound acids and releases them, preventing chlorophyll → pheophytin.
- Addition of **MgCO₃** or **NaHCO₃** to the brine maintains pH 7–8.
- HTST processing reduces overall cook value.

**Conclusion:** Colour loss is governed by acid-catalysed Mg²⁺ loss from chlorophyll, follow first-order kinetics, and is mitigated by pH control and metal-ion substitution. **(7 marks fully addressed)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 121\ °C = 394.15\ \text{K}$
- $T_2 = 115\ °C = 388.15\ \text{K}$
- $t_1 = 6\ \text{min}$
- $E_a = 75{,}000\ \text{J/mol}$
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1 — Equal chlorophyll loss condition:**
$$k_1 \cdot t_1 = k_2 \cdot t_2$$

**Step 2 — Arrhenius ratio:**
$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inverse temperature difference:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{394.15} - \frac{1}{388.15}$$

$$= 0.0025372 - 0.0025765 = -3.93 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute exponent:**

$$\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{75000}{8.314}\times (-3.93\times 10^{-5})$$

$$= 9020.7 \times (-3.93\times 10^{-5}) = -0.3544$$

**Step 5 — Compute ratio:**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{-0.3544} = 0.7016$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $t_2$:**

$$t_2 = t_1 \cdot \frac{k_1}{k_2} = 6 \times \frac{1}{0.7016}$$

$$\boxed{t_2 = 8.55\ \text{minutes at } 115\ °C}$$

**Interpretation:** To achieve the same chlorophyll loss, a 5 °C drop in temperature requires ~43% longer processing time. This illustrates why temperature control is more effective than time control in preserving colour.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the major flavour changes in thermally processed foods. Differentiate between desirable and undesirable flavour development. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mango pulp thermal process gives $F_0 = 8\ \text{min}$. The vitamin C destruction has $E_a = 90\ \text{kJ/mol}$ and $D_{121\ °C} = 40\ \text{min}$. Estimate the percentage of vitamin C retained after the process. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Classification of flavour changes:**

Flavour in thermally processed food is the balance between **desirable aroma development** and **undesirable off-flavour formation**.

**Step 2 — Desirable flavour development:**
- **Maillard reaction:** Forms pyrazines, furans, thiophenes → roasted, nutty, meaty notes (good in coffee, bread crust, roasted meat).
- **Caramelization:** Sugars → diacetyl, furfural, hydroxymethylfurfural → sweet, buttery, toffee notes.
- **Lipid-derived aroma:** Lipid–Maillard interaction produces 2-alkylpyridines and thiophenes → cooked-meat flavour.

**Step 3 — Undesirable flavour development:**
- **Strecker degradation:** Amino acids → aldehydes (e.g., methional from methionine → "potato off-flavour").
- **Lipid oxidation:** Aldehydes like hexanal, 2-nonenal → rancid, cardboard off-flavours.
- **Chlorophyll → pheophytin:** Vegetable off-notes.
- **Over-cooked sulphur compounds:** H₂S, dimethyl sulphide → "over-cooked cabbage" aroma.
- **Loss of fresh volatiles:** Top notes (esters, terpenes) are stripped → "cooked" / flat character.

**Step 4 — Quantitative link:**

| Reaction Type | E_a (kJ/mol) | Effect on Flavour |
|---|---|---|
| Volatile stripping (physical loss) | 20–40 | Loss of fresh top notes |
| Maillard (caramelization) | 100–160 | Desirable browning aroma |
| Strecker | 80–120 | Off-flavour aldehydes |
| Lipid oxidation | 70–100 | Rancidity |

**Step 5 — Optimization strategy:**
- Use **HTST** to retain fresh volatiles.
- Control **a_w** and **pH** to suppress oxidation.
- Add **antioxidants** (BHA, BHT, rosemary extract) in lipid-rich foods.
- **In-container sterilization** develops more cooked flavour than **aseptic UHT**.

**Conclusion:** Thermal flavour change is a balance; HTST optimizes for "fresh-cooked" perception, while in-container sterilization favours "well-developed" character. **(7 marks fully addressed)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:**
- $F_0 = 8\ \text{min}$
- $E_a = 90{,}000\ \text{J/mol}$
- $D_{121} = 40\ \text{min}$ (decimal reduction time for vitamin C at 121 °C reference)

**Step 1 — Calculate rate constant k at reference temperature:**

$$D = \frac{2.303}{k_{121}}$$

$$k_{121} = \frac{2.303}{40} = 0.05758\ \text{min}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply first-order decay for the F₀ process:**

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = 10^{-F_0/D}$$

$$= 10^{-8/40}$$

$$= 10^{-0.2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$10^{-0.2} = 0.6310$$

$$\boxed{C/C_0 = 63.10\% \text{ vitamin C retained}}$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity check at a different reference:**

If the actual process is at 110 °C, the equivalent $F_{110}$ is:

$$F_{110} = F_0 \cdot 10^{(121.1 - 110)/10} = 8 \times 10^{1.11} = 8 \times 12.88 = 103\ \text{min}$$

Now $D_{110} = D_{121} \cdot 10^{(110-121)/z}$. Assuming $z \approx 30$ °C (typical for nutrients):

$$D_{110} = 40 \cdot 10^{-11/30} = 40 \times 0.524 = 20.95\ \text{min}$$

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = 10^{-103/20.95} = 10^{-4.92} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$$

So at 110 °C the retention would be near zero — illustrating the **HTST benefit** for heat-sensitive vitamins.

**Conclusion:** ~63% of vitamin C is retained in the reference process, but a cooler alternative temperature would devastate retention. The HTST principle is therefore essential for nutrient preservation. **(7 marks fully addressed)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion on $E_a$:** Mixing kJ/mol and J/mol in the same calculation. Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **Temperature scale:** Using °C in Arrhenius instead of K. ALWAYS convert: $T(\text{K}) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
> 3. **Sign error in Arrhenius ratio:** Writing $\exp[(E_a/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)]$ instead of the correct $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
> 4. **Forgetting the difference between $z_{microbe}$ and $z_{quality}$:** Quality has 25–35 °C; microbes have ~10 °C. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Calling $D_{quality}$ the same as $D_{microbe}$:** They share the equation $D = 2.303/k$ but represent fundamentally different phenomena.
> 6. **Ignoring the come-up and cool-down phases:** These contribute to $F_0$ and $C_v$ and must be integrated, not skipped.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three quality pillars:** Colour, Texture, Flavour — all degrade via **first-order kinetics** dominated by the **Arrhenius equation**.
- **Quality $E_a$ < Microbial $E_a$:** The thermodynamic foundation of HTST processing.
- **Cook Value $C_v$** uses $z_{quality}$ (~28 °C); **Lethality $F_0$** uses $z_{microbe}$ (~10 °C). Both integrate temperature history.
- **Colour:**
  - Green vegetables: Chlorophyll → Pheophytin (acid-catalysed Mg²⁺ loss). Mitigate with pH control, MgCO₃ blanching, or Zn²⁺/Cu²⁺ substitution.
  - Maillard browning is desirable in bread/meat, undesirable in milk/white sauces.
- **Texture:**
  - Vegetables soften by **pectin β-elimination**.
  - Meat tenderises by **collagen → gelatin** (hydrolysis).
  - Higher temperature = softer texture (irreversible for vegetables, desired for meat).
- **Flavour:**
  - HTST retains "fresh" top-note volatiles.
  - Maillard and caramelization produce desirable aromas at higher temperatures.
  - Strecker degradation and lipid oxidation produce off-flavours.
- **Numerical tools:**
  - $D = 2.303 / k$
  - $z = 2.303 \, R \, T_{ref}^2 / E_a$
  - $C_v = \int 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z_{quality}} dt$
  - $C/C_0 = e^{-kt} = 10^{-t/D}$
- **Engineering mantra:** *"HTST — High Temperature, Short Time — saves colour, flavour, and nutrients while delivering microbial safety."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nutritional Changes Due to Thermal Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT616 - THERMAL PROCESSING OF FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nutritional Changes Due to Thermal Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nutritional Changes Due to Thermal Processing** refers to the physicochemical, biochemical, and structural alterations occurring in the macronutrients (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) and micronutrients (vitamins, minerals, pigments) of a food matrix when subjected to controlled heat treatment (blanching, pasteurization, sterilization, extrusion, baking, frying, etc.) for the purpose of preservation.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for **Thermal Processing of Food (OEFTT616)**, this topic is positioned as the bridge between *food microbiology/safety* (killing pathogens) and *food quality* (retaining nutritional value). The fundamental engineering challenge is to apply sufficient thermal lethality ($F_0$, $D$-value, $z$-value) to ensure microbial inactivation **while** minimizing the degradation rate of quality nutrients.

### 1.2 Key Nutrient Categories Affected

| Nutrient Class | Representative Components | Thermal Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| Proteins | Globular proteins, enzymes, amino acids | **Moderate to High** (denaturation, Maillard) |
| Carbohydrates | Starch, sugars, dietary fiber | **Low to Moderate** (gelatinization, browning) |
| Lipids | Triglycerides, phospholipids, PUFA | **High** (oxidation, hydrolysis) |
| Water-soluble vitamins | Vitamin C (ascorbic acid), B-complex (thiamine, folate) | **Very High** (heat-labile) |
| Fat-soluble vitamins | Vitamin A, D, E, K | **Moderate** |
| Pigments | Chlorophyll, anthocyanins, carotenoids, betalains | **High** (decolorization) |
| Minerals | Ca, Fe, K, Mg, trace elements | **Low** (mostly leach losses) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Cooking Tightrope"**
> Imagine you are walking on a tightrope between two cliffs. The **left cliff** represents *food safety* (raw, microbiologically dangerous food); the **right cliff** represents *food waste/over-cooking* (sterile but nutritionally empty food). Thermal processing is the tightrope walker, and the **rope** is the time-temperature profile. Every food nutrient is a tightrope walker too — some are stable (minerals — they can walk confidently), while others (Vitamin C, thiamine) are like clumsy walkers who need a much wider, gentler rope. The food engineer's job is to design a rope (a thermal process) that is steep enough to kill microbes but gentle enough for all nutrients to survive the crossing.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-frequency** KTU Module 4 question. Examiners frequently test:
> - The **order of heat sensitivity** among vitamins.
> - The difference between **Maillard browning** and **caramelization**.
> - The mechanism of **protein denaturation vs. protein hydrolysis**.
> - The **first-order kinetics** of nutrient degradation.
> - How **pH, water activity ($a_w$), and oxygen** modulate nutrient loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Effect on Proteins

#### 2.1.1 Denaturation
- Proteins unfold from their native tertiary/quaternary structure when heated above a critical temperature (typically **60–90 °C** for most globular proteins).
- The **primary peptide bonds remain intact** — only the 3D architecture collapses.
- Denaturation is **reversible at low temperatures** but becomes **irreversible** above a threshold due to coagulation and disulfide cross-linking.
- *Example:* Egg-white albumin (ovalbumin) coagulates at ~62 °C; meat myosin coagulates at ~40–60 °C.

#### 2.1.2 Hydrolysis
- Prolonged heating in the presence of water cleaves peptide bonds, releasing free amino acids and peptides.
- *Benefit:* Increases digestibility and bioavailability (e.g., heated soy protein is more digestible than raw soy, which contains trypsin inhibitors).

#### 2.1.3 Maillard Reaction (with reducing sugars)
- Carbonyl group of a reducing sugar (glucose, lactose) reacts with the free ε-amino group of **lysine**, producing Schiff bases → Amadori compounds → melanoidins (brown pigments).
- *Nutritional consequence:* **Lysine bioavailability is destroyed** because the ε-amino group is no longer free.
- Critical in milk processing (lactose + milk proteins), baking, and sterilization of infant formula.

#### 2.1.4 Cystine & Lysine Destruction
- **Lysine** is the most heat-sensitive essential amino acid (free ε-NH₂ group).
- **Cystine** destruction releases H₂S, producing off-flavors in overheated milk ("cooked" flavor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** The order of amino acid heat sensitivity (most to least) is:
> **Lysine > Cystine > Methionine > Tryptophan**

### 2.2 Effect on Carbohydrates

#### 2.2.1 Starch Gelatinization
- Heating starch in water above **60–70 °C** disrupts crystalline regions, allowing water absorption and granule swelling.
- On cooling, amylose retrogrades, forming gels — the basis of pudding and sauce texture.
- *Nutritional impact:* Gelatinized starch is **more digestible** (higher glycemic response) than raw starch.

#### 2.2.2 Caramelization
- Direct thermal degradation of sugars (no amino acids required) at temperatures **> 160 °C** (sucrose) or **> 100 °C** (fructose).
- Produces brown pigments and flavor compounds (diacetyl, furfural).
- *Nutritional impact:* Sugar is consumed, but no essential nutrient is destroyed directly.

#### 2.2.3 Maillard Browning (non-enzymatic)
- See Section 2.1.3. Optimum at **pH 4–7** and **$a_w$ 0.6–0.8**.
- Major concern in **baked goods, UHT milk, evaporated milk, infant cereals**.

#### 2.2.4 Hydrolysis of Polysaccharides
- Acidic or thermal hydrolysis breaks glycosidic bonds → oligosaccharides → monosaccharides.
- *Example:* Inversion of sucrose to glucose + fructose during jam making.

#### 2.2.5 Pectin Solubilization
- Heating pectin in acidic conditions (pH < 3.5) causes β-elimination (trans-elimination), breaking glycosidic linkages → loss of gel strength in over-processed jams.

### 2.3 Effect on Lipids

#### 2.3.1 Hydrolysis
- Heat + moisture cleave triglycerides into **free fatty acids (FFA)** and **glycerol**.
- FFA cause **rancid flavors** and accelerate oxidation.

#### 2.3.2 Oxidation (Lipid Peroxidation)
- Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA) react with O₂ via free-radical chain mechanism:
  - **Initiation:** RH + O₂ → R• + •OOH
  - **Propagation:** R• + O₂ → ROO• → ROOH + R•
  - **Termination:** ROO• + ROO• → stable products
- Produces **hydroperoxides** → secondary oxidation products (aldehydes, ketones) → **off-flavors, off-odors, toxic compounds**.
- *Critical nutrient loss:* **Essential fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic)** and **fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)**.

#### 2.3.3 Polymerization
- At very high frying temperatures (>180 °C), oxidized lipids polymerize into cyclic compounds, raising viscosity and reducing digestibility.

### 2.4 Effect on Vitamins

#### 2.4.1 Heat Stability Ranking (Most → Least Heat Labile)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order (most to least heat-labile):**
> **Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid) > Thiamine (B₁) > Folic acid > Vitamin A > Vitamin D > Vitamin E > Vitamin K > Vitamin B₂ (Riboflavin) > Niacin (B₃) > Biotin**

#### 2.4.2 Detailed Vitamin Behavior

| Vitamin | Form | Thermal Behavior | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vitamin C** | Water-soluble | Oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) → diketogulonic acid. **Aerobic destruction >> anaerobic.** | Most labile. Loss: 50–100% in HTST pasteurization of juice. |
| **Thiamine (B₁)** | Water-soluble | Cleaved by heat, especially in **alkaline** pH. | Critical loss in bread baking, milk heating. |
| **Riboflavin (B₂)** | Water-soluble | Relatively heat-stable but **light-sensitive**. | Loses very little in pasteurization. |
| **Niacin (B₃)** | Water-soluble | Very heat-stable. | No significant loss in normal processing. |
| **Folic acid** | Water-soluble | Heat-labile, especially in acidic pH. | Loss in UHT milk up to 30%. |
| **Vitamin A** | Fat-soluble | Isomerized (trans-cis) and oxidized; fat matrix protects. | Loss accelerated in fried foods with repeated oil use. |
| **Vitamin E (Tocopherol)** | Fat-soluble | Oxidized; antioxidant role self-sacrifices to protect PUFA. | Loss parallels PUFA oxidation. |

#### 2.4.3 Generalized Vitamin Degradation Kinetics
Most vitamin losses follow **first-order kinetics** (see Section 3 for derivation).

### 2.5 Effect on Pigments

| Pigment | Color | Thermal Effect | Chemistry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chlorophyll** | Green | Converted to **pheophytin** (olive-brown) | Mg²⁺ replaced by 2 H⁺ at high heat, acidic pH |
| **Anthocyanins** | Red/Blue/Purple | Hydrolyzed; brownish discoloration | Cleaved at C-3, sensitive to pH, O₂, SO₂ |
| **Carotenoids** | Yellow/Orange | **Isomerization** (trans → cis) and oxidation | Loss of color and provitamin A activity |
| **Betalains** | Red/Yellow (beets) | Less heat-labile than anthocyanins | Stable over a wide pH range |
| **Myoglobin/Hemoglobin** | Red meat | Converted to **metmyoglobin** (brown) | Oxidation of Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺; further heat → hemichrome |

### 2.6 Effect on Minerals

- Minerals themselves are **thermally stable** (no chemical change).
- **Real loss is by leaching** into processing water (blanching water, brine, syrup).
- *Example:* Blanching spinach loses 30–40% of its K and Mg into the water.

### 2.7 Effect on Enzymes

- Most enzymes are inactivated at **70–90 °C** (e.g., peroxidase, polyphenol oxidase, lipoxygenase).
- *Residual* enzyme activity in under-blanched vegetables causes post-processing quality loss (off-flavors, browning).
- *Peroxidase* is used as a **blanching adequacy indicator** (one of the most heat-resistant enzymes).

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 ESE high-yield. Memorize units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -kC$ | First-order nutrient degradation rate | — |
| 2 | $C_t = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$ | First-order decay integrated form | — |
| 3 | $\ln \left( \dfrac{C_t}{C_0} \right) = -kt$ | Logarithmic form for plotting | — |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{1}{t} \ln \left( \dfrac{C_0}{C_t} \right)$ | Rate constant from concentration data | time⁻¹ |
| 5 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Half-life of nutrient | time |
| 6 | $\% \, \text{Retention} = \left( \dfrac{C_t}{C_0} \right) \times 100$ | Nutrient retention percentage | % |
| 7 | $\ln \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)$ | Arrhenius equation for rate constant | — |
| 8 | $D = \dfrac{2.303}{k}$ | Decimal reduction time (90% loss time) | time |
| 9 | $\log \left( \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} \right) = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$ | $z$-value relation (analogous to microbial inactivation) | °C |
| 10 | $Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature quotient for every 10 °C rise | — |

**Boundary Conditions / Assumptions:**
- Reactions are **first-order** in nutrient concentration (works well for vitamins, pigments).
- **$E_a$** for typical nutrient degradation: **80–150 kJ/mol**.
- **$z$** for nutrient loss typically **20–40 °C** (vs. **5–10 °C** for microbes), meaning nutrients are less temperature-sensitive than microbes — this is the *entire basis* of HTST pasteurization succeeding without major nutrient loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Nutrient Degradation Kinetics

**Statement:** Most thermal nutrient losses (vitamin C, thiamine, color pigments) follow a first-order rate law, where the rate of nutrient loss is proportional to its instantaneous concentration.

**Step 1 — Write the differential rate expression.**
The rate of decrease in nutrient concentration $C$ with time $t$ at constant temperature is given by:

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \, C
$$

where:
- $C$ = nutrient concentration at time $t$ (mg/100g or %)
- $k$ = first-order rate constant (min⁻¹ or s⁻¹)
- Negative sign indicates a decreasing concentration.

**Step 2 — Separate variables for integration.**

$$
\frac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the limits.**
Integrate from initial concentration $C_0$ at $t = 0$ to concentration $C_t$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
\left[ \ln C \right]_{C_0}^{C_t} = -k \, t
$$

$$
\ln C_t - \ln C_0 = -k \, t
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to standard form.**

$$
\ln \left( \frac{C_t}{C_0} \right) = -k \, t
$$

Equivalently, in exponential form:

$$
C_t = C_0 \, e^{-k \, t}
$$

**Step 6 — Derive the half-life.**
Half-life is the time for $C_t = C_0 / 2$:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{C_0/2}{C_0} \right) = -k \, t_{1/2}
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) = -k \, t_{1/2}
$$

$$
-0.693 = -k \, t_{1/2}
$$

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k}
$$

**Step 7 — Derive the decimal reduction time $D$.**
$D$ is the time required for a 10-fold (90%) loss, i.e., $C_t = 0.1 \, C_0$:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{0.1 \, C_0}{C_0} \right) = -k \, D
$$

$$
-2.303 = -k \, D
$$

$$
D = \frac{2.303}{k}
$$

**Step 8 — Derive percentage retention formula.**

$$
\% \, \text{Retention} = \left( \frac{C_t}{C_0} \right) \times 100
$$

From first-order decay:

$$
\% \, \text{Retention} = e^{-k \, t} \times 100
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Equation for Nutrient Loss

**Statement:** The rate constant $k$ varies with absolute temperature $T$ according to the Arrhenius equation.

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius form.**

$$
k = A \, e^{-\frac{E_a}{R \, T}}
$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$
\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R \, T}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the equation at two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$.**

$$
\ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R \, T_1}
$$

$$
\ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R \, T_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two equations (eliminates $\ln A$).**

$$
\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Final form (used in KTU problems).**

$$
\ln \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = -\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

A plot of $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ gives a **straight line** with slope $-E_a/R$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — $z$-Value Relation for Nutrients

**Statement:** Analogous to microbial thermal death time, nutrient degradation follows a logarithmic $D$-vs-$T$ relationship.

**Step 1 — Take $\log_{10}$ of the first-order equation.**

$$
\log \left( \frac{C_0}{C_t} \right) = \frac{k \, t}{2.303}
$$

**Step 2 — Define $D$ as the time for $C_0 / C_t = 10$.**

$$
D = \frac{2.303}{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the $z$-value definition.**
The $z$-value is the temperature rise (in °C) required to reduce $D$ by a factor of 10:

$$
\frac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{\frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}}
$$

**Step 4 — Take $\log_{10}$.**

$$
\log \left( \frac{D_1}{D_2} \right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}
$$

**Step 5 — Final usable form.**

$$
z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log (D_1 / D_2)}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

#### Example 1: Vitamin C Loss in Pasteurized Juice (3-Mark Style)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – June 2024):**
> Orange juice contains 40 mg/100 mL of Vitamin C initially. After HTST pasteurization (90 °C, 30 s), the Vitamin C reduces to 32 mg/100 mL. Calculate the rate constant $k$ and the half-life of Vitamin C at this temperature.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use first-order equation.**

$$
k = \frac{1}{t} \ln \left( \frac{C_0}{C_t} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
k = \frac{1}{30} \ln \left( \frac{40}{32} \right)
$$

$$
k = \frac{1}{30} \times \ln(1.25)
$$

$$
k = \frac{1}{30} \times 0.2231
$$

$$
k = 7.44 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute half-life.**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k} = \frac{0.693}{7.44 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
t_{1/2} = 93.1 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify % retention.**

$$
\% \text{Retention} = \frac{32}{40} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

#### Example 2: Arrhenius Energy Calculation (14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:**
> The rate constant for thiamine destruction in milk is $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at 100 °C and $6.0 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹ at 120 °C. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_1 = 100 + 273 = 373 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 120 + 273 = 393 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius equation.**

$$
\ln \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = -\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the left side.**

$$
\ln \left( \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} \right) = \ln(2.4) = 0.8755
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the temperature term.**

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{393} - \frac{1}{373}
$$

$$
= 0.002545 - 0.002681 = -1.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and solve for $E_a$.**

$$
0.8755 = -\frac{E_a}{8.314} \times (-1.36 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
0.8755 = \frac{E_a \times 1.36 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314}
$$

$$
E_a = \frac{0.8755 \times 8.314}{1.36 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
E_a = \frac{7.279}{1.36 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
E_a = 53{,}522 \text{ J/mol} \approx 53.5 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

This value falls within the typical $E_a$ range of **80–120 kJ/mol** for many vitamins, indicating thiamine in this system is somewhat less temperature-sensitive than the average.

#### Example 3: Nutrient vs. Microbial $D$-value Comparison

> **Problem:**
> A nutrient has $D_{121.1} = 50$ min and $z = 25$ °C. A target microbe has $D_{121.1} = 0.5$ min and $z = 8$ °C. How many times more sensitive to temperature is the microbe compared to the nutrient? What does this mean for process design?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $D$ of nutrient at 130 °C.**

$$
D_{\text{nutrient}, 130} = D_{121.1} \times 10^{\frac{121.1 - 130}{25}} = 50 \times 10^{-0.356}
$$

$$
= 50 \times 0.4408 = 22.04 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $D$ of microbe at 130 °C.**

$$
D_{\text{microbe}, 130} = 0.5 \times 10^{\frac{121.1 - 130}{8}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-1.11}
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 0.0776 = 0.0388 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare sensitivities (ratio of $D$ values).**

$$
\frac{D_{\text{nutrient}}}{D_{\text{microbe}}} = \frac{22.04}{0.0388} = 568
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**
The microbe is degraded **568× faster** than the nutrient at 130 °C. This is the **theoretical foundation** of HTST/UHT processing — microbes are destroyed before significant nutrient loss occurs.

### 3.5 Summary Matrix — Effects Table (for Quick Reference)

| Nutrient Class | Key Reaction | Controlling Factors | Process Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proteins | Denaturation, Maillard, Lysine loss | Temperature, pH, $a_w$, sugar content | Reduce time, lower pH, remove O₂ |
| Carbohydrates | Gelatinization, Caramelization, Maillard | $T$, $a_w$, pH | Control $a_w$ < 0.3 or > 0.85 |
| Lipids | Hydrolysis, Oxidation, Polymerization | O₂, $T$, metals, light | Inert atmosphere, antioxidants (BHA, BHT, tocopherol) |
| Vitamin C | Aerobic oxidation | O₂, $T$, pH, metal catalysts | Deaeration, blanching, aseptic fill |
| Thiamine (B₁) | Hydrolytic cleavage | pH > 7 amplifies loss | Acidic conditions, avoid alkaline processing |
| Vitamin A | Isomerization, oxidation | $T$, O₂, light | Antioxidants, opaque packaging |
| Chlorophyll | Pheophytinization | pH, $T$ | Blanch in alkaline NaHCO₃ |
| Anthocyanins | Hydrolysis, oxidation | pH, O₂, SO₂ | Maintain pH < 3.5, use SO₂ |
| Minerals | Leaching (not destroyed) | Water contact | Reduce blanching water, steam blanch |

---
